wave742 #13 failing: /docs/gateway-mode.html missing 'ks-footer' brand-lock token (docs/HTML lint, not core logic).
Gateway 'streaming' re-chunks fully-assembled text into 80-char windows (router.js:6502-6505) rather than true upstream token streaming; adapter-native SSE is a noted follow-up.
Geo-fence HTTP 451 enforcement lives in the signup route (out of audited file scope) — isGeoFenced helper verified, enforcement call site not re-verified.
audit.summary: The Gateway / Route & Capture surface is largely production-wired and well-tested at the unit level (60/61 gateway tests; full passes on confidence/poisoning/lake/capture suites). The dispatch pipeline (auth -> input-PII-block -> route -> fallback-chain -> output-scan -> signed receipt -> capture) is real and integrated in router.js. Two concrete correctness gaps stand out: (1) redact_all/redact_captures OUTPUT redaction is computed but never applied to the client response on /v1/gateway/dispatch (input block mode works; the connectorProxy capture path redacts correctly, the dispatch route does not), and (2) the W807 streaming-entropy mid-response teacher-splice machinery is built and unit-tested but not wired into any HTTP route, so the gateway uses a simpler pre-scored-confidence selectRoute. Receipt signing, fail-closed connector proxy, tenant-fenced lake analytics, the 3-signal poisoning orchestrator, and per-tenant auth/rate-limit/quota are all solid. Remaining items are tech debt: dual redaction surfaces, synthetic SSE chunking, best-effort cost estimation defaulting to 0, and one docs brand-lock test failure. Full result written to C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/audit/w920/gateway.audit.json.

findings.title: Output redaction (redact_all / redact_captures) never reaches the client at /v1/gateway/dispatch
findings.technique: Substitute outputScan.output_text into the JSON and SSE response paths
findings.source: Cloudflare AI Gateway Guardrails (moderate model responses, block or flag): https://developers.cloudflare.com/ai-gateway/features/ ; Portkey output guardrails: https://portkey.ai/docs/product/ai-gateway
findings.current_state: router.js:6349 computes outputScan = pii.applyMode({text: outputText, mode: redact_mode}) but its redacted output_text is discarded. JSON path at 6556 spreads ...result.json (raw upstream verbatim); SSE path at 6471 streams the raw outputText. Only receipt.redaction_applied is populated, so redact_all/redact_captures customers still receive un-redacted PII in the body. Input block mode (6258-6267) works correctly.
findings.proposed_change: When outputScan.action === 'redact', rewrite result.json's assistant content (choices[0].message.content for OpenAI shape, content[].text for Anthropic shape) to outputScan.output_text before the spread at 6556, and feed outputScan.output_text (not raw outputText) into the SSE chunker at 6471. For redact_captures keep client body raw but persist only the redacted text to the observation row. Add a test asserting an SSN in a mocked upstream response is masked in both JSON and SSE client responses under redact_all.
findings.rationale: Correctness/compliance defect, not a feature gap. The product advertises output redaction (the mode exists, the scan runs) but silently fails to apply it on the primary dispatch route. A HIPAA/PII customer relying on redact_all leaks PHI to their own client while the receipt claims redaction was applied. Few-line fix on a path the audit already isolated; the connectorProxy route (router.js:4332-4342) is a working in-repo reference.

verdict.reason:
Confirmed real on the HTTP route /v1/gateway/dispatch. router.js:6349 computes outputScan = pii.applyMode({text: outputText, mode: redact_mode}); for redact_all, applyMode returns output_text=redacted and action='redact' (pii-redactor.js:183-191). But the route only consumes outputScan.redaction_applied (router.js:6368, folded into the receipt). The redacted output_text is never used: the JSON path (router.js:6556) spreads ...result.json (raw upstream verbatim) and the SSE path (router.js:6471) streams String(outputText) where outputText is the raw const assigned at 6343. So receipt.redaction_applied claims redaction occurred while the client body still carries raw PII/PHI. Input block mode (6258-6267) works correctly, as the finding states.

NOT already implemented on this route. There IS a correct implementation, but in a DIFFERENT code path: the kolm gateway call CLI in wrapper-cli.js:933-940 rewrites choices[0].message.content and .text to scrubbedOutput under redact_all. The integration test at tests/wrapper-integration.test.js:425-430 asserts the response is redacted under redact_all, but it drives the CLI (runCli(['gateway','call',...])), NOT the HTTP route, so it gives false coverage confidence. No test in tests/ asserts output-body redaction against /v1/gateway/dispatch (the route tests cover rate-limit, overhead, and SSE-branch structure only). The route is genuinely unpatched.

Effort (1 day): realistic, arguably generous — the CLI already demonstrates the exact two-shape rewrite pattern; the change is a few lines plus a route-level test.

Impact (critical): accurate as a correctness/compliance defect. A HIPAA/PII customer on redact_all leaks PHI to their own client while the receipt asserts redaction_applied. Note the finding slightly UNDERSTATES scope: redact_captures is also broken on this route for the capture row — the observation persisted at router.js:6400-6420 stores the receipt whose output_text (6365) is the raw outputText, so the capture row keeps raw PII too.

Caveats on the finding's wording: the cited 'working in-repo reference' at router.js:4332-4342 (connectorProxy) is actually INPUT redaction (swaps PII out of forwardBody before forwarding upstream), not output redaction; the true output-redaction reference is wrapper-cli.js:933-940. This does not change the verdict.

Relevant files: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/router.js (6343-6349 scan, 6368 receipt-only use, 6471 SSE raw, 6556 JSON raw, 6365/6400-6420 capture row), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/pii-redactor.js (114-192 applyMode contract), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/wrapper-cli.js (933-940 working reference), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/tests/wrapper-integration.test.js (425-430 CLI-only coverage).

findings.title: Prompt-injection / jailbreak detection exists in-repo but is unwired from the gateway input path
findings.technique: Inline input guardrail at dispatch (heuristic now, Prompt-Guard-2 classifier later)
findings.source: Meta Llama-Prompt-Guard-2-22M (DeBERTa-xsmall, -75% latency vs 86M, high recall @1% FPR): https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-Prompt-Guard-2-22M ; Portkey 60+ guardrail checks incl. prompt injection: https://portkey.ai/features/ai-gateway ; OWASP LLM Top 10
findings.current_state: src/adversarial-prompts.js exports classifyPromptAdversarial (8-category heuristic) but it is only reachable via a dedicated red-team route (router.js:21303). The gateway dispatch input stage runs PII scan only (6256); it does NOT screen for prompt injection. A namespace cannot block/flag injected prompts at the gateway.
findings.proposed_change: Add an optional input-guardrail stage right after the PII block (router.js:6258). Phase 1 (~2 days): call classifyPromptAdversarial(inputText); when namespace.injection_mode === 'block' and is_adversarial, return 400 injection_blocked with evidence spans, and always stamp injection_classes + injection_score into the receipt. Phase 2 (later): make the detector pluggable so a hosted Prompt-Guard-2-22M (DeBERTa-xsmall, CPU-runnable) can replace the heuristic; the heuristic stays as the zero-dependency default.
findings.rationale: Prompt injection is OWASP LLM01 and a table-stakes gateway guardrail in 2025 (Portkey, Cloudflare both ship it inline). kolm already built the classifier and a frozen taxonomy - it is simply not on the request path. Wiring an existing tested module behind a namespace flag is low-risk and turns a latent capability into a shipped guardrail; the receipt recording the verdict is a differentiator.

verdict.reason:
Could NOT refute - the finding is accurate. The gateway dispatch path (/v1/gateway/dispatch, router.js:6131) screens input for PII ONLY (stage 2, lines 6253-6267, pii.applyMode), then goes straight to route selection. It does not call classifyPromptAdversarial or sanitizeInput. The injection detector exists (src/adversarial-prompts.js classifyPromptAdversarial, 8-category heuristic) and there is even a runtime sanitizer (src/runtime-sanitizer.js sanitizeInput/wrapForRuntime), but both are reachable ONLY via the dedicated red-team routes: /v1/redteam/classify imports classifyPromptAdversarial at router.js:21303 and /v1/redteam/sanitize imports sanitizeInput at router.js:21381. The gateway handler imports only store/gateway-router/pii-redactor/gateway-receipt/provider-registry/_shared (lines 6211-6220) - never adversarial-prompts or runtime-sanitizer. The namespace config has no injection_mode field (schema at router.js:21955-21958 = capture_mode/redact_mode/route_chain/confidence_threshold only), and the receipt has zero injection fields (grep over src/gateway-receipt.js for injection/adversarial/jailbreak returned no matches; receiptInputs at line 6356 has no injection_classes/injection_score). So the proposed change is genuinely net-new wiring on the request path, not a duplicate.

REFINEMENTS to the finding: (1) The runtime-sanitizer is a stronger existing seam than the finding credits - sanitizeInput already implements block/redact/fallback/passthrough policies, so Phase 1 is really "import the existing sanitizer, add namespace.injection_mode, call it after PII block" rather than re-deriving block logic. This makes the 2-3 day estimate roughly fair-to-slightly-generous for code, but the receipt change has a hidden cost the finding understates: stamping injection_classes/injection_score into the SIGNED receipt means touching src/receipt-schema.js ALL_FIELDS + canonicalForSigning (the Ed25519-signed kolm-audit-1 schema, gateway-receipt.js:1-3) AND its lock-in tests, or else attaching it as a non-signed top-level field like latency_breakdown does (router.js:6398). The signed-schema route adds ~1 day for schema migration + test churn; the non-signed route is cheaper but weakens the 'receipt as proof' differentiator the rationale leans on. Net effort estimate of 3 days is realistic if they take the non-signed field path; closer to 4-5 if they put it in the signed canonical receipt. (2) Impact 'high' is slightly overblown for kolm's actual users - the heuristic is regex-only (router.js patterns at adversarial-prompts.js:252-275) and the module itself disclaims production-grade detection (comment lines 23-24); shipping it inline closes an OWASP-LLM01 checkbox and is a real differentiator on the receipt, but a regex guardrail is table-stakes-but-weak versus the Prompt-Guard-2 classifier the finding names for Phase 2. Adjusted to medium-high. (3) Unstated prerequisite: namespace create AND patch paths both need the new injection_mode field (router.js:21955 create + 21997 allowed-update list), and a false-positive escape valve is needed since block-mode on a noisy regex will reject legitimate prompts containing phrases like 'ignore previous'. Overall: real gap, low-risk to wire, but the receipt-signing dimension and regex-quality caveat temper the effort and impact.

findings.title: No true upstream streaming - the gateway buffers the full response then re-chunks 80 chars at a time (synthetic TTFT)
findings.technique: Provider-native SSE pass-through with per-provider stream parsers (OpenAI delta vs Anthropic event SSE)
findings.source: LLM gateway streaming guidance (avoid intermediaries that coalesce chunks; keep buffering off; async logging): https://techsy.io/en/blog/best-llm-gateway-tools ; Cloudflare/Portkey/LiteLLM proxy provider streams with minimal buffering
findings.current_state: capture.js forwarders call _w_N_hardenedFetch with requireJson, fully buffering the upstream body before returning. The dispatch SSE branch (router.js:6459-6544) slices the already-complete text into 80-char windows (6502-6505). Comment at 6455-6458 admits adapter-native streaming is a follow-up. Advertised 'live' TTFT is synthetic - the client waits for the entire upstream completion before the first 'token'.
findings.proposed_change: Add a streaming forward path in the adapters that, when body.stream===true, returns an async iterator/ReadableStream over the upstream SSE instead of buffering. Thread it through dispatchWithFallback (stream first attempt; commit after first byte, fall back only on pre-first-byte error). Parse provider-native SSE per provider (OpenAI chat.completion.chunk vs Anthropic message_delta), forward chunks immediately, assemble full text in parallel for the post-stream signed receipt (the receipt-after-stream model already exists at 6508-6527). Keep the buffered re-chunk path as fallback.
findings.rationale: TTFT is the dominant perceived-latency metric for chat UIs (10-20x perceived speedup). kolm's current 'streaming' gives zero TTFT benefit - the user waits for the full upstream completion then watches it dribble out, making the gateway strictly slower than calling the provider directly with streaming. This undermines the wrapper-tax story the receipts measure. Receipt-after-full-text already supports a post-stream receipt; the hard part is per-provider SSE parsing + fallback semantics.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED, not refuted. The code matches the finding precisely.

1) Buffer-then-rechunk is real. All three forwarders (forwardAnthropic/forwardOpenAI/forwardOpenRouter at src/capture.js:283-358) call _w_N_hardenedFetch with requireJson:true. hardenedFetch is the single chokepoint for all upstream POSTs (src/providers/_shared.js) and ALWAYS does `text = await res.text()` at line 243 — fully buffering the upstream body before returning. There is no getReader/ReadableStream/response.body streaming anywhere in the dispatch path. Even requireJson:false (line 244-248) still buffers via res.text() into {_raw}.

2) The synthetic SSE re-chunk is exactly as described. router.js:6449-6544 — the comment at 6455-6458 explicitly admits "adapter-native upstream-streaming is a follow-up that needs per-provider stream parsers." Line 6471 reads the already-complete `outputText`, lines 6502-6505 slice it into 80-char windows. The post-stream signed-receipt model already exists at 6508-6527 (kolm_receipt SSE event after the full text), exactly as the proposed_change notes.

3) gateway-router.js dispatchWithFallback -> dispatchToProvider (lines 89-272) has no streaming branch; it just awaits the buffered forwarder result. body.stream is not even forwarded upstream (the param shaper allowlists known fields), so the upstream is called non-streaming and kolm re-chunks the result.

4) The two streaming-contract.js consumers (router.js:2512 streamingCapabilities and :2518 normalizeStreamChunk on a client-supplied chunk) are descriptive catalog/normalizer endpoints — NOT an upstream SSE forwarder. capture-stream.js is the capture live-tail (browser/CLI watching the capture lake), unrelated to upstream LLM token streaming. docs/product-math-frontier.json:660 lists streaming as future "Add acceptance accounting to streaming" work and product-research-atlas.json treats streaming as a readiness item, confirming it is not shipped as true pass-through.

So alreadyPresent=false for the actual feature (true upstream streaming); already_partially_present=true in the original finding is accurate re the wire-format scaffolding + post-stream receipt.

Effort: 8 days is realistic to slightly light -> adjusted to 9. The hard parts are threading a stream through hardenedFetch, which is also the retry/timeout/malformed-JSON harness — streaming a body breaks the "retry on 429 after reading body" and "502 on unparseable JSON" semantics and the proposed "commit after first byte, fall back only pre-first-byte" requires re-architecting shouldFallback/dispatchWithFallback, plus per-provider SSE parsers (OpenAI chunk vs Anthropic event SSE vs OpenRouter vs Gemini native) plus parallel full-text assembly for the signed receipt.

Impact: high is defensible (kept). Synthetic TTFT makes the gateway strictly slower than calling the provider directly with streaming, which directly undermines kolm's wrapper-tax/latency receipt narrative. Mild caveat: kolm's core users care most about the distill/capture/audit-receipt path, so interactive chat-UI TTFT is somewhat secondary to its positioning — but the claim that current 'live' streaming gives zero TTFT benefit is factually correct and worth fixing.

findings.title: Routing is a pre-scored confidence NUMBER, not a learned/semantic router - leaving the RouteLLM cost lever on the table
findings.technique: Learned binary router (RouteLLM matrix-factorization / BERT classifier) selecting weak-vs-strong per prompt
findings.source: RouteLLM, LMSYS/UC Berkeley, ICLR 2025: https://github.com/lm-sys/RouteLLM (85% cost cut on MT-Bench, 95% of GPT-4 quality, >40% cheaper than commercial routers): https://www.lmsys.org/blog/2024-07-01-routellm/
findings.current_state: gateway-router.selectRoute (gateway-router.js:295) only short-circuits to fallback when the caller already supplied a confidence number below threshold (via x-kolm-confidence header / body.confidence, router.js:6273). kolm never computes that score - there is no learned router that predicts whether the cheap/local model will suffice. Routing is caller-driven, not gateway-driven.
findings.proposed_change: Ship a lightweight learned router that, given the prompt, predicts P(strong-model-needed) and sets the confidence input automatically when the caller doesn't supply one. Start with a calibrated logistic/embedding-similarity router (RouteLLM 'SW ranking' style) trained on the tenant's own captured win/loss pairs from the lake (fallback_reason + capture_eligible already provide labels). Expose route_decision:'auto' on the namespace; record predicted score + strong/weak choice in the receipt so savings are verifiable.
findings.rationale: RouteLLM is the strongest published evidence that prompt-aware routing yields 85% cost cuts at 95% quality - exactly kolm's pitch. kolm has the scaffolding (selectRoute consumes a confidence score) but offloads the hard part (computing it) to the caller, which almost no caller will do. Owning the router closes the loop with the captured-data flywheel (the lake stores the labels). Lower confidence because a quality-preserving router needs per-tenant calibration and careful eval to avoid silently downgrading hard prompts.

verdict.reason:
Could not refute — the finding accurately describes the actual code. Verified at the source: src/gateway-router.js:295-319 (selectRoute) only short-circuits to fallback when the caller supplies a `confidence` number below threshold; it never computes that score. src/router.js:6273 confirms `confidence = x-kolm-confidence header (line 6136) OR body.confidence` — purely caller-driven, with no gateway-side prompt-aware predictor in the dispatch path (router.js:6269-6327).

The two confidence routers that DO exist are a different mechanism and do not satisfy the finding: src/runtime-confidence-router.js (W709) and src/confidence-router.js (W807) both compute Shannon entropy from the STUDENT MODEL'S OWN token logprobs AFTER/DURING generation (post-hoc, token-level → mid-stream teacher splice). That is the opposite of a RouteLLM-style learned router that predicts P(strong-model-needed) FROM THE PROMPT BEFORE any model is called. I also confirmed src/curriculum-sort.js complexityProxy scores captured RESPONSES for training-data ordering (not prompts, not routing), and grep found no RouteLLM / matrix-factorization / BERT / prompt-difficulty classifier anywhere in src/. So a learned prompt-router is genuinely NOT implemented (alreadyPresent=false), though the scaffolding the finding cites (selectRoute consuming a score; lake labels via fallback_reason/capture_eligible; routing-events.js) is real.

Adjustments: (1) Effort 10d is optimistic — a quality-preserving router needs a trained/calibrated classifier or embedding-similarity model, per-tenant calibration, label extraction from the lake, a held-out eval harness to prove it does not silently downgrade hard prompts, an inference path inside the hot dispatch loop (latency budget for the gateway), receipt-schema additions to record predicted score, and the route_decision:'auto' namespace plumbing — realistically ~15-25d, so 18. (2) Impact high→medium: the 85%-cost/95%-quality headline is RouteLLM's MT-Bench (GPT-4 vs Mixtral) benchmark, not kolm's distilled-student-vs-frontier setup; kolm's existing W807 mid-stream entropy splice already captures a large part of the same cost lever in its actual architecture, so the marginal gain of a pre-call prompt-router is unproven for the distill use case and carries genuine silent-downgrade risk. Users (cost-conscious gateway tenants) would care, but the finding overstates the lift relative to what already ships.

findings.title: No weighted load-balancing / multi-key rotation - fallback is strictly sequential
findings.technique: Weighted load balancing across multiple keys/providers + least-busy / latency-based selection
findings.source: LiteLLM routing strategies (simple-shuffle default, least-busy, usage-based, latency-based): https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/routing ; Portkey load balancing with weights across keys/providers: https://portkey.ai/features/ai-gateway
findings.current_state: dispatchWithFallback (gateway-router.js:226-272) walks the chain strictly in order and only advances on failure. One upstream key per provider is read from env (router.js:6295-6304). No weighted distribution, no per-key rotation to spread rate limits, no latency/least-busy selection - every request hits the same primary until it fails.
findings.proposed_change: Add an optional load-balance mode to the chain entry: accept a weighted set [{provider, weight, key_pool:[...]}] and pick the first attempt by weighted random (LiteLLM simple-shuffle) or by lowest recorded p50 latency (latency-based, using lake.js latency aggregates that already exist). Keep ordered fallback for the remainder. Round-robin across a key pool per provider to spread provider-side rate limits. Record the chosen weight/strategy in the receipt.
findings.rationale: Multi-key load balancing is how production gateways survive provider rate limits and A/B model rollouts (LiteLLM, Portkey headline it). kolm's sequential-only chain means a single key's rate limit throttles the whole tenant and there's no way to canary a new model at 10% traffic. The lake already records per-provider latency, so latency-based selection is cheap. Medium because the existing 429-backoff + sequential fallback partially mitigates failures; this is about scale and rollout flexibility.

verdict.reason:
Factual claims verified against the actual code and are accurate. dispatchWithFallback (src/gateway-router.js:248-271) walks the chain strictly in order, returning on first ok or advancing only on shouldFallback failure. buildChainFromNamespace (gateway-router.js:344-366) builds an ordered [primary, ...fallback] chain with no weight/pool fields. Upstream keys are exactly one-per-provider read from env via _firstEnv (src/router.js:6295-6304; entry.upstreamKey = upstreamKeys[entry.provider], line 6321). The lake DOES aggregate per-provider latency (src/lake.js:80-106, providers[k].avg_latency), so the finding's "latency-based selection is cheap" premise is correct. The many "rotate"/"weight"/"round-robin" grep hits are unrelated: tenant API-key rotation, Ed25519 signing-key rotation, active-learning scoring weights, AI-Act risk-classifier weights, and a Kubernetes "LoadBalancer" service-type doc line. So this is genuinely NOT implemented and NOT already present.

NOT refuted on facts, but impact is overblown for kolm's actual product and users (refute the impact, not the existence): (1) kolm's thesis is local-first distillation + signed receipts, NOT a high-throughput multi-provider scale proxy a la LiteLLM/Portkey. The gateway routes a LOCAL artifact first then falls to ONE frontier teacher; the whole point of distillation is to MOVE traffic OFF the frontier provider, structurally reducing the rate-limit pressure this finding targets. (2) The single-key-per-tenant BYOC model is the real deployment shape; key-pools to "spread rate limits" assume an enterprise with many provider sub-accounts, which is not the solo/startup persona base (A-F). (3) The finding itself concedes 429-backoff + sequential fallback already mitigates the failure case (capture.js hardened fetch with 429 retries) — hence "medium" by its own author. (4) Canary/A-B rollout is a separate, narrower need already partly served by src/ab-routes.js. Effort is underestimated: a real weighted/least-busy/latency router needs per-key health + rotation state store, weighted-random and p50-latency selection, gateway.toml schema additions, plumbing through buildChainFromNamespace + selectRoute + dispatchWithFallback + the receipt schema (record chosen weight/strategy), plus tests across all of those — realistically ~9 days, not 5. Net: real gap, correctly not-present, but low impact for kolm's positioning and personas; this is a scale/rollout nicety, not a survival feature.

findings.title: PII detection is regex-only (phi-redactor) - misses contextual/implicit identifiers a transformer NER catches
findings.technique: Pluggable transformer NER recognizer (GLiNER / Presidio-style) behind the existing applyMode contract
findings.source: Presidio + GLiNER integration (GLiNER-base ~0.81 F1 multi-domain vs regex much lower): https://microsoft.github.io/presidio/samples/python/gliner/ ; hybrid RECAP outperforms NER by 82%: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.07551 ; Protecto NER PII comparison
findings.current_state: pii-redactor.js is a thin unifier over phi-redactor.js, which uses regex detectors for HIPAA-18 + 3 kolm classes. Comment at pii-redactor.js:21 says 'we deliberately do NOT add new detectors here'. Regex cannot catch implicit identifiers, coded language, names without obvious markers, or non-US formats - the documented weakness of regex-only PII.
findings.proposed_change: Keep regex as the always-on zero-dependency default, but make applyMode's detector pluggable: add an optional NER recognizer (a hosted GLiNER-base or Presidio service the standalone src/services/redactor.js could front) whose entity spans merge with regex findings before substitution. Gate per-namespace (privacy_engine: 'regex' | 'hybrid'). This also fixes the dual-redaction-surface drift the audit flagged - the standalone service becomes the in-path NER stage rather than a parallel unused surface.
findings.rationale: kolm leans hard on compliance/receipts, but regex-only PII is the weakest detector tier and will miss real PHI in clinical/free-text chat (regex name detection in particular is poor). A transformer NER lifts F1 substantially on multi-domain text. Medium not high because it adds a model dependency and latency, must stay opt-in, and the regex baseline is genuinely adequate for structured identifiers where it is strong. Bonus: consolidates the two parallel redaction surfaces the audit called out as drift risk.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, NOT already present. Current state is accurately described. src/pii-redactor.js (lines 14-23) is a thin unifier over src/phi-redactor.js, which is pure regex for HIPAA-18 + NPI/DEA/Medicaid. The comment at pii-redactor.js:20 ('we deliberately do NOT add new detectors here') and phi-redactor.js:26-33 ('NON-GOALS: No ML/NER ... For richer detection wrap this module with a downstream NER') confirm regex-only is the documented, intentional design. router.js:6256 and 6349 call pii.applyMode for input/output scans with mode driven by nsConfig.redact_mode. The standalone src/services/redactor.js (W240) is indeed a parallel surface fronting the same phi-redactor, validating the dual-surface drift point. The technical claim is correct: the regex NAME detector (phi-redactor.js:100-101) only fires on honorific/label prefixes, so an unlabeled name (the holdout's 'Maria Gonzalez was admitted' case) is missed — exactly what a transformer NER would catch.

NOT already present: searched src/ thoroughly — no privacy_engine namespace field, no NER/GLiNER/Presidio recognizer integration, no pluggable detector hook merging external entity spans with regex findings. The proposed pluggable applyMode contract genuinely does not exist.

Caveats: (1) effort_days=7 is underestimated — the pluggable seam in applyMode is ~1-2 days, but a real lift needs a hosted/bundled GLiNER or Presidio runtime (this JS path is otherwise zero-ML-dependency, so it means a separate Python/ONNX service), span-merge/dedup against the existing claim/overlap machinery in scanFindings, namespace-config schema + migration, latency budgeting in the per-request pipeline (currently tracked as pii_in_ms/pii_out_ms wrapper tax), a fail-open-vs-fail-closed decision when the NER service is unreachable, and holdout-based F1 validation. Realistically 12-20 days; set 14. (2) impact=medium is fair, lean-low: regex is genuinely strong on structured identifiers and kolm positions itself as the 'necessary half of Safe Harbor', so the gap only bites free-text clinical/support namespaces (the healthcare.html / customer-support.html segment) — not negligible there, but opt-in and segment-specific. Unstated prerequisites: model runtime dependency + latency, per-namespace config plumbing, and the regex must remain the always-on fallback. Finding's own caveats (must stay opt-in, adds model dependency/latency, regex adequate for structured IDs) are sound.

findings.title: Telemetry uses kolm.* OTel attributes, not the standard gen_ai.* GenAI semantic conventions
findings.technique: Emit OpenTelemetry GenAI semconv spans/metrics (gen_ai.*) alongside the kolm.* envelope
findings.source: OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions (gen_ai.client.token.usage, operation.duration, time_to_first_token, cache_read.input_tokens): https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/gen-ai/ ; Datadog native support v1.37+: https://www.datadoghq.com/blog/llm-otel-semantic-convention/
findings.current_state: otel-attrs.js + otel.js define a kolm-native attribute set (kolm.artifact.id, kolm.routing.decision, kolm.token.confidence_p50, etc.) - proprietary keys. The dispatch handler records internal metric events (recordMetricEvent) but does not emit standard gen_ai.* spans, so a buyer's existing Datadog/Honeycomb/Grafana LLM-observability pipeline cannot ingest kolm gateway traffic without custom mapping.
findings.proposed_change: In the dispatch handler, when an OTel tracer is registered, also set standard gen_ai.* attributes on the inference span: gen_ai.system (provider), gen_ai.request.model, gen_ai.response.model, gen_ai.usage.input_tokens/output_tokens, gen_ai.operation.name='chat', plus gen_ai.usage.cache_read.input_tokens once caching lands. Emit gen_ai.client.* metrics (token.usage, operation.duration, time_to_first_token). Keep kolm.* as additive enrichment. Purely an emit-more-keys change - no behavior change.
findings.rationale: The GenAI semconv is now the interoperability standard (Datadog, OpenLLMetry, OpenInference, every major APM consume it). Enterprise buyers expect to drop kolm into an existing OTel collector and see gen_ai.* spans light up. kolm has the OTel plumbing but speaks a proprietary dialect, forcing buyers to write mappers. Low-risk additive emission with real enterprise-adoption payoff; aligns kolm's verifiable-token-usage story with the standard the ecosystem reads.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, NOT already present. Grep for gen_ai/genai/GenAI across src/ and test/ returns ZERO attribute emission — the only gen_ai strings in code are URL citations (src/benchmark-evidence.js:35, src/runtime-adoption-packets.js:39) and docs/research markdown. The actual OTel keys emitted are entirely the proprietary kolm.* set: src/otel.js KOLM_OTEL_ATTRS is frozen at lines 368-407 (kolm.token.confidence, kolm.routing.decision, kolm.kscore.value, kolm.artifact.id, kolm.token.confidence_p50/p95, kolm.tenant.id_hash, kolm.namespace) and src/otel-attrs.js canonicalizes only into those same kolm.* keys. So a buyer's Datadog/Honeycomb/Grafana LLM pipeline cannot ingest kolm spans without a custom mapper. The finding's already_partially_present:true is correct only in the narrow sense that the OTLP exporter plumbing (init/startSpan/endSpan/metric/counter/flush in otel.js) and lazy @opentelemetry/api detection exist — the standard semconv dialect itself does not.

EFFORT slightly understated (3d -> realistically ~4-5d). Unstated dependency: the finding's current_state and proposed_change both say 'the dispatch handler', but the /v1/gateway/dispatch handler (router.js:6131+) emits NO OTel span at all — it only calls recordMetricEvent (internal kolm metrics, not OTel) and builds a receipt. The W733 OTel span machinery (__w733Otel.startSpan + setRoutingAttributes, router.js:5243-5385) is wired into a DIFFERENT route, /v1/route/chat/completions. So 'also set gen_ai.* on the inference span when a tracer is registered' first requires creating an inference span in the dispatch handler (currently absent), making it more than a pure emit-more-keys change. Additionally gen_ai.usage.cache_read.input_tokens is gated on caching landing (a separate, not-yet-built finding in this same audit) and gen_ai.client.time_to_first_token is meaningless until real upstream streaming lands (also a separate finding — current 'streaming' is synthetic 80-char re-chunking).

IMPACT (medium) is accurate and not overblown: real enterprise-interop value (gen_ai.* is the de-facto standard read by Datadog v1.37+, OpenLLMetry, OpenInference) but it is additive enrichment, not a correctness/security/compliance defect, and is correctly ranked below the redaction-leak, cache, and prompt-injection findings in the same audit. Enterprise buyers with existing OTel collectors would care. Net: legitimate upgrade, gap is genuinely unimplemented, effort modestly optimistic given the dispatch-handler-has-no-span dependency, impact rating fair.

findings.title: W807 streaming-entropy mid-response teacher splice is built + unit-tested but unreachable via HTTP
findings.technique: Wire streamingEntropyWindow + spliceToTeacher into the streaming dispatch path
findings.source: Speculative/cascade routing literature; kolm's own W807 design (confidence-router.js:1-38); RouteLLM cascade motivation: https://www.lmsys.org/blog/2024-07-01-routellm/
findings.current_state: confidence-router.js ships streamingEntropyWindow (O(1) ring buffer), emitSpliceWeaknessSignal, and the splice machinery; teacher-splice.js + failure-modes.js import them, but router.js has ZERO references (audit-confirmed). The live gateway only uses selectRoute with a pre-scored number. The advertised mid-response splice and per-token threshold telemetry never execute on a real request.
findings.proposed_change: Once true upstream streaming exists (streaming finding above), feed each streamed local-model token's top_logprobs through streamingEntropyWindow inside the streaming dispatch path; when window.exceeds(threshold) fires, splice in the teacher for the remainder and call emitSpliceWeaknessSignal so the weak segment climbs the re-distill queue. Gate behind a namespace flag (route_decision:'local' with splice_enabled). Scope as a follow-on to the streaming finding because it depends on streaming-passthrough + local models exposing logprobs.
findings.rationale: Documented build-but-unwired feature - the website implies mid-response splicing exists. Genuinely novel vs competitors (none do per-token entropy splicing in-gateway) and feeds the self-improvement flywheel. But gated on true streaming + local logprob access, only pays off when local-first routing is in use, and carries quality risk if the splice seam is rough. Medium impact, lower confidence because the dependencies are heavy and value is concentrated in the local-model-first user segment.

verdict.reason:
VERDICT: Finding is REAL and NOT already wired into a live HTTP path. The W807 splice machinery is built + unit-tested but unreachable from any real request.

EVIDENCE (verified against actual code):
- src/confidence-router.js defines streamingEntropyWindow (line 186), emitSpliceWeaknessSignal (line 270), resolveThreshold/tokenEntropy/THRESHOLD_TABLE. src/teacher-splice.js defines spliceToTeacher (line 142). Unit-tested in tests/wave807-confidence-routing.test.js (19 tests, 17 references to the three functions).
- Call-site search across src/ and cli/: the ONLY occurrences of spliceToTeacher / streamingEntropyWindow / emitSpliceWeaknessSignal outside their own export definitions are in comments. ZERO production invocations. confirmed.
- src/router.js references confidence-router ONLY as 'runtime-confidence-router.js' (the W709 module, lines 5302/5454), never the W807 'confidence-router.js'. src/gateway-router.js: no references. cli/kolm.js (line 16345, `kolm route doctor`) imports confidence-router but uses only resolveThreshold for a read-only doctor summary — never the splice/window functions.
- The file the W807 comments claim 'owns' the wiring, src/runtime-wrap.js, does not exist on disk.

IMPORTANT NUANCE (this finding is about the W807 path specifically, not all splicing): there IS a reachable HTTP route /v1/route/chat/completions/stream (src/router.js:5422) that does naive mid-response student->teacher splicing. BUT it is a different, older W709-era implementation: it uses a single scalar `ent > threshold` per-token check (not the O(1) windowed mean of streamingEntropyWindow), does not use spliceToTeacher's budget/fallback envelope, and does not call emitSpliceWeaknessSignal (so no weakness-signal -> re-distill feedback). Moreover that route only works in fixture mode — runtime-confidence-router.js does not define streamStudent/streamTeacher (verified, 0 matches), and the route guards them as optional duck-typed hooks (`if (RR && typeof RR.streamStudent === 'function')`). So there is NO real upstream token-streaming-with-logprobs source in production at all.

The website advertises the W807 behavior verbatim: public/account/confidence.html:109 'watches a streaming entropy window during student generation and splices in the teacher mid-response when the window crosses your threshold' plus splice-latency p50/p95/p99 and 'weakness signal -> re-distill queue' caveat. That dashboard reads from routing-events, which emitSpliceWeaknessSignal would populate — but since it never runs on live traffic, the panel shows empty/zero in production. So there is a genuine claim-vs-reality gap.

EFFORT: 9 days is optimistic. The finding correctly scopes this as a follow-on gated on a prior 'streaming finding' (true upstream streaming passthrough + local models exposing top_logprobs), NEITHER of which exists today (verified: no streamStudent/streamTeacher, the existing stream route is fixture-only). Building real SSE passthrough that surfaces per-token top_logprobs from a local/self-hosted server, threading streamingEntropyWindow into it, handling the splice seam (continuing teacher generation from partial student KV/context coherently across a model swap), wiring the per-tenant latency budget, and the emitSpliceWeaknessSignal feedback write — plus quality validation that the seam isn't visibly rough — is realistically 12-16 days. Adjusted to ~14.

IMPACT: medium is overblown; adjusting to LOW. (1) Most kolm upstream adapters (Anthropic, OpenAI-without-logprobs:true, many self-hosted) do not return per-token top_logprobs, as the code itself notes (router.js:5218-5219) — the entropy signal degrades to 'no_top_logprobs' and the feature no-ops. (2) Value is concentrated entirely in the local-model-first user segment, which is a minority of gateway traffic (the gateway's mainline is selectRoute with a pre-scored confidence number, router.js:6282). (3) Carries quality risk at the splice seam. (4) The headline confidence-routing value (route whole calls student-vs-teacher) is already shipped via runtime-confidence-router + selectRoute; per-token mid-response splicing is an incremental refinement, not a missing core capability. The main concrete payoff is closing the website-claim-vs-reality gap on confidence.html, which argues more for trimming the marketing copy than for a 2-week build.

WOULD USERS CARE: marginally, and only the local-first segment. Higher-leverage fix is to either (a) make the confidence.html copy match reality until streaming+logprobs land, or (b) prioritize the upstream-streaming-passthrough prerequisite first, since several findings depend on it.

findings.title: No conditional/metadata-based routing (user tier, region, A/B split) at the gateway
findings.technique: Conditional routing rules keyed on request metadata + guardrail outcomes
findings.source: Portkey conditional routing (route by user tier, region, model version, guardrail outcome): https://portkey.ai/docs/product/ai-gateway ; Cloudflare Dynamic Routes (rate-limit free users, 50/50 A/B): https://blog.cloudflare.com/ai-gateway-aug-2025-refresh/
findings.current_state: Routing is fixed per namespace (nsConfig.route_chain). There is no way to say 'free-tier -> cheap model, enterprise-tier -> strong model', 'EU traffic -> EU-resident provider', or 'split 10% to candidate model'. The only dynamic signal is the optional confidence number.
findings.proposed_change: Add a conditional-route rule list to the namespace config: an ordered set of {when: {plan, region, header_match, injection_flagged, ...}, then: <chain>} rules evaluated before buildChainFromNamespace, falling through to the default chain. Reuse req.tenant_record.plan (already in scope at 6144) and a region header. Stamp the matched rule id into the receipt so routing decisions are auditable. Composes cleanly with the conditional caching/guardrail stages.
findings.rationale: Conditional routing is a standard gateway control-plane feature for cost governance (cheap models for free users) and safe rollouts (A/B/canary). kolm's namespace model is close - it just lacks the predicate layer. Medium impact: valuable for teams running mixed user tiers and model migrations, not as foundational as caching/redaction. Low build cost because the inputs (plan, headers) are already in handler scope and the receipt can record the decision.

verdict.reason:
Verified against actual code. The /v1/gateway/dispatch handler (src/router.js:6131) builds its provider chain purely from nsConfig.route_chain (ordered provider:model array, cap 16) via gw.buildChainFromNamespace + gw.selectRoute (src/gateway-router.js:295,344). The ONLY dynamic routing signal is the optional confidence number (W807 ConfidenceRouter). _plan IS in scope at router.js:6144 but used only for rate-limiting (usageCheckLimit); region is computed but used only for metrics/telemetry (recordMetricEvent) and a separate /v1/residency tagging surface. There is no predicate layer keyed on plan/region/header/guardrail-outcome. PARTIAL OVERLAP: kolm has standalone A/B infra (src/ab-router.js pickVariant/assignArm with 90/10 hash-bucket splits, src/ab-routes.js /v1/ab/*, ab-metrics, ab-promote) but it is NOT wired into the gateway dispatch path — pickVariant/assignArm/getSplit are never imported or called in gateway-router.js or the dispatch handler; callers must manually POST /v1/ab/feedback with a variant they chose elsewhere. So the 'split 10% to candidate' primitive exists but is not integrated into automatic routing, and plan-tier/region routing have zero implementation. The finding's core claim (no conditional metadata-based routing at the gateway) is accurate.

EFFORT: 4d is plausible but slightly low. Predicate eval is cheap (inputs in handler scope), but the change also touches the namespace config schema + its create/PUT validation (allowed whitelist at router.js:21997, row shape at 21949), and stamping the matched rule_id into the receipt requires editing src/receipt-schema.js ALL_FIELDS + canonicalForSigning (signed/verified path) plus its verifier tests, CLI/TUI config surfaces, and lock-in tests. Realistic 4-6d.

IMPACT: medium is fair, arguably slightly generous. kolm's value prop is local-first confidence routing + signed receipts + distillation, not multi-tenant cost governance. Plan-tier 'free->cheap' has weak ROI because customers bring their own upstream keys (cost is theirs, not a kolm billing lever) and plan is already gated via rate limits. Region routing overlaps the existing /v1/residency surface. The genuinely valuable slice is wiring the existing A/B splitter into dispatch for safe model migration, which aligns with the distillation flywheel. Net: real gap, not refuted; impact medium-leaning-low, effort 4-6d.

findings.title: No MCP gateway surface for agent tool-call governance
findings.technique: MCP proxy with per-tool ACLs, signed tool-call receipts, and OTLP export
findings.source: Docker MCP Gateway (container isolation, secrets, call-tracing): https://www.docker.com/blog/docker-mcp-gateway-secure-infrastructure-for-agentic-ai/ ; agentgateway (JWT/OAuth, CEL RBAC, OTel): https://github.com/agentgateway/agentgateway ; mcp-gateway-registry: https://github.com/agentic-community/mcp-gateway-registry
findings.current_state: kolm has tool-use-capture.js and agent-telemetry.js, but no gateway surface that proxies MCP tool calls with per-tool access control, secret injection, and a signed receipt per tool invocation. The dispatch path governs model calls, not agent tool calls.
findings.proposed_change: Add a /v1/mcp/dispatch surface that fronts MCP servers the way /v1/gateway/dispatch fronts LLM providers: per-namespace tool ACL (which tools an agent key may call), secret injection (never expose raw tool credentials to the agent), input/output PII + injection scan on tool args/results, and a kolm-audit-1 receipt per tool call. Reuse the existing receipt signer, redactor, and OTel emission. Extends the verifiable-call story from model calls to agent actions.
findings.rationale: MCP gateways are the fastest-growing 2025-2026 gateway category (Docker, agentgateway, Lunar all entered), and kolm's signed-receipt model maps unusually well to per-tool-call auditing - a genuine differentiator if agents are a target segment. But it is a large new surface, depends on kolm's agent story being a priority, and the LLM-call gateway has higher-impact gaps to close first. Low impact reflects sequencing, not ceiling; flagged frontier so it is on the radar.

verdict.reason: Could not refute. The MCP tool-call governance gateway the finding describes does not exist in the code. Verified: (1) No /v1/mcp/dispatch route — grep for mcp/dispatch, per-tool ACL, mcp proxy in src/router.js returns nothing; the only /v1/gateway/* routes (dispatch at router.js:6131) front LLM PROVIDERS, not agent tool calls. (2) src/tool-use-capture.js is a pure parser that extracts tool_calls from upstream LLM responses for distillation training data — its own header says 'PURE module. No I/O, no persistence'; no proxy, ACL, or receipt. (3) src/tool-runtime.js is an in-process per-tenant handler-registry SCAFFOLD whose design contract explicitly says it does NOT sandbox, sign URLs, validate schema, or rate-limit ('Sandboxing is the tenant's job') and ships 'No built-in tool registry'. (4) services/mcp/server.js is the INVERSE of the finding — it exposes kolm's own signed .kolm artifacts AS MCP tools to agents, rather than proxying third-party MCP servers. The repo's own audit (docs/research/agent-mcp-install-governance-audit-2026-05-12.md) confirms its allowed_tools ACL is 'parsed but not enforced by the server' and its per-call _kolm.receipt is 'not HMAC-signed per call'. So no per-tool ACL enforcement, no secret injection, no tool-arg/result PII+injection scan, and no signed kolm-audit-1 receipt per tool invocation exist. The finding correctly self-marks already_partially_present:true (capture/parse primitives + the LLM-call receipt signer exist and are reusable). Secondary checks (do not change verdict): effort 14d is plausibly LOW given MCP transport (stdio/SSE/streamable-HTTP), a secret vault, and per-namespace ACL config all being net-new; impact 'low' is fair and the finding itself frames it as sequencing, not ceiling (frontier, confidence 0.55). Real users: relevant only if agents/MCP become a target segment — today kolm's positioning is distillation + LLM-call gateway, so this is genuinely a radar item, not a near-term gap.

checks.evidence: splitSeeds wraps canonicalSplitSeeds: 'The old divergent five-bucket scheme has been removed.'

The Node distillation worker (distill.mjs) runs end-to-end in doctor/stub modes with an honest manifest, content-hash resume, cumulative cost cap, teacher-holdout scoring and a full hash receipt chain (verified live: ready_for_full_pipeline:true on a torch 2.11.0+cu128 / RTX 5090 box). findings.title: Wire DoRA + rsLoRA + PiSSA into the LoRA config (one-line peft flags, measurable accuracy gain)
findings.technique: DoRA (use_dora=True), rsLoRA (use_rslora=True), PiSSA (init_lora_weights='pissa') — native peft.LoraConfig kwargs since peft >=0.11/0.12
findings.source: DoRA arXiv:2402.09353 (+3.7% LLaMA-7B, NVIDIA dev blog); rsLoRA arXiv:2312.03732 (high-rank gradient stability); PiSSA arXiv:2404.02948
findings.current_state: LoraConfig in train_lora.py:426-432 is vanilla: only r, lora_alpha, task_type, bias, lora_dropout. No use_dora/use_rslora/init_lora_weights. peft 5.x (transformers 5.5.1 confirmed on the box) supports all three.
findings.proposed_change: Add --lora-variant {lora,dora,rslora,pissa,lora-plus} to distill.mjs, thread to train_lora.py, set the matching LoraConfig kwarg. LoRA+ needs a custom optimizer lr-ratio — defer. Record lora_variant in training-summary.json + provenance. Default stays 'lora'.
findings.rationale: Near-free quality win: DoRA/rsLoRA/PiSSA are single LoraConfig kwargs in the peft version already installed. DoRA buys 1-4% accuracy at negligible cost; rsLoRA unlocks stable high-rank training (kolm uses r=16-64); PiSSA gives faster convergence. Highest ROI per engineering-day on the whole surface. Verified the trainer's LoraConfig has none today.

verdict.reason:
REAL but with corrections; NOT present in any production path. Verified every active trainer builds a vanilla LoraConfig with no use_dora/use_rslora/init_lora_weights: workers/distill/scripts/train_lora.py:426-432, train_lora_unsloth.py:142-156, apps/trainer/distill.py:982, contrastive_distill.py:437, trainer_real.py:142, trainer_local.py:98, plus the directml/rocm/remote_ssh backends. distill.mjs:474-486 only threads lora-r/alpha/dropout + neftune-noise-alpha — no variant flag. So the finding's current_state is accurate for the shipped distill surface.

CAVEAT (partial-present nuance): apps/trainer/lora_variants.py (266 lines) DOES fully implement rsLoRA, DoRA, LoRA+, LoRA-FA, NEFTune presets (build_peft_lora_config sets use_rslora/use_dora; quality_preset composes them). BUT it is dead code — a repo-wide grep shows it is imported by NOTHING outside itself (only an identical copy under tmp/). No trainer, no recipe loader, no CLI path consumes it. So the variant LOGIC exists but the production capability does not; the finding's already_partially_present:false is defensible. Notably even that orphan module does NOT implement PiSSA (init_lora_weights='pissa') — PiSSA is implemented nowhere in the repo. NEFTune is the one variant genuinely wired end-to-end (T2.2).

WRONG CLAIM: the finding says 'peft 5.x (transformers 5.5.1 confirmed on the box)'. I ran the box: peft is 0.19.1 (transformers 5.5.1). The version number is wrong (it conflated transformers with peft), but I confirmed peft 0.19.1's LoraConfig source contains use_dora, use_rslora, and init_lora_weights, so the technical conclusion still holds.

EFFORT (raised 2 -> 3): DoRA + rsLoRA are true one-liners, but PiSSA is NOT — init_lora_weights='pissa' needs SVD init at load (slow on big models) plus special adapter conversion on save (convert_pissa_to_lora / residual-model handling) or the saved adapter is incorrect. Two trainer entry points (HF + Unsloth, whose get_peft_model has different variant-kwarg support) plus manifest/provenance recording and a smoke test push it to ~3 days.

IMPACT (lowered high -> medium): DoRA's +3.7% headline is low-rank commonsense; gains compress at the r=16-64 kolm uses, and rsLoRA only matters at high rank. These are real but marginal polish on an SFT-on-teacher-strings pipeline whose larger credibility gap (per the same audit file) is the total absence of a logit-level KD loss. Worth doing as a cheap quality knob; not a high-impact differentiator on its own.

findings.title: Add a standard, Arena-correlated eval harness (Arena-Hard-Auto + AlpacaEval2-LC + IFEval)
findings.technique: Arena-Hard-Auto (LLM-judge pairwise vs baseline, Bradley-Terry, 98.6% Chatbot-Arena correlation), AlpacaEval 2 length-controlled (0.98 Spearman), IFEval (programmatic instruction-following, no judge)
findings.source: lmarena/arena-hard-auto (github); Arena-Hard/BenchBuilder arXiv:2406.11939; Length-Controlled AlpacaEval arXiv:2404.04475; IFEval arXiv:2311.07911
findings.current_state: src/bench-eval-suites.js is a custom in-repo manifest (asks_one_question_rate, judge_clarify_rate, mean_chars, cost_per_1k). Good for kolm's support narrative but not externally recognized. adversarial-eval.js name-drops MixEval-Hard but uses a deterministic local judge fallback, not the real benchmark. kolm already has a Bradley-Terry impl (src/bradley-terry.js) — half the Arena-Hard machinery exists.
findings.proposed_change: Add src/bench-arena-hard.js: run candidate + a fixed baseline (base student) over the Arena-Hard-Auto prompt set, judge pairwise with a strong teacher (council can judge), feed win/loss into the EXISTING bradley-terry.js to produce an Arena-Hard score with CIs. Add IFEval as a no-judge programmatic suite. Add length-control to the AlpacaEval-style win-rate. Surface as new suites in bench-eval-suites.js so `kolm bench compare` gets industry-standard numbers the website can cite.
findings.rationale: kolm's X04 mandate is 'every number on kolm.ai must trace to verifiable measurement' — but current suites are kolm-invented, so 'beats Claude/GPT-4o-mini' rests on custom metrics an outsider can't reproduce. Arena-Hard-Auto + AlpacaEval2-LC are the two highest Chatbot-Arena-correlated automatic benchmarks and what buyers trust. kolm already has Bradley-Terry, so marginal cost is the prompt set + judge plumbing, not the stats engine. Directly hardens the headline benchmark claims.

verdict.reason:
PARTIALLY REFUTED on framing, but the core gap is REAL and NOT fully present. Verdict: real gap, more already-built than the finding credits, but no end-to-end runnable harness exists.

WHAT ALREADY EXISTS (more than the finding implies):
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/apps/eval/packs.py ALREADY ships official loaders for ArenaHard (HF dataset lmsys/arena-hard-auto-v0.1, load_arenahard) and AlpacaEval (tatsu-lab/alpaca_eval, load_alpacaeval), plus MedQA/FinQA/LegalBench/GPQA/HumanEval/MT-Bench. These are the REAL benchmark prompt sets, not name-drops.
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/apps/eval/judge.py ALREADY implements pairwise LLM-judge (judge_pairwise) with (A,B)/(B,A) order bias-correction producing A-win-rate — i.e. the core Arena-Hard / AlpacaEval win-rate engine. So 'judge plumbing' and the prompt sets already exist, not just Bradley-Terry.
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/bradley-terry.js is a full BT fitter with Wald CIs (fitBradleyTerry, predictPairProb).

WHAT IS GENUINELY MISSING (net-new):
- IFEval: NO programmatic instruction-following checker exists anywhere in code; all 'ifeval'/'instruction-follow' matches are in public/*.html marketing pages and the audit folder. Real net-new work (original IFEval = ~25 verifiable instruction types).
- Length-controlled win-rate (AlpacaEval2-LC GLM regression): absent. judge.py emits raw win-rate only.
- INTEGRATION: the Python packs.py/judge.py are ORPHANED — nothing imports them (no CLI verb, no runner, no route; only its own pycache shows it's never been a dependency). The `kolm bench compare` surface (cli/kolm.js ~48074, src/bench-eval-suites.js) only knows 4 custom JS suites and has NO path to ArenaHard/AlpacaEval. The audit's own research_summary admits 'no standard eval harness'.
- The proposed 'feed verdicts into the EXISTING bradley-terry.js' is questionable: BT is JS and scoped (per its own header) to K-Score human-preference calibration, while the judge is Python — so this is cross-language plumbing the finding underestimates, not a free reuse.

EFFORT: 6 days is optimistic-to-fair. Prompt sets + pairwise judge already done (saves time), but IFEval verifiers + AlpacaEval2-LC regression + Python<->JS bench-surface bridging are underestimated. Realistic ~6-9 days.

IMPACT: real for the X04 'every number traces to verifiable measurement' credibility mandate, but 'high' is slightly overblown. kolm's buyers distill narrow support/domain models; Arena-Hard/AlpacaEval measure broad chat quality, not the support-clarity K-Score the product actually sells. It hardens the website 'beats Claude/GPT-4o-mini' headline (credibility/marketing) more than it unblocks a user workflow. Adjusted to medium.

PREREQS finding omits: a way to actually run the orphaned Python packs (a runner/CLI verb), and the Python<->JS bridge to surface results in `kolm bench compare`.

findings.title: GaLore / FSDP2 for memory-efficient FULL fine-tune (full-ft is in the enum but impractical today)
findings.technique: GaLore (gradient low-rank projection: full-rank weight updates at LoRA-like memory, optimizer-state -65%) + 8-bit GaLore; FSDP2 / FSDP-QLoRA for multi-GPU sharding
findings.source: GaLore arXiv:2403.03507 (full-rank quality, fits 7B on 24GB; HF TrainingArguments optim='galore_adamw'); FSDP2 (pytorch); Answer.AI FSDP+QLoRA
findings.current_state: full-ft is a declared DISTILLATION_METHOD but the trainer only wires LoRA/QLoRA; a true full fine-tune of a 7B+ on a single 5090 OOMs (W869 VRAM note confirms even bf16 7B is tight with teacher resident). DeepSpeed pass-through + accelerate --multi_gpu/FSDP flags exist but are 'partial / never exercised' per the audit (single-GPU box).
findings.proposed_change: Add --optim galore_adamw / galore_adamw_8bit support in train_lora.py (HF Trainer accepts these optim strings + optim_target_modules) so full-rank updates fit consumer VRAM — making the 'full-ft' enum honest on one GPU. Pair with existing FSDP flags for multi-GPU and add a smoke test that actually exercises the accelerate launch path (currently untested). Record optimizer + projection rank in manifest.
findings.rationale: kolm advertises full-ft as a method but cannot realistically run it on its reference hardware; GaLore is a single Trainer optim string giving near-full-rank quality at LoRA-ish memory, making the claim real. Lower impact than DoRA/Liger because most users want adapters not full-FT, but it closes an honesty gap in the enum and the multi-GPU FSDP path is shipped-but-untested (an audit-flag class).

verdict.reason: VERIFIED, not refuted, though impact is overblown. Core claims check out against actual code: (1) `full-ft` IS in the enum (workers/distill/catalog.mjs:198) but train_lora.py:426-433 unconditionally calls get_peft_model(base, lora_cfg) with no full-fine-tune branch anywhere — selecting full-ft silently degrades to a plain LoRA fp16 train (distill.mjs:493 just omits --qlora). The enum value is genuinely non-functional. (2) GaLore is absent from all product code (no `galore`/`galore_adamw`/`optim_target_modules` token outside the audit JSON itself); src/distill-efficiency.js covers only precision/grad-ckpt/early-stop. (3) --optim passthrough exists (train_lora.py:295,483) but GaLore also needs optim_target_modules which is NOT wired, so --optim galore_adamw alone fails — finding states this correctly. (4) FSDP/multi-GPU IS shipped-but-untested: distill.mjs:515-534 builds `accelerate launch --multi_gpu --use_fsdp --fsdp_sharding_strategy=FULL_SHARD`, and no test exercises it (tests/wave727-accelerate.test.js is speculative-decoding, unrelated despite the shared word). already_partially_present:true is accurate (--optim + FSDP flags present, GaLore wiring absent), so alreadyPresent=false for the proposed GaLore/full-ft-honesty change overall. CAVEATS that reduce severity below the claimed 'medium': the Studio doc (public/docs/studio-distill.html:97) already scopes full-ft to 'rare; only for <1B', a size that fully fine-tunes on a 5090 with NO GaLore needed — so GaLore's headline (7B full-ft on 24GB) targets a use case kolm doesn't advertise. The actual defect is simpler/cheaper than the proposal: either make full-ft truly skip get_peft_model, or drop it from the enum. Unstated dependency: the proposed FSDP smoke test needs a multi-GPU box (audit confirms single-GPU reference hardware), so it can't run where it matters. Effort ~3d is plausible only if it includes the real full-ft trainer branch; adjust impact to low (most distill users want adapters; this is a narrow honesty/correctness gap on the audited surface).

Two independent docker-compose + k8s generators (serve-autodetect.js vs deploy-generators.js) with different image refs (ggerganov vs ggml-org), ports (8788 vs 8000), structure — duplicate sources of truth.

Air-gap bundle is not self-contained: vllm wheelhouse / llama.cpp binary must be pre-staged on an internet box; installer exits 1 otherwise.

Out-of-the-box local quantize does nothing until the operator manually builds a venv + installs requirements.txt (by-design isolation, real first-run friction).
v2 passport sub-objects (kv_cache/speculative_decoding/prompt_cache/continuous_batching) have attach helpers but no integration that populates them on a real manifest.
audit.summary: The Run/Serve & Deploy surface is structurally solid and unit-tested: serve auto-detect (gguf->llama.cpp / safetensors->vllm|mlx) is a clean pure decision table wired into the CLI; deploy generators (docker-compose, k8s, vLLM config, deterministic air-gap tar.gz with offline verifier) are real and green; hardware detection has an honest CPU floor; remote-compute catalog + launch-plan builders are correct; the quantize worker isolates heavy ML deps with per-method doctor gating and a real 724-line quantize.py. The largest substantive gap: NO production path ever produces a measured ('tested') runtime passport — the referenced apps/export/probe.py does not exist, so recordTestedPassport and all v2 enrichment helpers are unit-test-only and every artifact ships estimate-only performance rows. Secondary issues: a self-contradicting exllamav2 path inside serve-autodetect.js, two divergent docker/k8s generators, willArtifactFit is a stub, serve doesn't pre-check artifact existence, the /bin/false fallback is non-portable on Windows, and the remote-compute catalog is hard-coded/unverified. Tests: r1/r3/r4/wave250 = 74 pass / 0 fail / 1 skip; deploy/airgap suites = 77 pass / 1 fail (cosmetic stale sw.js cache-token assertion only, not a functional regression). Full result written to C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/audit/w920/run.audit.json."

findings.title: Multi-LoRA serving (one base, N adapters, per-request switch) is implemented in Python but not wired into serve.py, the CLI, or any HTTP endpoint
findings.technique: S-LoRA / Punica-style multi-adapter serving with dynamic load/unload endpoint
findings.source: S-LoRA arXiv:2311.03285; Punica arXiv:2310.18547; vLLM LoRA docs https://docs.vllm.ai/en/stable/features/lora/ (POST /v1/load_lora_adapter, VLLM_ALLOW_RUNTIME_LORA_UPDATING); Anyscale multi-LoRA https://docs.anyscale.com/llm/serving/multi-lora
findings.current_state: apps/runtime/multi_lora.py has a full MultiLoraRouter (register/generate/LRU evict, S-LoRA+Punica+LoRAX cites). But serve.py only loads ONE adapter: has_lora_pack() extracts a single adapter_model.safetensors and binds it once via enable_lora=True + PeftModel.from_pretrained (serve.py:160-161, 265-268). serve.py never imports multi_lora.py. No CLI flag mounts multiple adapters; no /v1/load_lora_adapter or /v1/adapters route exists. One fine-tuned variant = one whole server process today.
findings.proposed_change: Wire MultiLoraRouter into serve.py request loop: add a --lora-modules id=path,... CLI flag (matching vLLM spelling) plumbed via KOLM_LORA_MODULES; pass LoRARequest per request keyed off the OpenAI model field; add POST /v1/load_lora_adapter + /v1/unload_lora_adapter + GET /v1/adapters guarded behind an explicit KOLM_ALLOW_RUNTIME_LORA flag. Record served adapter_id in the receipt (multi_lora.py:38 already promises this).
findings.rationale: This is the single biggest economic differentiator of every serving competitor (vLLM, LMDeploy, SGLang, Fireworks, Predibase/LoRAX): serve dozens of customer-specific fine-tunes on one GPU instead of N processes each holding the base weights. kolm distills LoRA adapters as a first-class output (compile-targets.js lora target) but cannot serve more than one at a time, so the value of the distill product leaks at the serve boundary. The Python module already exists, so this is integration not invention.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, not already wired. apps/runtime/multi_lora.py contains a complete MultiLoraRouter (register/_swap_active via PeftModel.load_adapter+set_adapter/LRU _evict_if_needed/generate returning adapter_id+swap_ms, plus receipt_block() at line 354 pinning adapter_id) but it is dead code: grep across the whole repo (excluding the module itself, audit files, and docs) finds ZERO importers. serve.py confirms the single-adapter claim exactly — has_lora_pack() (serve.py:54-66) extracts ONE adapter_model.safetensors, binds it once via vLLM enable_lora=True (serve.py:160-161) or, on the transformers path, PeftModel.from_pretrained + merge_and_unload() (serve.py:265-269, which actively fuses the single adapter — the literal opposite of hot-swappable multi-adapter serving). serve.py never imports multi_lora. The CLI serve --http path (cli/kolm.js ~13644-13850) passes one positional artifact and exposes only --optimize/--kv-cache/--speculative/--num-speculative-tokens/--prompt-cache/--max-batch — no --lora-modules or any multi-adapter flag. src/router.js has no /v1/load_lora_adapter, /v1/adapters, or adapter_id route (grep: no matches). No test references multi_lora/MultiLoraRouter by name (the 5 test-file hits matched the bare word "adapters"). So the finding's five technical claims are all accurate.

SEVERITY IS ELEVATED BY A DOCS OVERCLAIM the finding did not mention: public/research.html lists "Multi-LoRA serving (S-LoRA pattern)" as item #30 inside a block the closer at line 1220 explicitly calls "This is the production-shipping list ... bench-checked against real captures," states "The receipt pins which adapter ran each request," and links a /research/multi-lora-serving.html report page. The receipt-pinning and per-request swap it advertises live only in the unwired module, so the public page describes serve-time behavior the product cannot perform — a credibility gap, not just a missing feature.

EFFORT: 5 days is light-but-defensible, nudge to ~7. The hidden cost the finding glosses: multi_lora.py is a transformers/PEFT implementation (PeftModel.set_adapter), but serve.py PREFERS vLLM, whose multi-LoRA uses its own LoRARequest + per-request adapter-dir API, not PeftModel — so "wire MultiLoraRouter into serve.py" is really two separate integrations (a vLLM LoRARequest path AND the existing PEFT path), plus the OpenAI model-field routing, three HTTP endpoints, the KOLM_ALLOW_RUNTIME_LORA guard, and the receipt/K-score signing the docs already promise.

IMPACT: down-rate critical -> high. The single-adapter serve path is functional, so the distill product is not broken; the gap is (a) cannot co-tenant N customer fine-tunes on one GPU — a real serving-economics differentiator vs vLLM/LoRAX/Predibase — and (b) a shipped-list overclaim to fix. Genuine and worth doing, but not product-down critical. kolm's distill-LoRA-first users would care, especially any multi-tenant/serving customer.

Files cited by the finding are correct: apps/runtime/serve.py, apps/runtime/multi_lora.py, cli/kolm.js, src/router.js. Add public/research.html (item #30, lines 1083-1090) and public/research/multi-lora-serving.html to the change set to retire/qualify the shipped claim.

findings.title: EAGLE-3 / Medusa / lookahead self-speculation modules exist but serve.py only supports the heavier draft-model spec path
findings.technique: EAGLE-3 training-time-test self-speculative decoding (de facto 2026 industrial standard)
findings.source: EAGLE-3 arXiv:2503.01840; EAGLE 3.1 (vLLM+TorchSpec, 2026-05-26) https://vllm.ai/blog/2026-05-26-eagle-3-1; SGLang H100 373 vs 158 tok/s (2.36x), 1.70x system throughput
findings.current_state: src/speculative-decoding.js + serve.py wire ONLY the draft-model path (llm_kwargs['speculative_model']=draft_model, serve.py:157-159) using DRAFT_PAIRINGS to a separate small model. apps/runtime/eagle3.py, medusa.py, lookahead.py exist as standalone modules but serve.py imports none of them. src/spec-decode.js can TRAIN an eagle3/medusa head but the trained head is never loaded at serve time. The cheap, no-second-model EAGLE path (current SOTA) is built but unreachable from kolm serve.
findings.proposed_change: Extend resolveSpeculative() to detect an EAGLE/Medusa head packed in the .kolm artifact (spec_decode_pair_hash) and, when present, set vLLM speculative_config={method:'eagle3', model:<head>} instead of a draft model. Import eagle3.py into serve.py engine builders. Prefer the in-artifact EAGLE head over DRAFT_PAIRINGS when both resolve. Closes the loop between kolm spec-decode train and kolm serve.
findings.rationale: EAGLE-3 is now the default in vLLM and SGLang and gives 1.4-2.4x decode speedup with zero accuracy change and no second model resident in VRAM. kolm already trains the head; not serving it wastes the work and leaves the headline tok/s below every competitor. Wiring is moderate because the artifact-binding (spec_decode_pair_hash) already exists in the receipt chain.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, NOT REFUTED. The finding's core technical claims all hold against the actual code:

1. serve.py wires ONLY the draft-model path. apps/runtime/serve.py:157-159 sets llm_kwargs["speculative_model"]=draft_model + num_speculative_tokens; the draft is resolved from DRAFT_PAIRINGS via pick_draft() (serve.py:448). It never sets vLLM speculative_config={method:'eagle3',...}.

2. The self-speculation modules exist but are unreachable from serve. apps/runtime/eagle3.py (attach_eagle3 sets engine.speculative_config with draft_model_type:'eagle3'), medusa.py, and lookahead.py are standalone. Grep across all .py confirms serve.py imports NONE of them (eagle3.py and lookahead.py match only themselves; medusa.py is imported nowhere). serve.py's only imports are stdlib + lazy vllm/transformers/apps.trainer.speculative.

3. src/spec-decode.js can TRAIN an eagle3/medusa head (trainSpecDecode, draftKind default 'eagle3', DRAFT_KINDS=['eagle','eagle2','eagle3','medusa']) via cmdSpecDecode in cli/kolm.js:21933, but the trained head (written to a run_dir) is never loaded at serve time. resolveSpeculative() in src/speculative-decoding.js only ever returns a draft_model from DRAFT_PAIRINGS — no in-artifact head detection. So the loop between `kolm spec-decode train` and `kolm serve` is genuinely open.

WHERE THE FINDING IS WRONG (raises effort, does not refute):
The rationale claims "the artifact-binding (spec_decode_pair_hash) already exists in the receipt chain" — FALSE. spec_decode_pair_hash appears ONLY as a comment in src/spec-decode.js:13. It is never written into any manifest/artifact/receipt, and trainSpecDecode writes to an isolated ~/.kolm/spec-decode-runs/ dir that no compile or serve path reads. So the artifact-binding the finding leans on for "moderate wiring" must be built from scratch: compile must pack the head into the .kolm, the manifest must record it, verifyArtifact must bind it, AND eagle3.py's kwargs (draft_model_type/posterior_threshold) do not match current vLLM speculative_config={"method":"eagle3","model":...} schema, so the module needs rework not just an import. Training also depends on an external $KOLM_SPECDECODE_TRAINER (default state is no_trainer_installed). Net: ~7 days, not 4.

IMPACT adjusted high->medium: the default serve path ALREADY does speculative decoding (draft-model, 2-3x). EAGLE-3 is an incremental decode-speed gain plus removal of the second-model VRAM cost — a refinement of a shipped capability, not a missing one. Most kolm users serve via the draft-model path that already works.

Evidence files (absolute):
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/apps/runtime/serve.py (lines 137,157-159,448,460-463)
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/apps/runtime/eagle3.py (attach_eagle3, never imported)
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/apps/runtime/medusa.py, lookahead.py (standalone, unimported)
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/speculative-decoding.js (resolveSpeculative, DRAFT_PAIRINGS only)
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/spec-decode.js (trains head; spec_decode_pair_hash is only a comment at line 13)
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/cli/kolm.js:21933 (cmdSpecDecode)

findings.title: Disaggregated prefill/decode (disagg.py) is fully designed with a spec schema but never wired into serve or deploy generators
findings.technique: P/D disaggregation (DistServe/Mooncake) for ~2x goodput on multi-GPU/multi-host fleets
findings.source: DistServe arXiv:2401.09670; Mooncake arXiv:2407.00079; vLLM disagg production-stack https://docs.vllm.ai/projects/production-stack/; LMSYS DeepSeek-R1 96xH100 5x output throughput (May 2025)
findings.current_state: apps/runtime/disagg.py has the full design: SINGLE/INPROC/CROSSHOST modes, a kolm artifact serve.disagg spec block, kv_transport nvlink/rdma/tcp, and emits vLLM --kv-transfer-config kwargs. But serve.py does not import disagg.py and the k8s emitter generates only single-pool Deployments. The 1.4-2.5x goodput is designed and unreachable.
findings.proposed_change: Phase 1 (cheap): wire INPROC disagg into serve.py when >=2 local GPUs and the artifact carries serve.disagg.mode=inproc, passing disagg.py engine kwargs straight through. Phase 2: have the k8s generator emit a prefill Deployment + decode Deployment + KV-transfer Service when serve.disagg.mode=crosshost, reusing disagg.py router contract. Gate behind explicit opt-in; default stays SINGLE.
findings.rationale: Disagg moved from research to core architecture at Meta/LinkedIn/Mistral/HF in 2025. The INPROC path is a genuine free 1.4-1.8x on any 2-GPU box and is low-effort because the module is done. CROSSHOST is higher effort and only matters for large fleets, so phase it. Ranked medium because it only helps multi-GPU users, a minority of kolm's likely deployers.

verdict.reason: VERIFIED REAL, NOT ALREADY PRESENT. apps/runtime/disagg.py is a complete, self-contained module (DisaggMode SINGLE/INPROC/CROSSHOST, DisaggConfig.for_vllm_engine_args emitting kv_transfer_config kwargs, from_spec_field reading a serve.disagg block, auto_pick, CrossHostRouter) but has ZERO call sites. apps/runtime/serve.py builds a single vllm.LLM(**llm_kwargs) and never imports disagg.py / DisaggConfig / from_spec_field / KOLM_DISAGG. src/deploy-generators.js generateKubernetesManifests emits exactly one single-pool Deployment + Service + ConfigMap + PVC + Job + HPA with no prefill/decode split or KV-transfer Service; generateVllmConfig never emits --kv-transfer-config. The serve.disagg spec field is parsed nowhere in src/. No test under tests/ exercises disagg.py. The CLI serve path passes no disagg flag/env. The only other 'disagg' mention is an unrelated comment in apps/trainer/moe.py pointing at adapter_pool.py. So the 1.4-2.5x goodput is genuinely designed-and-unreachable. CAVEAT 1 (effort underestimated): the finding frames Phase 1 INPROC as 'cheap... pass engine kwargs straight through,' which is technically wrong. vLLM DistServe-style disaggregation is NOT achieved by handing kv_transfer_config to a single in-process LLM(); it needs two separate engine instances (prefill producer + decode consumer) plus a router. serve.py's single-LLM/synchronous _gen design would require real restructuring, and disagg.py's INPROC branch even returns kv_role='kv_both' (not a valid producer/consumer split). 6 days is optimistic. CAVEAT 2 (impact slightly overblown): a 2-GPU box can already get throughput via tensor_parallel_size (already exposed in generateVllmConfig); disagg's advantage over TP shows up mainly at high concurrency / multi-node, and the finding itself concedes it helps only the multi-GPU minority. Given kolm's local/air-gap-leaning audience, low-to-medium is more defensible than medium. Unstated prereqs: vLLM 0.7+ working KV connectors, matched pool configs, RDMA/IB for CROSSHOST, and a /v1/disagg/prefill + /v1/disagg/decode HTTP contract the serve shim does not expose.

findings.title: No multi-LoRA / batched-LoRA kernel awareness; deploy quantization config doesn't steer vLLM toward Marlin/Machete W4A16 kernels
findings.technique: Marlin (2.6x GPTQ, 10.9x AWQ) and Machete (Hopper mixed-input GEMM) W4A16 kernel selection
findings.source: Red Hat Machete https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2024/10/14/introducing-machete-mixed-input-gemm-kernel; vLLM quantization guide (AWQ 67->741 tok/s with Marlin) https://jarvislabs.ai/blog/vllm-quantization-complete-guide-benchmarks
findings.current_state: src/export-fp8.js + export-nvfp4.js produce hardware-native low-bit artifacts. src/forge-fit.js ranks awq-4bit/gptq-4bit but has no notion of Marlin/Machete kernels (grep: no marlin/machete/w4a16 anywhere). deploy-generators.js passes a quantization string but does not hint kv_cache or kernel selection, and vLLM picks the slow kernel unless the model layout is kernel-compatible. A W4A16 model deployed without Marlin runs up to ~10x slower.
findings.proposed_change: Add kernel-awareness to forge-fit ranking and the runtime passport: for awq/gptq-4bit on Ampere+/Hopper, annotate 'marlin-eligible'/'machete-eligible' and surface it in the serve banner + deploy README. Optionally set vLLM quantization to 'gptq_marlin'/'awq_marlin' in the generated config when target_hardware supports it.
findings.rationale: kolm's whole pitch is 'quantize then run fast on your hardware'. Shipping a W4A16 artifact whose serve config doesn't request the Marlin/Machete path leaves a 2.6-10.9x kernel speedup on the table for the exact quantization formats kolm produces. It's a config-and-annotation change, not a kernel build, so effort is low for credibility-high throughput numbers.

verdict.reason:
Technical premise VERIFIED in actual code, not refuted. Grep for marlin/machete/w4a16/gptq_marlin/awq_marlin returns zero hits in product source (only the audit file itself + aspirational research docs at docs/research/*).
