Confirmed across all relevant layers: (1) src/forge-fit.js QUALITY_ORDER ranks awq-4bit/gptq-4bit (lines 114-118) with no kernel notion; (2) src/deploy-generators.js generateVllmConfig (line 458) passes `quantization` straight through with no kernel/Marlin steering, and is not even referenced from router.js; (3) src/runtime-passport.js has rich v2 enrichment (kv_cache/speculative_decoding/prompt_cache/continuous_batching) but NO kernel-eligibility field; (4) src/quantization-oracle.js tracks `runtimes` per method but no kernel selection; (5) apps/runtime/serve.py never sets a `quantization=` kwarg at all and has zero marlin/machete reference. src/kernel-selector.js EXISTS but is unrelated — it picks latency-vs-batching workload profiles, not GEMM kernels. So already_partially_present:false is accurate.

IMPACT IS OVERBLOWN — downgrade medium->low. The "up to ~10x slower" / "2.6-10.9x left on the table" headline does not reflect the real user scenario. The generated configs explicitly target vLLM 0.6.x+ and `vllm/vllm-openai:latest`, and modern vLLM AUTO-SELECTS gptq_marlin/awq_marlin kernels by default for Marlin-compatible GPTQ/AWQ checkpoints on Ampere+ (detected from the checkpoint's quantization_config). You generally do NOT need to set quantization:'gptq_marlin' manually. The 67->741 tok/s number is the Marlin-vs-no-Marlin delta, not "kolm's config vs ideal" on current vLLM. The real residual value is the annotation/credibility surface (proving the artifact is Marlin-eligible, fitting kolm's receipt ethos) plus a safety-net explicit hint for older vLLM / non-standard packing — genuine but worth far less than the cited multipliers. It also overlaps heavily with the sibling finding in this same audit about deploy-config drift (FP8 KV-cache + prefix-cache parity), which is the higher-value config-parity work and largely subsumes the kernel-hint piece.

EFFORT roughly right, slightly low at 3 days. The hard part is a correct kernel-eligibility predicate (Marlin sm80+, Machete sm90, plus group-size/act-order packing constraints) without overclaiming — not the trivial "awq/gptq on Ampere+" the proposal implies. ~3.5 days with tests.

Verdict: real, not already present, but a low-impact polish/annotation item (not a frontier throughput unlock) because the underlying kernel speedup is already delivered by vLLM auto-detection.

findings.title: apps/export/probe.py (the only documented estimated->tested passport upgrade path) does not exist, so 'estimated' is permanent in production
findings.technique: Real measured runtime passport via a load-and-probe harness
findings.source: kolm src/spec-compile.js:580-586 names probe.py as the upgrade path; recordTestedPassport (runtime-passport.js:585-619) is the strict consumer waiting for it
findings.current_state: spec-compile.js:583 references apps/export/probe.py as the path that would load exported bytes into a live runtime and emit tested rows; ls apps/export/ confirms it is MISSING (coreml.py, executorch.py, onnx.py, tensorrt.py exist but no probe.py). recordTestedPassport is strict + correct but has no production caller. Every shipped passport is therefore status=estimated forever; the v2 enrichment helpers are exercised only by unit tests.
findings.proposed_change: Build apps/export/probe.py: take an exported artifact + a runtime pick (reuse serve-autodetect decision), boot it for one short generation, measure tok_s_p50, ttft_ms, peak memory_mb, and emit the TESTED_REQUIRED_NUMERIC rows recordTestedPassport expects. Wire kolm compile --probe (or auto on a GPU host) to call it and stamp a tested passport. Attach v2 sub-objects (kv_cache dtype used, spec acceptance rate, prefix-cache hit rate) from serve.py /info counters.
findings.rationale: kolm's entire brand is 'the receipt is the proof' and 'every number traces to a measurement'. A passport that can only ever say 'estimated' contradicts that core claim on the Run surface. The consumer (recordTestedPassport) and schema are already built and strict; the missing piece is the one harness that produces real numbers. High leverage on the trust story.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL (could not refute). Core technical claims all check out against the actual code: (1) apps/export/probe.py does NOT exist (apps/export/ has coreml/executorch/onnx/tensorrt/gguf/mlx/run/registry/model_card/sbom/ai_act_docs .py, no probe.py); spec-compile.js:583 names it as the upgrade path. (2) recordTestedPassport (runtime-passport.js:585) has zero production callers — only its own def, a comment, 2 test files, and the audit JSONs reference it. (3) compileSpec only ever emits status='estimated' via estimatePassport (spec-compile.js:616); it never calls recordTestedPassport. (4) The `kolm runtime probe` command that assurance-case.js:315 tells users to run does NOT exist — cmdRuntime has no `probe` subverb. (5) serve.py measures tok_s/ttft_ms and the sidecar exposes /metrics, but neither writes a tested passport back into a manifest, so there is no closed estimated->tested loop. So in shipped artifacts, every runtime_passport row is status='estimated' permanently.

ONE NUANCE the finding gets wrong: src/export-gguf.js ALREADY contains a real load-and-probe harness — coherenceTest() spawns llama-cli, generates ~100 tokens, measures tok_s/wall_ms, and emits status:'tested' (lines 653-670). So probe.py is NOT 'the only path' and the tested branch is not 'exercised only by unit tests' as a code-path matter. BUT both exportGguf() call sites (cli/kolm.js:8118, :8612) hard-code dryRun:true, making that tested branch unreachable from any shipped CLI command (only an env-gated test KOLM_S1_REAL_ROUNDTRIP=1 hits it). Net: the practical conclusion ('estimated is permanent in production') stands. The cleaner fix is arguably to wire export-gguf's existing tested path into the real compile flow rather than build probe.py from scratch — but capturing ttft_ms/peak-mem/v2 sub-objects (kv_cache dtype, spec acceptance, prefix-cache hit) across runtimes is genuinely extra work.

Key files: src/spec-compile.js:580-626, src/runtime-passport.js:585-619, src/export-gguf.js:616-685, cli/kolm.js:8116-8124 + 8597-8618 (both dryRun:true), src/serve-metrics-sidecar.js, apps/runtime/serve.py:480-499.

findings.title: kolm serve never fs.existsSync(artifact) before spawning the runtime child, and buildCommand falls back to /bin/false on unmapped runtimes, both producing opaque failures, worst on Windows
findings.technique: Pre-flight artifact + runtime validation with clear NOT_FOUND/UNSUPPORTED envelopes
findings.source: kolm audit findings (cli/kolm.js:13907-14062; src/serve-autodetect.js:417 /bin/false; selectRuntime exllamav2 drift line 53-56)
findings.current_state: cmdServe checks the optional .manifest.json sidecar but never stats the model file, so a typo'd path fails deep inside the spawned vLLM/llama.cpp child with that runtime's error, not a kolm NOT_FOUND. serve-autodetect buildCommand returns {bin:'/bin/false'} for unmapped runtimes (exllamav2 is returned by RUNTIME_SELECTION but has no KNOWN_RUNTIMES entry / no buildCommand branch); on Windows '/bin/false' is an opaque ENOENT instead of a clear 'runtime not wired' message.
findings.proposed_change: Add fs.existsSync(ap) up front in cmdServe -> exit EXIT.NOT_FOUND with the resolved path. Replace the /bin/false fallback with a structured {ok:false, error:'runtime_not_wired', runtime, hint} that cmdServe surfaces cleanly on all platforms. Resolve the exllamav2 drift: add it to KNOWN_RUNTIMES + a buildCommand branch, or remove it from RUNTIME_SELECTION so the two tables stop contradicting (the header already warns about this).
findings.rationale: First-run experience: a bad path or half-wired runtime should fail with a kolm-shaped error in <1s, not a vLLM traceback or a Windows ENOENT. Cheap, removes a known same-file contradiction the source itself flags, and improves cross-platform behavior on the host class (Windows) the maintainer develops on.

verdict.reason:
Partially confirmed, but impact is overstated. I verified the actual code at C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/cli/kolm.js and C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/serve-autodetect.js.

(1) MISSING fs.existsSync(ap) — REAL. _cmdServeAutodetect (cli/kolm.js:13895-14077) resolves ap at 13907, reads only the OPTIONAL sidecar (13945 is the single existsSync in the whole function, on ap+'.manifest.json'), runs detectRuntime, then spawns the child at 14062 with no stat of the model file. A typo'd raw-model path fails inside the spawned llama-server/vLLM child, not as a clean kolm NOT_FOUND. This IS an inconsistency: the two sibling serve paths both validate — the --http path (13654 fs.existsSync(ap) -> EXIT.NOT_FOUND) and the manifest-emitter path (13319 same). So the proposed fix is genuinely absent on this one path. Note the spawn('error') handler (14072-14076) already gives a kolm-shaped ENOENT message — but only for a missing RUNTIME BINARY, not a missing model file.

(2) /bin/false fallback (serve-autodetect.js:417) — REAL as code but effectively DEAD. buildCommand is only ever called by detectRuntime with llama.cpp/vllm/mlx/ollama; the else-branch is unreachable in the boot path. The cross-platform Windows ENOENT concern is therefore theoretical, not a live failure mode.

(3) exllamav2 drift — REAL contradiction but NOT reachable in production. selectRuntime() (the only function that returns exllamav2, via RUNTIME_SELECTION.exl2, lines 53-56/155) is called ONLY from tests/wrapper-r-enrich.test.js — never from cmdServe. The real boot path uses detectRuntime(), whose artifactFormat() only recognizes gguf/safetensors/mlx and returns 'unsupported' otherwise; and the autodetect routing regex at cli/kolm.js:13297 is /\.(gguf|safetensors|mlx)$/i, so a .exl2 file never even enters this path. selectRuntime and detectRuntime are never composed, so 'selectRuntime hands back a runtime serve cannot boot' cannot occur at runtime. The file header (152-154) already documents this as a known drift hazard.

Net: only sub-claim (1) is a worth-fixing user-facing papercut, and even that affects the uncommon raw-file autodetect path, not the primary .kolm --http flow (which already validates). EXIT.NOT_FOUND exists (=5). Effort is overstated: the core fix is ~3 lines (fs.existsSync(ap) guard after line 13907) plus an optional table cleanup; ~0.5 day, not 2. Impact is low, not medium — the highest-traffic serve path already does the right thing, and parts (2)/(3) are dead/test-only code, not live bugs.

findings.title: Two parallel docker/k8s emitters (serve-autodetect vs deploy-generators) disagree on image refs and ports, a duplicate source of truth
findings.technique: Single deterministic deploy-artifact generator
findings.source: kolm audit (serve-autodetect.js:425-627 ggerganov + :8788 vs deploy-generators.js:60-425 ggml-org + :8000); serve comment line 421 admits 'minimal-but-valid fallback'
findings.current_state: serve-autodetect emits docker/k8s with image ggerganov/llama.cpp and port 8788; deploy-generators emits ggml-org image and port 8000, different structure. kolm serve --docker/--k8s uses one; kolm deploy uses the other. Two operators following two kolm paths get two incompatible manifests for the same model.
findings.proposed_change: Delete the serve-autodetect docker/k8s branch and route kolm serve --docker/--k8s to deploy-generators.js (the deterministic, test-covered, round-trip-validated one). Keep serve-autodetect to the pure runtime-decision table. Update ggerganov->ggml-org consistently (ggerganov is the old org name).
findings.rationale: Two generators for one job is a latent inconsistency that bites when one is updated and the other isn't, and ggerganov/llama.cpp was renamed to ggml-org/llama.cpp so one emitter already points at a stale image name. Deduping to the tested generator is a near-free correctness + maintenance win.

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED real, not already deduped. Two independent emitters exist: src/serve-autodetect.js:425-627 (buildDockerCompose/buildK8sManifests, image ghcr.io/ggerganov/llama.cpp:server at lines 427/431/493/497, default port 8788) and src/deploy-generators.js:60-425 (generateDockerCompose/generateKubernetesManifests, image ghcr.io/ggml-org/llama.cpp:server at 118/204, port 8000). ggerganov->ggml-org IS the real upstream rename, so serve-autodetect points at a stale org name. Both are independently test-pinned: tests/r3-kolm-serve.test.js:203 asserts 'ggerganov'; tests/r4-deploy-generators.test.js:67 asserts 'ggml-org' and :119/122/127 assert port 8000. So a dedup must also update the serve test. TWO framing corrections to the finding: (1) The finding says 'kolm deploy uses the other' — imprecise. cmdDeploy (cli/kolm.js:32183) only does bundle --airgap + --device SSH, NOT docker/k8s. deploy-generators docker/k8s is reached via 'kolm export --format docker-compose|k8s' (cli/kolm.js:38950-38964). So the divergent paths are 'kolm serve --docker/--k8s' vs 'kolm export --format ...'. (2) The 8788 vs 8000 split is partly intentional (local serve default vs cluster export default) and serve's port flows from --port, so port is not a hardcoded fork like the image is. NOT already implemented (no shared generator; the two emitters are wired to separate CLI surfaces). Effort 1 day is realistic-to-slightly-high for a clean dedup+test update; the minimal correctness fix (ggerganov->ggml-org one-token change) is far less. Impact 'low' is accurate: only an operator diffing both paths' manifests notices, and the stale-image variant would fail at docker pull time anyway, self-surfacing. Genuine near-free correctness win on the stale image; the 'duplicate source of truth' label is fair as maintenance debt but the two generators legitimately serve different surfaces, so they are not pure duplicates.

findings.title: No edge serve runtime: CoreML/ExecuTorch/ONNX EXPORT exists but kolm serve has no WebGPU/transformers.js/ONNX-Runtime/CoreML run path
findings.technique: Edge/on-device serving (WebGPU via transformers.js or wllama, CoreML on Apple, ExecuTorch on mobile, ONNX Runtime on Jetson/DirectML)
findings.source: kolm has apps/export/coreml.py, executorch.py, onnx.py (export side); WebLLM/transformers.js WebGPU is the browser-serve standard; devices.js:635 claims 'transformers.js ... production runtime ships' but no serve wiring exists
findings.current_state: serve-autodetect decision table covers only gguf/safetensors/mlx -> llama.cpp/vllm/mlx/transformers. There is no branch for ONNX, CoreML, ExecuTorch, or WebGPU even though kolm EXPORTS to all of them (apps/export/*). devices.js catalogs Jetson/DirectML and even claims a transformers.js production runtime, but kolm serve cannot actually boot any edge runtime. The export->run loop is open on the edge.
findings.proposed_change: Add edge serve branches: (1) .onnx -> onnxruntime-genai server for Jetson/DirectML; (2) kolm serve --web that emits a static WebGPU page using transformers.js/WebLLM pointed at the exported artifact (no GPU server needed); (3) document CoreML/ExecuTorch as device-bundle outputs (not network-serve). Start with WebGPU --web since it needs no runtime install and directly demos 'run on your hardware' in a browser.
findings.rationale: kolm's promise is 'compile to run on YOUR hardware' and it already exports to every edge format, but you cannot actually kolm serve any of them, so the edge story dead-ends at the export step. WebGPU --web is the highest-leverage entry because it turns any exported model into a zero-install browser demo, reinforcing the core pitch. Lower confidence because edge serving is fragmented and per-target maturity varies.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL (not refuted). The core gap is confirmed in code. `kolm serve` autodetect (`_cmdServeAutodetect` at cli/kolm.js:13895 + `detectRuntime`/`RUNTIME_SELECTION` in src/serve-autodetect.js) only matches `.gguf|.safetensors|.mlx` (extension guard at kolm.js:13297) and only routes to llama.cpp/vllm/mlx/ollama. There is NO `kolm serve` branch for .onnx, CoreML, ExecuTorch, or WebGPU, and no `--web` flag exists anywhere in cli/kolm.js or src/. Export side exists as the finding states (apps/export/onnx.py, coreml.py, executorch.py, tensorrt.py all present), so the export->serve loop genuinely dead-ends on edge.

The finding's `already_partially_present: true` is accurate and important: an ONNX *run* path DOES exist — src/runners/onnx-runner.js + dispatchRuntime/runOnnxTarget in src/artifact-runner.js execute a .kolm whose manifest.runtime_target='onnx' via onnxruntime-node (artifact-runner.js:555). But that is single-shot `kolm run` inference, NOT an HTTP serve runtime — so "ONNX serve" is correctly characterized as absent while "ONNX run" exists. The devices.js:635 "transformers.js ... Production runtime ships" note is a device-CATALOG entry (desktop-cpu-x64 class capabilities), not serve wiring — there is no transformers.js/WebGPU/WebLLM static-page emitter anywhere in the tree. So the finding's read of devices.js is fair.

EFFORT: 6 days covers 3 sub-items and is plausible-to-optimistic. The WebGPU `--web` static-page emitter (emit HTML + CDN transformers.js/WebLLM pointing at the exported artifact) is genuinely ~1-2 days and zero-install — the highest-leverage piece. ONNX serve via onnxruntime-genai is heavier than implied: plain `optimum-cli export onnx` (what onnx.py produces) is NOT the genai format the genai server needs, so there's an unstated conversion/decode-loop dependency. CoreML/ExecuTorch are correctly scoped as device-bundle (not network-serve). Net: WebGPU subset realistic; full 6-day scope optimistic.

IMPACT: medium is fair-leaning-low. The pitch is "compile to run on YOUR hardware" and the serve surface does dead-end on edge formats, but (a) ONNX one-shot run already works, (b) llama.cpp/ollama/gguf cover the dominant local-serve case all six personas actually use, and (c) edge serving is fragmented with uneven per-target maturity (confidence 0.6 is appropriately hedged). Users would value the zero-install browser demo for the pitch, but it is an enhancement, not a blocker. Recommend prioritizing only the WebGPU `--web` slice.

Relevant files: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/serve-autodetect.js, C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/cli/kolm.js (cmdServe ~13281, _cmdServeAutodetect ~13895), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/runners/onnx-runner.js, C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/artifact-runner.js (dispatchRuntime ~534), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/devices.js:635, C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/apps/export/onnx.py

findings.title: Prefix caching is a single on/off env flag; no cross-request reuse policy, no RadixAttention-style cache-aware scheduling, no hit-rate telemetry
findings.technique: SGLang RadixAttention / vLLM APC with cache-aware scheduling + hit-rate reporting
findings.source: SGLang RadixAttention vs vLLM APC https://medium.com/byte-sized-ai/prefix-caching-sglang-vs-vllm-token-level-radix-tree-vs-block-level-hashing-b99ece9977a1; SGLang 16.2k vs vLLM 12.5k tok/s (29%)
findings.current_state: serve.py sets enable_prefix_caching from KOLM_PROMPT_CACHE (on/off/auto) and that is the whole lever. No reporting of cache hit rate in /metrics or /info, and no guidance on when it helps (it hurts when memory is tight and overlap is low). vLLM APC is the underlying mechanism; SGLang RadixAttention is 29% faster on prefix-heavy multi-turn but kolm doesn't offer SGLang as a runtime at all.
findings.proposed_change: (1) Surface prefix-cache hit rate in serve.py /metrics + /info (vLLM exposes it) so the receipt can prove the win. (2) Make 'auto' = on only for chat/RAG workloads (already half-done) and warn-off when modelFitGrid shows memory is tight. (3) Stretch: add SGLang as a serve-autodetect runtime for safetensors+cuda when the workload is prefix-heavy, since it beats vLLM 29% there. Items 1-2 are cheap; 3 is the bigger lift.
findings.rationale: Prefix caching is already enabled, so the marginal win is mostly telemetry (prove the hit rate, true to kolm's receipt ethos) plus optionally offering SGLang where it's measurably faster. Ranked low because the core capability is present; this is polish + an optional second runtime, not a missing capability.

verdict.reason:
PARTIALLY REAL, but the finding contains one material factual error and one overstatement. Verified against actual code:

TELEMETRY GAP (item 1) — REAL and not present. apps/runtime/serve.py exposes only /info (lines 492-493), /health, /ready, /v1/models — there is NO /metrics endpoint in serve.py. /info (the vLLM VLLMEngine.info(), lines 231-247) surfaces prefix_cache:bool plus a metrics block (requests, ttft_ms_avg, tok_s_avg, acceptance_rate) but NO prefix-cache hit rate. Confirmed no prefix-cache hit-rate field exists anywhere: the many cache_hit/cache_hit_rate matches in src/router.js, src/runtime-policy.js, src/usage-analytics.js are the gateway-level exact/semantic RESPONSE cache, a different layer from the serving engine's prefix KV cache. So the receipt cannot prove a prefix-cache win today. This part stands.

PREFIX CACHE = single on/off flag — ACCURATE. _resolve_prompt_cache_flag() (serve.py:88-93) reads KOLM_PROMPT_CACHE and returns True for any non-off value; that flag is wired only into vLLM enable_prefix_caching (line 151). No cross-request reuse policy, no cache-aware scheduling.

AUTO POLICY (item 2) — finding says workload-aware auto is "already half-done"; that is INACCURATE. _resolve_prompt_cache_flag just defaults on-by-default; it does NOT specialize by chat/RAG workload and does not consult modelFitGrid/memory at all. The gap is real but the finding overstates current progress.

SGLANG (item 3) — finding's claim "kolm doesn't offer SGLang as a runtime at all" is FACTUALLY FALSE. apps/runtime/backends/sglang.py is a fully-implemented SGLangBackend with explicit RadixAttention prefix-sharing documentation, registered in apps/runtime/backends/__init__.py _REGISTRY (line 46) and surfaced via kolm compute list. SGLang IS offered as a dispatch/remote runtime. What is genuinely absent is SGLang in the in-process serve-autodetect boot path: src/serve-autodetect.js KNOWN_RUNTIMES = ['llama.cpp','vllm','mlx','ollama'] and RUNTIME_SELECTION has no sglang row. So the narrow gap (autodetect-boot SGLang for safetensors+cuda prefix-heavy) is real, but the headline framing is wrong.

EFFORT: 4 days is plausible-to-optimistic. Items 1-2 (read vLLM gpu_prefix_cache_hit_rate from llm_engine stats, thread into /info, add a /metrics endpoint, add workload-aware auto + memory warn via modelFitGrid) are ~1-2 days. Item 3 (in-process SGLang boot path with spawn, health, OpenAI-compat wiring + tests) realistically 3-5 days alone since serve.py is vLLM/transformers-only in-process. Net 4-7 days; mild underestimate.

IMPACT: low is correct (the finding's own rating). Core prefix caching already works; vLLM hit-rate exposure is the only telemetry vLLM readily gives, and SGLang is already reachable via dispatch — so the marginal user value is polish, not a missing capability. Few kolm users (mostly single-GPU self-hosters per memory) run prefix-heavy multi-turn at the concurrency where the 29% SGLang delta materializes.

NOT alreadyPresent as a whole: the specific deliverables (prefix-cache hit-rate telemetry in serve.py + workload/memory-aware auto + in-process SGLang autodetect) are not implemented, even though the underlying prefix caching and a separate SGLang dispatch backend both exist. Keep but correct the SGLang framing and demote the "auto already half-done" assumption.

checks.name: Ed25519 signed receipt build + offline verify (gateway-receipt.js)
checks.evidence: buildAndSignReceipt() assembles a kolm-audit-1 receipt, validates, canonicalizes, signs via ed25519.buildSignatureBlock; verifyReceipt() strips signature, recomputes canonical, calls verifySignatureBlock (pure, no network). src/ed25519.js uses real Node crypto.sign/verify with PEM keypairs (generateKeyPair:56, sign:71, verify:80). Closes historical gap in docs/research/receipt-verification-truth-2026-05-12.md item #1 'No Ed25519/public-key receipt mode is implemented'. Tests green.

checks.name: Receipt schema validation + canonical signing order (receipt-schema.js)
checks.evidence: validateReceipt enforces 15 required fields, schema=='kolm-audit-1', receipt_id regex, ISO timestamp, route_decision enum, hash regex, numeric bounds. canonicalForSigning strips signature_ed25519 and emits keys in frozen ALL_FIELDS order. wrapper-receipt-schema #1-3 pass.

checks.name: Public receipt verify endpoint /v1/verify/:receipt_id
checks.evidence: Unauthed route validates id format, looks up observation row by receipt_id, runs grec.verifyReceipt(row.receipt), returns receipt + chain hashes + verify result. Matches verify_url stamped by gateway-receipt.js:_verifyUrl.

checks.name: Evidence DAG build/trace/descendants/revoke (evidence-dag.js)
checks.evidence: buildDag validates ids/kinds/uniqueness/edge refs/self-loops + Kahn cycle detection (195-227). trace/descendants/revoke return frozen results. Wired router.js:79,8336,8359. r5-evidence-dag tests pass.

checks.name: Assurance case export v1 + v2 (assurance-case.js)
checks.evidence: buildAssuranceCase folds claims + 8 controls across SOC2/HIPAA/EU-AI-Act/ISO-27001, never throws, downgrades to package-gated/external-proof-needed with limitations. generateAssuranceCase v2 uses CLAIM_STATUSES_V2. Wired router.js:8622,8664. r6-assurance-case tests pass incl 'NEVER invents evidence_ids'.

checks.name: Artifact lifecycle state machine (artifact-lifecycle.js)
checks.evidence: transition() validates VALID_TRANSITIONS, fails closed on missing actor, requires revoke reason + supersede successor_id, atomic persist. canPull() false only for revoked. Wired router.js:8272,8299. r2-artifact-lifecycle tests pass.

checks.name: Append-only HMAC-chained audit log (audit.js)
checks.evidence: appendAudit chains event_hash=HMAC(secret,prev||canonical) inside withTransaction (W258-BE-5 race fix:119). verifyAuditChain reports breaks. Wired router.js:13665,13531,22449. wave448 + wave770 tests pass.

checks.name: Provenance credential schema (provenance.js)
checks.evidence: buildArtifactCredential/buildOutputCredential/verifyCredential sign HMAC-SHA256 over canonicalJson. Explicit it is NOT real C2PA and says 'tamper-evident' not 'tamper-proof' (1-21) -- honest scoping. Output references parent for offline chain walk.

checks.name: Cost displacement reporting (cost-displacement.js)
checks.evidence: computeDisplacement re-prices local receipts vs PROVIDER_RATE_CARD counterfactual, never invents prices (flags partial:N_unrepriced:362), reports payback. calculateSavings v2 adds by_provider. Wired router.js:25739. r8-cost-displacement tests pass.

checks.name: Drift detector pure-compute signals (drift-detector.js)
checks.evidence: computeDriftSignals computes 3 signals with self-calibrating SPC 2-sigma/3-sigma ladder, refuses fabrication on thin baselines. klDivergence/scoreSeverity/DriftDetector provide KL path. Wired router.js:23480. r7-drift-detector tests pass (unit-level).

checks.name: route_decision enum vs frontier_fallback consumer mismatch
checks.evidence: drift-detector._fallbackRate (277: route_decision==='frontier_fallback') and cost-displacement (297) branch on 'frontier_fallback', but the builder coerces route_decision to only local|frontier (gateway-receipt.js:110) and ROUTE_DECISIONS=['local','frontier']. Observation rows store receipt.route_decision (router.js:6406) so on real data fallback_rate_delta is structurally ~0; real signal is in separate fallback_reason field. r7 tests pass only because fixtures inject the value directly (tests/r7:110,189). End-to-end wiring gap unit tests miss.

checks.name: fallback_reason enum drift (schema vs gateway-router values)
checks.evidence: FALLBACK_REASONS allows low_confidence/class_mismatch/upstream_*/poison_detected/null but gateway-router.js returns 'empty_chain' (239) and 'low_confidence_prior' (317). If either reaches receiptInputs.fallback_reason (router.js:6354) validateReceipt rejects it and buildAndSignReceipt throws receipt_invalid, aborting the gateway call. Latent schema-drift risk.

checks.name: Lifecycle side-effect hooks are in-memory stubs
checks.evidence: blockPulls/alertDeployments/linkSuccessor only append to in-memory _sideEffectLog. Comments explicit: 'Production wiring is expected to replace this' (258), 'production wiring routes to the alert ladder' (276). Pull-blocking via canPull()==false+410 works, but alert/successor side effects not wired to a real subscriber; log resets per process.

checks.name: Two divergent lifecycle transition tables (TRANSITIONS vs VALID_TRANSITIONS)
checks.evidence: TRANSITIONS (v2 class) allows monitored->drift_detected->re_evaluated, no 'undeployed'/signed->archived. VALID_TRANSITIONS (v1 fn) includes undeployed + signed->archived but not drift_detected/re_evaluated. drift_detected/re_evaluated absent from LIFECYCLE_STATES (59). Same artifact gets different legal moves per path; real tech debt.

checks.name: Cross-tenant field exposure on public /v1/verify/:receipt_id
checks.evidence: Unauthed route does all('observations') across ALL tenants and returns full receipt by id. Receipt carries only hashes (no raw text), but namespace_id, provider, model, cost_usd, token counts are returned to any anonymous caller holding a receipt id. Documented public-verification design, but exposing cost/token/namespace anonymously warrants an explicit decision.

gaps: INTEGRATION: drift-detector fallback_rate_delta and cost-displacement frontier_fallback branch read a route_decision value ('frontier_fallback') the receipt builder/observation writer never emits (only local|frontier). On production data the headline drift fallback signal is structurally ~0; the real fallback signal is in the separate fallback_reason field. Unit tests pass only because fixtures inject the value directly.

SCHEMA DRIFT: gateway-router.js emits fallback_reason values 'empty_chain' and 'low_confidence_prior' absent from FALLBACK_REASONS; if they reach buildAndSignReceipt they fail validation and abort the call.
STUB: artifact-lifecycle blockPulls/alertDeployments/linkSuccessor are in-memory no-op logs not wired to a durable subscriber/alert ladder; log resets per process.
TECH DEBT: two lifecycle transition tables (TRANSITIONS vs VALID_TRANSITIONS) with different legal moves; drift_detected/re_evaluated states are not in LIFECYCLE_STATES.
DESIGN DECISION NEEDED: public /v1/verify/:receipt_id returns cost_usd, token counts, namespace_id, provider, model to unauthenticated callers via a global all('observations') scan.
DOC INCONSISTENCY (minor): receipt-schema.js header says hashes are 'kept short (16 hex)' but _sha256Short produces 32 hex; regex allows 16-64 so harmless, but the comment is wrong.
audit.summary: The Govern / Receipts & Compliance surface is substantially implemented and well-tested at the unit level. All 9 audited modules (gateway-receipt, receipt-schema, evidence-dag, assurance-case, drift-detector, cost-displacement, artifact-lifecycle, provenance, audit) export their headline symbols, are wired into router.js endpoints (/v1/verify, /v1/receipts/*, /v1/evidence/*, /v1/assurance/*, /v1/artifacts/:id/lifecycle, /v1/audit/*, /v1/savings/displacement, /v1/drift/status), and their dedicated suites pass (r2/r5/r6/r7/r8 + wrapper-r1-r8 + wrapper-receipt-schema + wave888i-receipt-export + wave770/wave448 = all green; 115 tests across the runs I executed). Real cryptography is present: Ed25519 public-key receipt signing/verification (closing the historical 2026-05-12 gap), HMAC-chained append-only audit log with tamper-evident verification, and an honestly-scoped provenance credential schema that explicitly avoids C2PA conformance claims. The modules are disciplined about never fabricating evidence (assurance-case downgrades with limitations; drift refuses thin baselines; cost-displacement flags unrepriced rows). The most material finding is an end-to-end wiring gap: both the drift detector's fallback-rate signal and cost-displacement's counterfactual branch on a route_decision value 'frontier_fallback' the receipt builder can never emit (it coerces to local|frontier), so on production observation rows that signal is inert -- the unit tests miss this because they inject the value directly. A related latent schema-drift risk is gateway-router emitting fallback_reason values outside the receipt FALLBACK_REASONS enum. Secondary items: lifecycle side-effect hooks are in-memory stubs, two divergent lifecycle transition tables, and the public verify endpoint exposing cost/token/namespace fields to anonymous callers. No fabricated functionality found; gaps are integration/wiring and tech-debt, not missing features. Full result written to C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/audit/w920/govern.audit.json.

findings.title: frontier_fallback route_decision consumers are dead code on real receipts
findings.technique: Single source of truth for the fallback signal
findings.source: Internal audit confirmed: src/drift-detector.js:277, src/cost-displacement.js:297 branch on route_decision==='frontier_fallback'; builder coerces to local|frontier only (gateway-receipt.js:110); rows store receipt.route_decision (router.js:6406).
findings.current_state: Drift fallback_rate_delta and cost-displacement fallback bucketing key off route_decision==='frontier_fallback', a value the receipt builder can never produce (ROUTE_DECISIONS=['local','frontier']). On real data fallback_rate is structurally ~0 and the cost-displacement fallback bucket is always empty; the true signal lives in the separate fallback_reason field. r7/r8 tests pass only because fixtures inject route_decision='frontier_fallback' directly.
findings.proposed_change: Pick one representation. Recommended: keep route_decision two-valued and rewrite _fallbackRate() and cost-displacement to count fallbacks as route_decision==='frontier' AND fallback_reason!=null (rows already persist fallback_reason at router.js:6407). Update r7/r8 fixtures to the real persisted shape so the test exercises the production path.
findings.rationale: The drift detector's headline 'traffic shifted away from the artifact' signal and the cost report's fallback accounting are both silently zeroed in production. This is a correctness hole in the two most operator-facing govern outputs, and the fix is a few lines because fallback_reason is already on the row.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED REAL — not refuted. Verified every claim against the actual code.

MECHANISM (all confirmed):
- src/receipt-schema.js:83: `ROUTE_DECISIONS = Object.freeze(['local','frontier'])`. validateReceipt (line 147-148) rejects any other value.
- src/gateway-receipt.js:110: `r.route_decision = opts.route_decision === 'local' ? 'local' : 'frontier'` — hard coercion. The signed receipt can NEVER carry 'frontier_fallback'.
- src/router.js:6354: a real local->frontier fallback sets `fallback_reason` (non-null) when attempted.length > 1; line 6358 resolves route_decision to 'frontier'. Persisted at router.js:6406 (route_decision) + 6407 (fallback_reason) as SEPARATE fields on the observations row.
- src/runtime-policy.js:467 `reason:'frontier_fallback'` is a decision REASON string, not a route_decision — it does not rescue the consumers.

DEAD CODE (confirmed):
- src/drift-detector.js:277 `_fallbackRate` counts only `route_decision==='frontier_fallback'`. On real observation rows this is always 0, so fallback_rate is structurally 0 and fallback_rate_delta is structurally 0/null. This is a HEADLINE signal: drift-detector.js:21 lists it as signal #1 and SEVERITY_THRESHOLDS.high_fallback_rate_delta=0.05 (line 640) escalates drift to 'high' — so the escalation is effectively disabled in production.
- src/cost-displacement.js:297 the `frontier_fallback` branch never fires on real data; a real fallback (route_decision='frontier') instead lands in the 'frontier' branch (line 293) and is counted as frontierCount. So frontier_fallback_count (line 384) is always 0 in the operator-facing cost report.

WHY TESTS PASS (confirmed): r7/r8 fixtures build raw row objects via local helpers (r8 rec() at tests/r8-cost-displacement.test.js:37-50; r7 receipt() at :34) that bypass buildAndSignReceipt and set route_decision:'frontier_fallback' directly (r7 lines 110/123/189/238/364; r8 line 98 with assert frontier_fallback_count==1 at :124). They exercise a shape production cannot emit.

ALREADY IMPLEMENTED? No. This is a latent correctness bug, not a shipped/intentional feature. The only occurrence of 'frontier_fallback' as a route_decision value in src/ is a comment (cost-displacement.js:49).

FIX FEASIBILITY: The proposed remedy is sound and the data dependency holds — drift-detector reads store.findByTenant('observations') (drift-detector.js:223), the same table the router writes fallback_reason to (router.js:6407). So rewriting the predicate to `route_decision==='frontier' && fallback_reason!=null` works with persisted data. Note: cost-displacement currently differentiates fallback (full frontier price, no displacement) from plain frontier (also no displacement) — both already add actual to baseline, so the savings math is unaffected; only the fallback BUCKET COUNT is wrong. So cost-displacement's savings number is correct; only its reporting breakdown is misattributed.

EFFORT: 1.5 days is slightly generous (core change is ~10 lines across two predicates) but reasonable once you re-run/repair r7+r8 suites including the SPC ladder paths (drift-detector.js:346/517-518) and the snapshot export path (lines 785-789). Keep at 1.5.

IMPACT: high (not critical). It silently zeros two operator-facing govern signals (the drift 'traffic shifted away from artifact' escalation and the cost-report fallback bucket). It is a silent-zeroing/mis-attribution bug, not a crash or data-corruption issue, and cost SAVINGS totals remain correct — so high, not critical. Users running Govern/Receipts who rely on drift severity to trigger re-distill would never see fallback-driven escalation; they would care.

findings.title: Per-call gateway receipts are Ed25519-signed but not transparency-log anchored
findings.technique: Sigstore/Rekor transparency-log inclusion for live receipts (not just .kolm artifacts)
findings.source: Sigstore model-transparency v1.0 (Apr 2025) https://blog.sigstore.dev/model-transparency-v1.0/ ; Rekor v2 GA https://blog.sigstore.dev/rekor-v2-ga/ ; internal: gateway-receipt.js has zero sigstore references.
findings.current_state: src/sigstore.js produces cosign-compatible bundles with Rekor submission + RFC 6962 inclusion-proof verification, but is wired only into .kolm artifact builds (src/artifact.js). The per-call gateway receipt (gateway-receipt.js -> /v1/verify/:id) gets Ed25519 only, signed with a self-generated per-machine key (~/.kolm/signing-key.pem). An outside party cannot prove a given receipt existed at a given time or that the key wasn't minted after the fact.
findings.proposed_change: Add optional, async, off-hot-path Rekor anchoring for receipts: batch-submit receipt canonical digests to a configurable/self-hostable Rekor v2 instance behind KOLM_SIGSTORE_REKOR_URL, store {logIndex, inclusionProof, integratedTime} on the observation row, surface it from /v1/verify/:id. Default stays dry-run/offline so it never blocks a call.
findings.rationale: kolm's pitch is 'the receipt is the proof.' Today the proof is only as good as the signer's key custody. Transparency-log anchoring is what Sigstore shipped for ML models in 2025 and is the difference between tamper-evident and externally-verifiable-without-trusting-kolm. The infra already exists; it just isn't on the live path.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL (could not refute). The finding's technical claims all check out against the actual code:

1. gateway-receipt.js has ZERO sigstore/rekor references (grep confirmed). buildAndSignReceipt (src/gateway-receipt.js:105) signs with Ed25519 only via ed25519.buildSignatureBlock. The live gateway dispatch path (src/router.js:6384) calls buildAndSignReceipt, persists the observation (router.js:6400), and never touches any sigstore/rekor function.

2. The signer defaults to a self-generated per-machine key at ~/.kolm/signing-key.pem (src/ed25519.js:191, loadOrCreateDefaultSigner Path 3 generates+persists). Production can override via KOLM_ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY, but the default live path is exactly as the finding describes.

3. /v1/verify/:receipt_id (src/router.js:21843) returns the receipt + Ed25519 verifyReceipt result + chain_prev_hash/chain_hash — but those chain fields are read as `row.chain_hash || null` and are NOT written on the gateway observation insert, so they are null for gateway receipts. NO rekor/transparency-log data is surfaced.

4. The 'infra already exists' claim is accurate: src/sigstore.js fully implements submitToRekor (POST hashedrekord), fetchRekorEntryByLogIndex, attestWithRekor, attestArtifactWithRekor, and real RFC 6962 inclusion-proof verification (verifyRekorInclusionProof, lines 400-477), gated on KOLM_SIGSTORE_REKOR_URL. It is wired ONLY into .kolm artifact builds (src/artifact.js), the /v1/sigstore/* routes (router.js:3205+), and the binder.js require_rekor policy gate (binder.js:1341) — all of which operate on artifact receipt.json carrying signature_sigstore, never on per-call gateway observations. The govern audit notes (audit/w920/govern.audit.json) independently confirm gateway receipts are Ed25519-only and reference docs/research/receipt-verification-truth-2026-05-12.md, whose 'anchors: 0 always empty' gap maps directly to this finding.

alreadyPresent=false: the receipt-anchoring path the finding proposes does not exist; only the artifact-anchoring path does (correctly captured by the finding's already_partially_present:true).

EFFORT — underestimated. 4 days is optimistic. submitToRekor/attestWithRekor exist, but: (a) the observation row has no rekor field and is never re-written after insert (router.js:6400-6421), so you need an async post-write update path that doesn't exist for observations (the only async anchoring today is per-artifact attestArtifactWithRekor, which rewrites a zip, not a DB row); (b) the proposal says 'batch-submit receipt canonical digests' — true batching into one Merkle leaf needs an aggregation tree, otherwise it degrades to one Rekor entry per call (higher cost/latency); (c) postgres-store path, /v1/verify surfacing, and tests. Realistic 5-8 days.

IMPACT — overblown. 'high' is too strong for kolm's actual users. Default stays dry-run/offline so almost no self-hosted user runs a Rekor instance; Ed25519 plus the intended hash-chain already provide meaningful tamper-evidence; and the same govern audit lists higher-severity gaps (route_decision 'frontier_fallback' never emitted so drift fallback signal is structurally ~0; fallback_reason enum drift that can abort gateway calls; cross-tenant field exposure on the public unauthed verify endpoint). This is a genuine trust upgrade well-aligned with the 'receipt is the proof' pitch, but medium impact is more accurate.

Files confirmed: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/gateway-receipt.js, src/sigstore.js, src/ed25519.js, src/router.js (6384, 21843, 3205-3318), src/binder.js (1341), audit/w920/govern.audit.json.

findings.title: Move signing from a per-machine on-disk key to KMS/HSM-backed signing
findings.technique: External key custody (AWS KMS / GCP Cloud KMS-HSM / Vault transit) with auditable rotation
findings.source: AWS KMS key rotation https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/rotate-keys.html ; HashiCorp Vault key-management engine; ISO 27001 A.12 / PCI DSS 10.x rotation-logging (2025 KMS guidance).
findings.current_state: ed25519.js loadOrCreateDefaultSigner generates and persists a private key at ~/.kolm/signing-key.pem (0600), or reads raw PEM from KOLM_ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY. No external-custody signer, no rotation ceremony, no separation between signing key and host process. A host compromise discloses the private key and lets an attacker forge any 'verified' receipt.
findings.proposed_change: Add a signer interface in ed25519.js with pluggable backends: (1) PEM (default), (2) KMS sign (key never leaves HSM; pass digest get signature), (3) Vault transit. Stamp signing_key_id with the KMS key version so rotation is visible in receipts, and emit a KEY_ROTATED audit event (op already exists in audit.js:42) on every version bump. Keep embedded public-key bytes so offline verify is unchanged.
findings.rationale: Enterprise/BAA buyers will ask where the signing key lives and how it rotates. 'A 0600 file on the app host' fails most third-line reviews. KMS-backed signing with logged rotation is the standard answer and directly strengthens the SOC2 CC6.1 / ISO A.5 claims the assurance case already makes.

verdict.reason:
NOT REFUTED — the core proposal (true external-custody signing where the private key never leaves the HSM) is genuinely absent. Verified against actual code:

CURRENT STATE IS ACCURATE. src/ed25519.js loadOrCreateDefaultSigner (lines 174-233) does exactly what the finding says: prefers KOLM_ED25519_PRIVATE_KEY env / _PATH file, else generates and persists ~/.kolm/signing-key.pem at mode 0600. sign() (line 71) is PEM-only and synchronous. gateway-receipt.js buildAndSignReceipt (line 105) calls this synchronously and always signs with a local private key. A host compromise discloses the key. Confirmed.

WHAT ALREADY EXISTS (partial scaffolding, more than the finding's already_partially_present:false admits): src/keys.js (Wave 193) ships a rotation lifecycle with a KMS_TARGETS enum [aws-kms, gcp-kms, azure-keyvault, vault, local], rotateKey/listKeys/exportKmsIntent, NIST SP 800-57 overlap window, per-target import-format + native-api hints, CLI verbs `kolm keys list|rotate|fingerprint|export` (cli/kolm.js:15414+). gateway-receipt.js:131 already stamps signing_key_id (env KOLM_SIGNING_KEY_ID or signer fingerprint). The public key is already embedded for offline verify. audit.js:42 KEY_ROTATED op exists and router.js emits it in 5 places.

THE GAP THE FINDING CORRECTLY TARGETS IS REAL. src/keys.js is explicitly NOT KMS-backed signing — its own header (lines 22-27) states it "does not call any KMS API"; line 157 always does generateKeyPair() LOCALLY and returns private_key_pem for a customer 'wrap/import intent' (api_status: awaiting_operator_hook). That is key IMPORT (kolm holds the plaintext key) not HSM SIGNING (digest in, signature out, key never exposed). No kmsSign/signDigest exists anywhere in src/. The repo's own blueprint (kolm-ai-100-percent-codebase-completion-blueprint line 6841, "KMS truth") flags this: copy must not say "KMS integrated" when kolm only emits wrap intent. So the proposed pluggable signer with a real HSM-sign backend is genuinely missing.

EFFORT 5d is optimistic; adjusted to 8. Reasons: sign()/buildAndSignReceipt are synchronous and PEM-coupled, so a pluggable async signer ripples through every receipt caller; per-backend SDK + IAM/credential wiring; key-version→signing_key_id mapping and a rotation ceremony tied to KMS versions; mocked/live KMS tests. One backend (GCP Cloud KMS or Vault transit) in ~5-8d is plausible; all three robustly is closer to 10-12d.

UNSTATED PREREQUISITE the finding glosses: AWS KMS does NOT natively sign Ed25519 (only ECDSA/RSA); GCP Cloud KMS and Vault transit do support Ed25519. So an "AWS KMS Ed25519" backend either requires changing the algorithm per-backend (which would change embedded public-key bytes and complicate the "offline verify unchanged" claim) or dropping AWS from the Ed25519 path. The proposal understates this.

IMPACT high is accurate, not overblown: enterprise/BAA third-line reviews do ask 'where does the signing key live / how does it rotate', a 0600 file on the app host is a weak answer, and the repo already advances SOC2 CC6.1 / ISO assurance claims this would directly back. Real users in the Govern/Compliance surface (the audience for receipts) would care.

Relevant files: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/ed25519.js (sign line 71, loadOrCreateDefaultSigner 174-233), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/keys.js (header 22-27, generateKeyPair line 157, rotateKey 144), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/gateway-receipt.js:131, C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/audit.js:42, C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/cli/kolm.js:15414-15528, C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/docs/research/kolm-ai-100-percent-codebase-completion-blueprint-2026-05-25.md:6841.

findings.title: Adopt RFC 8785 (JCS) canonicalization for the signed payload
findings.technique: Standardized JSON Canonicalization Scheme instead of bespoke fixed-order JSON.stringify
findings.source: RFC 8785 JSON Canonicalization Scheme https://www.rfc-editor.org/info/rfc8785/
findings.current_state: receipt-schema.canonicalForSigning emits keys in frozen ALL_FIELDS order via JSON.stringify with no spaces. Deterministic kolm-vs-kolm, but NOT RFC 8785: number serialization is left to V8 (cost_usd like 0.0001 or a future 1e-7 could serialize inconsistently across runtimes), and non-ASCII bytes in namespace_id/redaction strings are not JCS-normalized. A non-Node verifier (the C/Rust SDKs, a buyer's Go tool) can recompute a different byte string and fail an otherwise-valid signature.
findings.proposed_change: Replace canonicalForSigning's serializer with an RFC 8785 JCS implementation (lexicographic sort + ECMAScript number serialization + I-JSON). Pin cost_usd to a fixed decimal string or integer micro-USD to sidestep float serialization. Add a cross-language conformance fixture (sign in Node, verify in the Rust SDK) to the release-verify gate.
findings.rationale: kolm ships C and Rust SDKs and tells buyers they can verify offline. The moment a verifier in another language disagrees on canonical bytes, offline verify breaks for that buyer. JCS is the boring standard fix and makes the receipt portable rather than Node-canonical-only.

verdict.reason: The code-level claim is accurate: src/receipt-schema.js:196-205 canonicalForSigning uses JSON.stringify with frozen ALL_FIELDS order and no RFC 8785 normalization, and cost_usd is an unpinned float (src/gateway-receipt.js:128). RFC 8785/JCS is NOT implemented anywhere. BUT the finding's core risk -- a non-Node verifier recomputing different canonical bytes and failing an otherwise-valid signature -- is REFUTED by the actual SDK code. No non-Node verifier recomputes the gateway kolm-audit-1 canonical bytes at all: the Rust SDK verify() (sdk/rust/src/lib.rs:181-183) and C SDK kolm_verify() (sdk/c/kolm.h:274-279) both just GET /v1/verify/{cid} (server-side Node verification), and the Python SDK verify() (sdk/python/kolm/client.py:275-284) shells out to the Node CLI. Offline Ed25519 recompute of canonicalForSigning runs ONLY in Node (gateway-receipt.js verifyReceipt -> ed25519.js verifySignatureBlock), so there is no second implementation to disagree with it. The finding also conflates two systems and lists wrong files: the C/Rust/Python *format* verifiers (kolm-format.h, packages/runtime-rs/src/canonical.rs+verify.rs, sdk/python/kolm/format.py) verify the .kolm ARTIFACT chain (HMAC+Ed25519 over canonicalJson), a different schema from the gateway kolm-audit-1 receipt the Govern surface uses; the listed sdk/rust/src/lib.rs is an HTTP client with no canonicalizer (the real Rust canonicalizer is packages/runtime-rs/src/canonical.rs, which is itself deliberately bespoke-bit-matched-to-Node, not JCS, per its own header comment lines 1-6). Effort is underestimated: pinning cost_usd to fixed decimal changes signed bytes, invalidating all historical receipts and forcing a kolm-audit-2 version bump with dual-verify, plus building an actual cross-language gateway-receipt verifier for the conformance fixture (none exists) -- ~5-8 days, call it 6. Impact downgraded medium->low: it is forward-looking portability hardening (correct in principle, the boring-standard fix) but the described offline-verify-breakage cannot occur today since no independent gateway-receipt verifier exists. Users would only care once someone writes a non-Node verifier for the audit receipt, which the SDKs intentionally do not do.

findings.title: Emit in-toto v1 Statements / SLSA provenance for .kolm artifacts
findings.technique: in-toto Attestation Framework v1 + SLSA Provenance predicate for ML models
findings.source: in-toto attestation https://github.com/in-toto/attestation ; SLSA Provenance v1.0 https://slsa.dev/spec/v1.0/provenance ; SLSA-for-ML 2025 https://debugg.ai/resources/slsa-for-ml-2025-signed-datasets-reproducible-training-attested-inference
findings.current_state: Artifacts carry a bespoke kolm credential (provenance.js, kolm-credential/0.1) + a cosign sigstore bundle. There is no in-toto Statement (_type: https://in-toto.io/Statement/v1) with subject digests and a SLSA Provenance predicate (builder id, buildType, materials). grep for in-toto/slsa/predicate in artifact.js returns nothing.
findings.proposed_change: Add an in-toto v1 Statement wrapper around the existing signed payload: subject=[{name,digest:{sha256}}], predicateType=SLSA provenance v1, predicate.materials={base_model, teacher, dataset fingerprints from distill-provenance.js}, predicate.builder.id=kolm compile. Sign the DSSE envelope with the same Ed25519 key. Lets cosign verify-attestation / kyverno / conftest consume kolm artifacts natively.
findings.rationale: The 2025 ML supply-chain consensus (Sigstore model-signing, SLSA-for-ML) is in-toto/SLSA, not bespoke credential schemas. kolm already computes everything the predicate needs (teacher, dataset, k_score). Wrapping it in the standard envelope means a buyer's existing CI policy gate verifies a kolm artifact with zero kolm-specific code.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, not already present. I confirmed against the actual code at C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack.

What exists today: src/provenance.js emits a bespoke `kolm-credential/0.1` schema (HMAC-signed, claim_generator/assertions/ingredients shape borrowed loosely from C2PA — the file comment admits it is "NOT a real C2PA assertion library"). src/sigstore.js produces a cosign-compatible bundle, but it is a `messageSignature`/`hashedrekord` (Rekor v0.0.1), NOT a DSSE attestation. src/distill-provenance.js + src/artifact-lineage.js already compute everything a SLSA predicate needs (teacher vendor/model/version, student_base repo/revision, training_corpus_hash). So the finding's premise is correct: the materials exist but are wrapped in a non-standard envelope.

What is genuinely absent (verified via Grep across src/ and cli/): zero hits for `in-toto.io/Statement`, `predicateType`, `_type: Statement/v1`, DSSE `payloadType`, or `application/vnd.in-toto`. artifact.js (2416 lines) has none of it. The finding's specific claim "grep for in-toto/slsa/predicate in artifact.js returns nothing" is accurate.

This is NOT a 'shipped thing flagged as missing' false positive. The opposite: the repo openly treats this as a deliberate future target. public/slsa.html says "SLSA Level 3 attestation is still a target until..." and references "in-toto SLSA v1.0 provenance emitted by the release workflow" as not-yet-done. src/compliance-certification-packet.js lists `external_blocker: 'signed_release_provenance_missing'` and an action item to "Attach signed release provenance... after public release."

Effort: 4 days is underestimated -> adjusted to 7. The sigstore path is a hashedrekord, not DSSE, so this is not a thin wrapper: you must build the in-toto Statement + SLSA Provenance v1.0 predicate, switch to DSSE PAE pre-auth encoding, add a DSSE/in-toto verifier, thread distill-provenance materials through artifact.js's buildPayload, and avoid regressing the ~15-check existing verifier surface that touches artifact-runner.js, binder.js, pipeline-ship.js, router.js, and cli/kolm.js. ed25519.js already exposes sign/verify so reusing the key is feasible.

Impact: medium is accurate. Real value for the Govern/Receipts surface — enterprise buyers can run `cosign verify-attestation`/kyverno/conftest against a .kolm with zero kolm-specific code, which is the 2025 ML supply-chain consensus (Sigstore model-signing, SLSA-for-ML). But it is additive interop, not a capability gap: the existing offline-verifiable credential + Rekor inclusion-proof verification already covers the underlying integrity/provenance need. Unstated prerequisite worth flagging: a policy gate that meaningfully trusts predicate.builder.id wants a hardened, identity-bound release workflow (Fulcio/OIDC) — slsa.html itself names that as the actual blocker. This finding scopes only the envelope (the achievable half); a buyer auditing builder identity would still find SLSA L3 unmet.

findings.title: Generate OSCAL assessment-results from the assurance case for machine-readable compliance
findings.technique: NIST OSCAL (assessment-results / SSP / component-definition) export
findings.source: NIST OSCAL https://pages.nist.gov/OSCAL/ ; CSWP 53 (Dec 2025) https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/cswp/53/charting-the-course-for-nist-oscal/ipd
findings.current_state: assurance-case.js folds claims + controls across SOC2/HIPAA/EU-AI-Act/ISO-27001 and renders JSON + PDF; GRC connectors shape data for OneTrust/ServiceNow/IBM OpenPages. There is no OSCAL output, the NIST-standard machine-readable format federal/financial buyers and GRC tooling increasingly ingest to automate control assessment.
findings.proposed_change: Add an OSCAL exporter mapping the existing assurance-case claims+controls into an OSCAL assessment-results document (observations carrying receipt_id/evidence_id as machine evidence) plus a component-definition mapping kolm features to control ids. Reuse V2_DEFAULT_CONTROL_ROWS. Turns 'audit takes months' into the OSCAL-automatable path NIST is standardizing.
findings.rationale: kolm already has the claims+controls+evidence graph; OSCAL is just the standard serialization buyers' GRC stacks consume. High-leverage differentiator vs Credo AI/Arthur because kolm can populate OSCAL observations with cryptographically verifiable receipt evidence, not self-attestation.

verdict.reason:
REAL, not already present. The OSCAL grep hits in src/ and cli/kolm.js are false positives matching "autOSCALing" (HorizontalPodAutoscaler / autoscaling/v2), not NIST OSCAL. Confirmed by reading the actual code:

- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/assurance-case.js emits only kolm's proprietary specs (kolm-assurance-case-1 / kolm-assurance-case-2). It builds claims + controls + evidence_ids and exposes V2_DEFAULT_CONTROL_ROWS, but no OSCAL serialization exists.
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/router.js routes /v1/assurance/artifact/:id (line 8622) and /v1/assurance/workspace/:id (line 8664) hard-validate format to exactly ['json','pdf'] and reject anything else (bad_format). No oscal format branch.
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/reg-grc-connectors.js shapes payloads for OneTrust/ServiceNow/IBM OpenPages only — none is OSCAL.
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/compliance-certification-packet.js lists a 'fedramp-boundary' control row but produces HTML/auditor-report evidence packets, not OSCAL documents.

So already_partially_present is true ONLY for the upstream claims+controls+evidence graph; the OSCAL exporter itself is absent. alreadyPresent (the OSCAL feature) = false.

Effort: finding says 4 days; raised to ~6. The building blocks (pure functions, frozen control rows, validate + route plumbing) are favorable, but OSCAL assessment-results is a strict deeply-nested schema (UUIDs everywhere, metadata/roles/parties, import-ap, results[].observations[], findings, back-matter resources) and the finding ALSO proposes a component-definition doc, doubling the surface. Mapping kolm's 4-value taxonomy (supported/partial/unsupported/unknown) into OSCAL objective-status/finding semantics plus schema-validation tests realistically lands 5-7 days. Consistent with this repo's chronic underestimation pattern.

Impact medium is accurate, slightly generous. OSCAL matters to federal/FedRAMP and regulated-finance buyers + the GRC tooling that ingests it — a narrow but high-value segment (only enterprise persona D, government/regulated subset). Irrelevant to personas A/B/C/E/F. The receipt-backed-evidence-vs-self-attestation differentiator is genuinely strong, so medium (not high) is right.

Unstated dependencies/risks the finding glosses over: (1) without bundling/validating against the real NIST OSCAL JSON schema, output won't actually be ingestible — that undermines the whole value prop. (2) OSCAL assessment-results normally references an assessment-plan and SSP/component-definition; emitting it alone is only partly useful (finding partly anticipates this). (3) Crucially, V2_DEFAULT_CONTROL_ROWS uses SOC2/ISO/HIPAA IDs (CC6.1, A.5.1, 164.312...), NOT NIST 800-53 control IDs — FedRAMP-grade OSCAL tooling assesses against 800-53, so reusing the rows verbatim yields OSCAL that validates structurally but doesn't map to the catalog GRC stacks score against. That control-mapping gap is real work the 4-day estimate ignores.

findings.title: Add PSI / MMD / ADWIN standard drift statistics alongside the bespoke SPC detector
findings.technique: Population Stability Index, Maximum Mean Discrepancy, ADWIN adaptive windowing
findings.source: Production drift survey 2025 https://deepchecks.com/how-to-detect-concept-drift-with-machine-learning-monitoring/ ; ADWIN-U 2025 https://www.researchgate.net/publication/393510925
findings.current_state: drift-detector.js computes 3 signals (fallback_rate_delta, hash-bucket pseudo-embedding cosine distance, volume_ratio) with a self-calibrating 2-sigma/3-sigma SPC ladder + a KL path. PSI (de-facto industry standard reviewers expect), MMD (standard high-dimensional kernel two-sample test for embedding/text drift), and ADWIN (streaming adaptive-window detector) are absent. The 256-dim sha256-bucket pseudo-embedding is a weak distribution proxy vs MMD over real embeddings.
findings.proposed_change: Add PSI as a first-class signal (PSI>0.2 warn, >0.25 alert, the convention reviewers know), add an MMD kernel two-sample option when real embeddings are available (drift-detect.js already takes caller-supplied embeddings), and offer ADWIN for the streaming volume/fallback series instead of fixed lookback windows. Keep SPC as the default for thin baselines.
findings.rationale: When a model-risk reviewer (SR 11-7 shop, financial buyer) asks what drift test you run, 'PSI and MMD' is the expected answer; 'a sha256-bucket cosine with a 2-sigma ladder' invites a fight. kolm already has the pure-compute harness and self-calibration plumbing, so adding the named-standard detectors is mostly statistics, not infra.

verdict.reason:
NOT REFUTED — finding stands, and is if anything understated. Word-boundary grep across all of src/ confirms PSI, MMD, and ADWIN are entirely absent from the implementation. The runtime drift path (computeDriftSignals in src/drift-detector.js, wired into /v1/drift at src/router.js:23480) computes exactly what the finding describes: fallback_rate_delta + a 256-dim sha256-bucket pseudo-embedding cosine distribution_distance + volume_ratio, classified by a self-calibrating 2sigma/3sigma SPC ladder. The only other statistic present is histogram KL (src/drift-detect.js:220 and src/drift-detector.js:670) — not PSI, not MMD, not ADWIN.

Critically for the Govern/Compliance surface: the finding undersells the problem. The public docs page public/docs/govern/drift.html:85 already advertises "Population stability index over embedding centroids" as kolm's input-distribution statistic, and line 109 documents an `input_psi_max = 0.25` spec.toml knob — yet a strict grep shows no code ever computes PSI or parses input_psi_max anywhere in src/. So this is a live doc-vs-code gap on the exact surface a model-risk (SR 11-7) reviewer would inspect, not merely a missing nice-to-have. The 0.25 alert convention the finding cites is the same one the docs already claim.

Effort (4 days): roughly realistic, slightly optimistic at the high end. PSI is cheap (~0.5d) because the histogram-binning + KL plumbing in drift-detect.js is reusable. ADWIN is the heaviest piece (needs persisted per-namespace streaming window state with a confidence-bound shrink test — a new stateful concern vs today's stateless windowed reads) and pushes toward 4-6 days if all three land with tests + threshold calibration + SPC-ladder integration + docs reconciliation.

Impact (medium): accurate. Value concentrates in the enterprise/financial compliance segment where "what drift test do you run? PSI/MMD" is expected vocabulary; most solo/startup distill users won't ask. Medium is right.

Unstated dependencies: (1) MMD is gated on REAL embeddings — drift-detect.js accepts caller-supplied embeddings but nothing in the lake-read path produces them (the lake path uses the sha256 pseudo-embedding), so MMD would be dormant until an actual embedder is wired in. (2) Landing PSI should reconcile the already-documented-but-unparsed input_psi_max knob. (3) ADWIN needs new persisted window state. Test harness exists (tests/r7-drift-detector.test.js, tests/wave813-drift-detection.test.js), so the additions are testable.

findings.title: Fix the RFC 6962 Merkle inclusion-proof verifier (incorrect walk + broken equality check)
findings.technique: Correct RFC 6962 / RFC 9162 inclusion-proof verification
findings.source: RFC 9162 Certificate Transparency v2 https://www.rfc-editor.org/info/rfc9162/ ; internal: src/sigstore.js:400-477 verifyRekorInclusionProof.
findings.current_state: verifyRekorInclusionProof has a suspect RFC 6962 subproof walk: the lone-right-edge branch sets cursor=sib with a comment '// unused' and continues without consuming a hash in a way that doesn't match the RFC algorithm; and the root comparison (lines 466-469) wraps both sides in Buffer.alloc(32,0).fill(...slice(0,32)) before crypto.timingSafeEqual, which truncates to 32 bytes and is brittle/incorrect for non-32-byte or mismatched-length roots. Real Rekor proofs for non-trivial tree shapes can be mis-verified (false reject, or false accept if lengths line up).
findings.proposed_change: Replace the hand-rolled walk with the canonical RFC 9162 verify_inclusion algorithm (left/right decision via tree-size arithmetic), and compare roots with a plain length-checked crypto.timingSafeEqual on full digests (no alloc/fill/slice). Add RFC + live-Rekor test vectors. Until fixed, treat real (non-dry-run) inclusion proofs as 'present but unverified' rather than asserting verified=true.
findings.rationale: A transparency-log proof that can mis-verify is worse than none because it launders trust. The dry-run default returns inclusion verified=false anyway, so blast radius today is limited to operators running a real Rekor; but if finding #3 (anchor live receipts) ships, this verifier becomes load-bearing and must be correct first.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED REAL, not correctly implemented. I ran the ACTUAL verifyRekorInclusionProof (src/sigstore.js:400-477) against textbook-correct RFC 6962 trees (canonical MTH + inclusion-path generation) for n=1..9, every leaf index, plus a length-mismatch probe. Results: (1) 6 of 45 valid proofs FALSE-REJECTED — every one a right-edge leaf in a non-power-of-2 tree (n=3 m=2; n=5 m=4; n=6 m=4,5; n=7 m=6; n=9 m=8), exactly the shapes that hit the broken lone-right-edge branch `cursor = sib; // unused; continue` (lines 441-451). The `index%2===1 || index+1===size` decision diverges from canonical RFC 9162 verify_inclusion for unbalanced subtrees. (2) FALSE-ACCEPT confirmed: a 33-byte claimed root whose first 32 bytes match the correct root verified=true, because Buffer.alloc(32,0).fill(claimedRoot.slice(0,32)) (lines 466-469) truncates before crypto.timingSafeEqual; a length-checked compare would reject. Both halves of the finding reproduce.

Test-coverage gap is real: wave258 #3/#4 and wave162 #4 only exercise single-leaf trees (treeSize:1, hashes:[]) or rejection; wave258 #1/#2/#5/#6 are source-regex asserts, not behavioral. The buggy multi-level walk is genuinely untested, so it passes CI today.

Blast radius matches the finding: default build is dry-run (rekor_log_entry:null, dry_run:true), where the verifier is never called (sigstore.js:353-354) — typical user unaffected. It only becomes load-bearing when an operator sets KOLM_SIGSTORE_REKOR_URL and runs attestArtifactWithRekor/kolm sigstore-attest against a real Rekor; then line 360 both wrongly rejects legitimate non-power-of-2 proofs and (via truncation) could launder a forged root. EFFORT: 2 days fair — walk swap is ~20 lines of known algorithm, compare fix trivial, bulk is real multi-shape + live-Rekor test vectors. IMPACT: medium is accurate and correctly self-gated (low today, load-bearing once live-anchoring finding #3 ships). Compliance/self-hosted-Rekor operators would care; broad user base not yet. Files cited (src/sigstore.js, tests/wave150-sigstore.test.js) are correct, though tests/wave258-audit-fixes.test.js and tests/wave162-sigstore-rekor.test.js are the more directly relevant test homes.

findings.title: Wire the lifecycle revoke/supersede side-effects to a real subscriber
findings.technique: Durable side-effect dispatch on terminal artifact transitions
findings.source: Internal audit: src/artifact-lifecycle.js:260,277,294 (in-memory _sideEffectLog stubs); EU AI Act Art. 12 lifecycle logging + post-market monitoring.
findings.current_state: ArtifactLifecycle fires blockPulls/alertDeployments/linkSuccessor on revoke/supersede, but defaults only append to an in-memory _sideEffectLog that resets per process; comments say 'Production wiring is expected to replace this.' Pull-blocking works (canPull()==false -> 410), but deployment alerting and successor-linking are not delivered anywhere durable. A revoked-key event does not actually notify bound deployments.
findings.proposed_change: Route the three hooks through a durable dispatcher: write an audit_events row (ARTIFACT.* op), enqueue a deployment alert via the existing alert ladder used by drift-alert.js, and persist the successor link on lifecycle.json (so the supersession chain survives restart). Keep the in-memory log as a test seam only.
findings.rationale: Revocation is a compliance-critical control (compromised key, license violation). 'Pulls 410' is necessary but not sufficient: a buyer expects the deployments serving the revoked artifact to be alerted and the audit trail to record it durably. Today that side effect evaporates with the process.

verdict.reason:
Finding is REAL and confirmed against the code, not refuted. Verified facts:

1. PRODUCTION PATH FIRES NO SIDE EFFECTS AT ALL. The router's only lifecycle-transition route, POST /v1/artifacts/:id/lifecycle/transition (src/router.js:8299-8328), calls the v1 `transition()` function (imported as lifecycleTransition at router.js:62). That v1 function (artifact-lifecycle.js:167-211) persists state+history to lifecycle.json but does NOT call blockPulls/alertDeployments/linkSuccessor and does NOT write an audit row.

2. The side-effect hooks only fire from the `ArtifactLifecycle` CLASS (artifact-lifecycle.js:401-407), which is NOT used anywhere in production. A repo-wide grep shows ArtifactLifecycle / blockPulls / alertDeployments / linkSuccessor / getSideEffectLog are referenced only in the module itself, in tests/wrapper-r-enrich.test.js, and in the audit JSONs. So the finding actually understates the gap: the in-memory log fires only on a code path no live route exercises.

3. Even on the class path, the three hooks just append to a process-local `_sideEffectLog` (artifact-lifecycle.js:244-308) that resets per process; the module comment literally says "Production wiring is expected to replace this." A revoked-key event does NOT notify bound deployments and does NOT write a durable audit_events row.

What ALREADY works (so it is partially present): canPull()==false → HTTP 410 Gone IS durable because revoked state lives on disk in lifecycle.json and both download handlers read it (router.js:8254-8266 and 8752). The supersession successor_id is ALSO already persisted on the v1 path because transition() writes it into the history entry (router.js:8317 passes successor_id; artifact-lifecycle.js:200). So "pull-blocking works / successor survives restart in history" — but the dedicated alert + audit-trail side effects evaporate.

I set alreadyPresent=false because the SUBSTANCE of the proposed change (durable audit row on ARTIFACT.* revoke/supersede + a delivered deployment alert) is genuinely not implemented; the existing pieces are exactly the partial-present subset the finding already acknowledges (already_partially_present:true is correct).

EFFORT (2.5 days): roughly realistic, slightly optimistic. Durable infra to wire into does exist — audit.js appendAudit() is an append-only HMAC chain (trivial to call), and drift-alert-store.js holds the webhook-delivery mechanism (the finding mislabels it as "drift-alert.js"; the real drift-alert.js is pure KL/JSD math with no delivery ladder). Real costs the estimate underweights: (a) audit.js AUDIT_OPS has NO ARTIFACT.* revoke/supersede op codes, so new stable op codes must be added (chain hashes the op, so this must be done carefully); (b) "alert bound deployments" needs a deployment→artifact-binding lookup, and no clean existing registry was found; (c) the route uses v1 transition() not the class, so wiring requires either moving the route onto the class or threading hook calls into the v1 function plus reconciling the two divergent transition tables (VALID_TRANSITIONS vs TRANSITIONS). 2-3 days is the right ballpark.

IMPACT (medium): accurate, not overblown. The load-bearing compliance control — blocking pulls of a revoked/compromised artifact — already works durably via 410. The gap is audit-trail durability + deployment notification, which matters for EU AI Act Art. 12 lifecycle logging and procurement-buyer expectations but is secondary to the access block that already functions. Medium is correct.

Dependencies/prereqs not stated: new AUDIT_OP codes (chain-stable); a deployment-binding registry to know which deployments to alert; reconciling/choosing between the two transition tables before adding hooks to the v1 path.

Users who care: compliance/procurement buyers (the Govern surface's target) expect the revoke event in the durable audit chain and expect bound deployments to be notified — today both evaporate with the process.

Relevant files (absolute): C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/artifact-lifecycle.js (lines 167-211 v1 transition with no side effects; 244-308 in-memory stubs; 325-412 unused class), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/router.js (lines 8299-8328 the live route, 8254-8266 the working 410 guard), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/audit.js (appendAudit + AUDIT_OPS missing ARTIFACT.* codes), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/drift-alert-store.js (the real webhook delivery ladder), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/tests/wrapper-r-enrich.test.js (only consumer of the class + hooks).

findings.title: Reconcile the two divergent lifecycle transition tables
findings.technique: Single canonical state machine
findings.source: Internal audit: src/artifact-lifecycle.js:47 (TRANSITIONS) vs :79 (VALID_TRANSITIONS).
findings.current_state: Two transition tables coexist: VALID_TRANSITIONS (used by transition()) allows signed->archived and has an 'undeployed' transient state; TRANSITIONS (used by class ArtifactLifecycle) does NOT allow signed->archived, lacks 'undeployed', and adds drift_detected/re_evaluated. The same logical artifact can be in a state legal under one table and illegal under the other depending on which API the caller used. Audit replay across the two is ambiguous.
findings.proposed_change: Promote TRANSITIONS (the fuller R-2 ladder) to canonical, express VALID_TRANSITIONS as a derived/compat view, and make both transition() and ArtifactLifecycle.transition() validate against the same map. Add a test asserting both entry points accept exactly the same moves from every state.
findings.rationale: A lifecycle state machine is an audit primitive; two tables that disagree mean the 'allowed transitions' control can't be stated unambiguously to an auditor. Low impact today because most callers use one path, but it's a latent correctness/audit-replay hazard that's cheap to close.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, NOT already reconciled. C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/artifact-lifecycle.js does contain two divergent transition tables that share the same on-disk persistence (_writeLifecycle -> lifecycle.json):

- VALID_TRANSITIONS (line 79), used by the exported transition() function (lines 167-211). signed -> [deployed, archived]; revoked -> [archived]; includes a transient 'undeployed' state; deployed -> [monitored, superseded, revoked, undeployed].
- TRANSITIONS (line 47), used by class ArtifactLifecycle.transition() (lines 353-411). signed -> [deployed, revoked] (NO signed->archived); revoked -> [] terminal; NO 'undeployed'; adds drift_detected + re_evaluated edges.

The disagreements the finding lists are accurate: signed->archived legal under VALID only; signed->revoked legal under TRANSITIONS only; revoked->archived legal under VALID only; 'undeployed' only in VALID; 'drift_detected'/'re_evaluated' only in TRANSITIONS. Both paths persist to the same lifecycle.json, so a record written by one IS read by the other — the audit-replay-ambiguity premise is sound in principle. The proposed single-canonical-map reconciliation and the cross-entry-point equivalence test do NOT exist (tests/r2-artifact-lifecycle.test.js #8 pins VALID_TRANSITIONS only; tests/wrapper-r-enrich.test.js pins TRANSITIONS separately — no test asserts the two agree).

HOWEVER the impact is correctly self-rated 'low' and the 'depending on which API the caller used' hazard is essentially latent, not active: the ArtifactLifecycle class is wired into NO production surface. Both real entry points — src/router.js (imports loadOrInit + VALID_TRANSITIONS, lines 61-68; GET /lifecycle and POST /lifecycle/transition) and cli/kolm.js (lc.loadOrInit at lines 5987-6230) — exclusively use the transition()/VALID_TRANSITIONS path. The class is constructed only in one test file (tests/wrapper-r-enrich.test.js). So today every real artifact only ever moves through one table; the second table is reachable only from a unit test. The dual-table design is also explicitly documented as intentional in the code comments (lines 41-46, 311-323): VALID_TRANSITIONS is kept as a v1 compat surface that callers pinned.

Effort 1.5d is slightly high for promoting one map to canonical + a derived compat view + one equivalence test in a ~400-line module; 1d is realistic. Real users (auditors needing one unambiguous 'allowed transitions' statement) would care in principle, but the practical exposure is near-zero until ArtifactLifecycle is actually used in production. Net: a genuine, cheap-to-close latent correctness/audit-hygiene item, correctly scoped as low impact. Not refuted.
