findings.title: Model-free IFD/Superfiltering difficulty score for per-pair quality (self-guided, no teacher, no nomic)
findings.technique: Instruction-Following Difficulty (IFD) / Superfiltering — perplexity-ratio difficulty computed by the STUDENT model itself; a tiny model (even GPT-2-class) ranks examples consistently with large models.
findings.source: Cherry LLM / 'From Quantity to Quality' (NAACL'24) arXiv:2308.12032; Superfiltering arXiv:2402.00530; repo github.com/tianyi-lab/Cherry_LLM
findings.current_state: src/data-curate.js quality stage calls scoreCandidateLocal() (src/distill-preference.js:220) — a hand-tuned heuristic over the OUTPUT only: length buckets, CoT markers, refusal regex, +0.3*token-overlap with a seed. It cannot tell an easy pair from a hard/informative one, and ignores the input entirely. Drops anything below minQuality=0.35.
findings.proposed_change: Add Python worker workers/data/scripts/score_ifd.py computing IFD = perplexity(answer|instruction)/perplexity(answer alone) using the configured base/student model (or a tiny gpt2 proxy when no GPU). Keep mid-to-high IFD band (drop trivially-easy and broken). Wire as an optional 'difficulty' signal in curatePairs alongside the heuristic, degrading to heuristic when python/torch absent (same pattern as _dedupViaPython).
findings.rationale: Quality scoring is the weakest link in CURATE today (pure output-side heuristic). IFD is the best-fit frontier method for kolm: self-guided, needs NO external model/teacher and NO 270MB embedder, matches the 'user owns artifacts, runs local' ethos, and proven to hit full-data quality with 5-10% of examples on Alpaca/WizardLM. Improves headline distill quality-per-pair, not just hygiene.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED, not refuted — the finding's current-state description is accurate and the proposed technique is genuinely absent.

1) Current state confirmed: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/data-curate.js quality stage (line 297) calls scoreCandidateLocal(_pairOutput(p)) from C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/distill-preference.js:220. scoreCandidateLocal is output-only (length buckets, hard/soft CoT regex, refusal regex, structure bonus, optional +0.3*tokenOverlap with seedOutput); it never reads the instruction/input. Default minQuality=0.35 drops below-threshold pairs (data-curate.js:293-302). Description matches the code exactly.

2) NOT already implemented: grep across the repo finds no IFD, no Superfiltering, no model-based/conditional perplexity, no score_ifd.py. The proposed workers/data/scripts/ directory does not exist at all — curate's dedup worker actually lives at C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/workers/distill/scripts/dedup_pairs.py (the finding's file path is slightly wrong but the gap is real). The CURATE stage itself is real and wired (cli/kolm.js cmdDataCurate at line 17118, src/router.js, scripts/data-curate-smoke.mjs).

3) Partial-but-distinct prior art the finding misses: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/curriculum-sort.js (W717, shipped + tested in tests/wave717-curriculum-distill.test.js) already computes a per-pair difficulty score that includes a perplexity component. But it is a corpus-relative SHANNON UNIGRAM perplexity (not the model-based conditional perplexity-ratio of IFD), and it is used for curriculum ORDERING in the trainer, not for FILTERING in CURATE. So the difficulty concept exists in a different form/place; true IFD does not. This is why already_partially_present should arguably be true at the concept level, but the specific IFD method and its use in curation are absent.

4) Effort underestimated: the _dedupViaPython degradation pattern is established so the JS wiring + CLI flag are cheap (cmdDataCurate harness already parses opts). But the python worker must load the configured base/student model via torch+transformers, run two forward passes per pair (PPL(answer|instruction) and PPL(answer)), implement the gpt2-CPU proxy fallback, do tmpfile JSONL plumbing, and ship tests. 3 days is light; 4-5 is realistic.

5) Impact: medium, not high. IFD is a strong conceptual fit for kolm's local/no-teacher/no-embedder ethos and quality scoring is genuinely the weakest curate stage. But the headline 5-10%-of-data result is from Alpaca/WizardLM; kolm's typical corpora are small support-distill sets where aggressive IFD-band pruning may not pay off and could remove needed coverage. Unstated prerequisites: requires the base/student model to be locally loadable (config plumbing for which model + tokenizer to use), torch+transformers in the worker env, and a defined IFD band threshold per corpus — none of which exist today. Users running local distill would benefit, but the win is incremental quality-per-pair, not the transformational gain "high" implies.

findings.title: Clustering-then-intra-cluster SemDeDup so semantic dedup scales past a few hundred pairs
findings.technique: SemDeDup: k-means cluster embeddings, then compute cosine only WITHIN each cluster and drop pairs above a similarity threshold. Turns O(n^2) full-pairwise dedup into near-linear; removes 50% of data with minimal loss.
findings.source: SemDeDup arXiv:2303.09540; NVIDIA NeMo Curator semantic-dedup docs (docs.nvidia.com/nemo/curator)
findings.current_state: workers/distill/scripts/dedup_pairs.py does a GREEDY full-pairwise scan (dedup(): for each row compare against every kept row). Dense path is a growing matmul; ngram path is an explicit O(n*kept) python loop. Fine for hundreds of merge-time pairs, but the Data Engine corpus (CSV/docs/log imports) can be 10K-500K rows where this is quadratic and slow.
findings.proposed_change: Add k-means pre-clustering (reuse computed embeddings) and run the greedy threshold pass only inside each cluster — the exact SemDeDup algorithm NeMo Curator ships. Bonus: cluster assignments double as the CURATE cluster_id, replacing the current 3-gram-prefix hash bucket with real semantic clusters.
findings.rationale: The dedup machinery, threshold (0.92), report plumbing, and degrade-to-ngram fallback all exist — only the scaling algorithm is naive. Low-risk, high-leverage upgrade that unblocks the plan's 10K-pair curate smoke test and fixes the coarse-cluster caveat the curate header already admits.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED, not refuted. The finding's current-state description is precisely accurate against the actual code. dedup() in C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/workers/distill/scripts/dedup_pairs.py (lines 260-298) is a greedy full-pairwise scan with no clustering: dense path grows kept_mat @ v (O(n*kept)), sparse/ngram path is an explicit nested Python for-loop over kept rows. No k-means, SemDeDup, sklearn, faiss, or MiniBatchKMeans exists anywhere in production code (grep hits are only in audit/research/docs files and comments). The cluster_id in C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/data-curate.js comes from _bucketKeyFor -> _bucketKey in active-learning.js, which is a 3-word-prefix string bucket, NOT learned embedding clusters; the curate header at lines 18-23 explicitly admits this coarse-cluster caveat. The planned workers/data/scripts/embed_pairs.py does not yet exist. KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md line 69 confirms a planned 10K-pair curate smoke test, and the plan specifies only nomic + cosine>0.92 (line 57) with no scaling/clustering algorithm, so the quadratic concern is genuine for that path.

Effort: 2 days is slightly optimistic. The dense SemDeDup core (reuse nomic embeddings -> MiniBatchKMeans -> intra-cluster greedy) is small, but the proposed cluster_id reuse needs new plumbing: the Python script currently returns only a JSON summary + deduped JSONL, NOT per-row cluster assignments back to data-curate.js, and a decision is needed for the ngram/no-sklearn fallback (which cannot cluster without a dep). Adjusted to ~2.5 days for the full proposed scope.

Impact: medium is accurate, even generous. Real for the unbuilt 10K-500K-row INGEST->CURATE corpus path, but shipped reality is hundreds of merge-time pairs (per the script's own docstring), and data-curate.js forces --embedder ngram today, so the actual hot path users hit is the sparse loop, not the dense matmul. Dependency caveat: SemDeDup needs the dense embedding path, which is opt-in and absent on no-GPU boxes where the whole degrade-to-ngram design exists; there it would fall back to the same naive loop. Net: a correctly-diagnosed, low-risk, plan-aligned upgrade — not present, not refuted.

findings.title: Actually request the nomic embedder for dedup + clustering instead of hardcoding the ngram fallback
findings.technique: Dense sentence-embedding semantic dedup (nomic-embed-text-v1.5 cosine) — the backend the plan (C2.2) and dedup_pairs.py default both call for.
findings.source: dedup_pairs.py default --embedder nomic-embed-text-v1.5; KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md line 57 ('cosine > 0.92')
findings.current_state: src/data-curate.js:203 hardcodes ['--embedder','ngram'] when shelling to dedup_pairs.py, so the Node curate path NEVER asks for nomic even when sentence-transformers is installed. dedupThreshold (0.92) is plumbed but operates on hashed char/word n-gram cosine — coarse lexical signal, not semantic. The plan's DoD ('removes >90% of near-dups on a 50%-dup set') is unlikely to pass on paraphrased near-dups with ngram only.
findings.proposed_change: Plumb an embedder choice from curatePairs opts (default 'auto'): pass nomic-embed-text-v1.5 and rely on dedup_pairs.py's existing graceful fallback to ngram when model/deps absent. Surface backend_used in the curate report so the Data Health panel shows whether semantic or lexical dedup ran. Keep the 270MB download an explicit opt-in hint.
findings.rationale: A one-line capability that is silently disabled. The python script ALREADY supports nomic and degrades cleanly; the Node caller just never asks. Closing it makes semantic dedup real and lets the plan's curate DoD actually pass. Lowest-effort high-impact item in the set.

verdict.reason:
Confirmed against actual code, not refuted. C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/data-curate.js:203 hardcodes ['--embedder','ngram'] in the args passed to dedup_pairs.py, so the Node curate path can NEVER request nomic, even with sentence-transformers installed. The python script C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/workers/distill/scripts/dedup_pairs.py:61 defaults --embedder to nomic-embed-text-v1.5 and already implements a clean graceful fallback to ngram (lines 337-349) when the model/deps are absent (unless --no-fallback). So the capability exists in python but is forced off by the Node caller. The ngram backend (lines 233-250) is a hashed char-trigram + word bag — purely lexical, not semantic — so paraphrased near-dups will not be caught well. KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md:57 (C2.2) explicitly calls for nomic-embed-text-v1.5 cosine>0.92, and the CURATE smoke DoD (line 69, '50% near-dup dataset -> dups removed') is unlikely to pass on paraphrases with ngram only.

Second file confirmed: cli/kolm.js cmdDataCurate (lines 17118-17150) plumbs --min-quality and --dedup-threshold but exposes NO embedder flag, so there is no user-facing way to opt into semantic dedup either.

The 'surface backend_used' part is also genuinely missing: _dedupViaPython captures summary.backend (data-curate.js:242) but curatePairs (lines 306-316) drops it — it only sets report.dedup='ok' and never propagates the backend into the curate report, so the Data Health panel cannot tell whether semantic or lexical dedup ran.

Effort (0.5d) realistic: add an embedder opt to curatePairs default 'auto', replace the hardcoded 'ngram', expose --embedder on the CLI, propagate report.dedup_backend, plus a test. The finding correctly anticipates the only real gotcha — the 270MB nomic download — by defaulting to 'auto' with graceful fallback and keeping the download an explicit opt-in hint, so it does not silently force a 270MB pull on every fresh curate run. Impact kept high because it directly gates a plan DoD (C2.2) that currently cannot be satisfied with the backend pinned to ngram; the only reason it is not 'critical' is that on a fresh box without sentence-transformers users still fall back to ngram, so the real lift requires the user to also install the embedder.

findings.title: DEITA-style complexity x quality x diversity selection (cap the corpus to the most informative subset)
findings.technique: DEITA: multiply an Evol-Complexity score by an Evol-Quality score, then greedily select score-first while enforcing embedding-space diversity (a representativeness/distance filter). 6K well-selected pairs beat 60K random.
findings.source: DEITA 'What Makes Good Data for Alignment' (ICLR'24) arXiv:2312.15685; repo github.com/hkust-nlp/deita
findings.current_state: CURATE has no SELECTION step — it only FILTERS (drop below 0.35, drop dups, drop CoT) and tags clusters. No 'keep the best N diverse examples' or complexity*quality joint ranking. ingestFile imposes a budget cap but it is a row-count/byte cap, not an informativeness cap. Persona/evol augment exists but nothing prunes the resulting pile to a diverse high-value subset.
findings.proposed_change: Add a selection stage to curatePairs (opts.target_size): rank survivors by combined_score = difficulty(IFD) * quality(heuristic), then greedily add highest-score pairs while skipping any within cosine<diversity_tau of an already-selected pair (reuse dedup embeddings). Emit a selection report. This is DEITA's score-first-diversity-aware loop.
findings.rationale: Once IFD scoring and cluster embeddings exist, DEITA selection is the natural payoff and the single most-cited instruction-tuning data result (10x less data, equal/better model). For a distillation product where teacher tokens cost money, fewer better pairs directly lowers user spend AND raises K-Score. Moderate confidence: right target_size is task-dependent and needs a default heuristic.

verdict.reason:
Verified against actual code; the finding survives refutation attempt but is over-scoped and over-rated.

NOT already present (confirmed). src/data-curate.js (curatePairs) runs exactly 5 stages: quality-filter (drop <0.35), dedup (shell to dedup_pairs.py), cluster-tag, cot-drop, pii-redact. There is NO selection/cap-by-informativeness stage, no combined complexity*quality ranking, and no diversity-aware greedy keep-top-N. The CLI curate handler (cli/kolm.js ~17125-17136) only wires --no-* toggles, --min-quality and --dedup-threshold; there is no --target-size/selection flag. 'DEITA', 'Evol-*', 'score_ifd', 'diversity_tau' appear ONLY in the audit finding file itself, nowhere in product code. The referenced worker workers/data/scripts/score_ifd.py does NOT exist (the directory workers/data/scripts/ doesn't exist at all). No IFD/instruction-following-difficulty scoring exists anywhere in src/ or workers/.

current_state is accurate: ingest caps in src/data-ingest.js are MAX_FILE_BYTES (500MB), MAX_ROW_CHARS (~8k tokens), and DEFAULT_BUDGET_USD ($5, cost cap on the teacher-backed describe path) — all byte/char/dollar caps, exactly NOT an informativeness cap as the finding states. The only 'target_size' tokens in the repo refer to MODEL size (phi-3-mini, 7b), unrelated to corpus selection.

Effort 3d is underestimated -> ~6d. The proposal explicitly stacks on two prerequisite findings ('once IFD scoring and cluster embeddings exist'). Neither exists: there is no IFD scorer/model/worker, and the current dedup path uses an 'ngram' backend that returns only kept rows + a summary — it emits NO embeddings back to JS, so the proposed 'reuse the dedup embeddings' for cosine<diversity_tau has nothing to reuse. Real embedding plumbing plus an IFD scorer (needs a model to compute perplexity with/without instruction) must be built first. 3d assumes both prereqs done; standalone this is 5-8d.

Impact high -> medium. The DEITA '6K beats 60K' result is genuine and well-cited, and fewer/better pairs is a defensible win on a curate surface users already run. But the rationale overstates teacher-spend savings: selection happens AFTER pairs are generated/ingested, so it cuts training cost, not teacher token cost, unless paired with selective augmentation (not proposed). K-Score uplift is plausible but unproven for this product, and the finding itself flags the target_size default as task-dependent/unsolved. Real and worth doing, but medium-impact and bigger than 3 days.

findings.title: Balance / distribution-skew analysis in CURATE (the plan's C2.4/C2.8, currently unbuilt)
findings.technique: Category-balance auditing: flag >3:1 cluster ratios and compare the training cluster histogram to the PRODUCTION traffic histogram (coverage analysis), surfacing over/under-represented topics with counts.
findings.source: KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md C2.4/C2.8; NeMo Curator data-blending stage; DoReMi domain-reweighting intuition arXiv:2305.10429
findings.current_state: src/data-curate.js builds report.coverage (per-cluster histogram) but never analyzes it — no balance ratio flag, no production comparison. The production-distribution machinery exists elsewhere: active-learning.detectCoverageGaps() accepts production_histogram and data-feedback.identifyProdGaps() consumes it. CURATE imports/uses none of it (grep 'balance|production_distribution' in data-curate.js = nothing).
findings.proposed_change: In curatePairs after clustering, compute max:median cluster ratio and emit report.balance = {worst_ratio, over_represented, under_represented}. When a production_histogram is passed (already available from active-learning), compute per-cluster train-vs-prod skew. Mostly wiring existing pieces into the curate report so the Data Health Balance bar (plan step 9) has real numbers.
findings.rationale: The plan explicitly lists C2.4 and C2.8 as CURATE deliverables, the histogram is already built, and production-comparison code exists in two sibling modules. Low-risk integration that fills a named hole and makes the planned Data Health Balance bar non-cosmetic.

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED REAL, NOT PRESENT. src/data-curate.js (lines 318-328) builds report.coverage as a {cluster_id:count} histogram but performs zero analysis: no balance ratio, no report.balance, no production comparison. Grep for balance|production_histogram|over_represented|worst_ratio|3:1 in data-curate.js = nothing. It imports activeLearning ONLY for _bucketKey clustering, never detectCoverageGaps. The cited sibling machinery is genuinely real and unused by CURATE: active-learning.detectCoverageGaps() (src/active-learning.js:365) accepts opts.production_histogram, and data-feedback.identifyProdGaps() (src/data-feedback.js:109) consumes coverage gaps. The KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md explicitly names C2.4 (train-vs-prod distribution), C2.5 (gap counts), and C2.8 (flag >3:1 category ratio) as CURATE deliverables, plus step 9 Data Health "Balance bar (with category ratios)" — so this is a named planned hole, not a shipped/intentional feature mis-flagged. No test asserts report.balance. The "balance" hits in other modules (lang-balanced-sampler.js etc.) are cross-lingual NMT sampling, an unrelated concept. EFFORT: over-estimated (rare for this repo). The local max:median ratio flag is ~15 lines of arithmetic over the existing histogram; ~0.5-1 day with tests, below the claimed 1.5. The "just wire detectCoverageGaps in" framing oversells reuse — that function is shaped around capture-row arrays, not a curate cluster histogram, so per-cluster train-vs-prod skew is computed directly, and the prod half depends on a production_histogram actually being plumbed to curatePairs callers (CLI/router), an unstated prerequisite. IMPACT: medium is fair — the local balance bar needs this to be non-cosmetic; the prod-comparison half is conditional on captures existing. Team/enterprise dataset builders care; distribution skew silently degrades distilled models. Relevant file: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/data-curate.js.

findings.title: DCLM/Nemotron-style learned quality classifier as an optional CURATE filter
findings.technique: fastText (or small linear) binary quality classifier trained on a high-quality vs low-quality seed split; keep top-scoring fraction. DCLM-Baseline showed model-based filtering is the single biggest lever (+6.6 pts MMLU vs heuristics).
findings.source: DataComp-LM arXiv:2406.11794 (DCLM-Baseline); Nemotron-CC ensemble quality classifier (developer.nvidia.com); Ultra-FineWeb arXiv:2505.05427
findings.current_state: All filtering in src/data-curate.js is rule-based (length/CoT/refusal). No learned quality signal of any kind. The plan's C2.1 ('quality score: length+relevance+completeness+grammar+confidence') is implemented only as length/CoT heuristic — relevance/completeness/grammar not modeled.
findings.proposed_change: Add optional workers/data/scripts/quality_classifier.py: bootstrap a fastText classifier from the user's own curated-good vs filtered-bad split (or a shipped seed), score each pair, keep top fraction. Self-improving — learns the user's domain notion of 'good'. Degrade to heuristic when fasttext/python absent.
findings.rationale: DCLM proves model-based filtering beats heuristics by a wide margin at pretraining scale. For small task-distillation corpora the win is smaller with bootstrap risk (needs good/bad seed split), so it ranks below IFD which needs no labels. Ship as opt-in once IFD lands; lower confidence because cold-start labeling is a real UX cost.

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED, not refuted. Verified against actual code: all CURATE filtering in src/data-curate.js is rule-based. The quality stage calls scoreCandidateLocal (src/distill-preference.js:220) which scores ONLY on hard/soft CoT markers, a refusal regex, length bands, and list-structure presence (+ optional token overlap vs a seed). No learned quality signal exists. src/data-feedback.js:312 comment literally states "a length proxy, not a learned quality signal." Repo-wide search for fasttext/DCLM/FineWeb/quality_classifier returns ZERO product-code hits — only the audit files themselves and unrelated user-facing 'text classifier' distillation examples plus the Nemotron model entry in model-registry.js (a distillable model, not a data filter). Plan item C2.1 promises 'length + relevance + completeness + grammar + confidence' but relevance/completeness/grammar are genuinely unmodeled, exactly as the finding states. effort: 3 days is at the low end. The Node degrade-to-python shell pattern already exists (_dedupViaPython is a direct template), so wiring is cheap, but the cold-start good/bad seed labeling UX, tmp plumbing, and tests push it to ~4 days realistically. impact: medium is fair but borderline-generous. DCLM's +6.6 MMLU lever is web-scale pretraining; kolm users curate tiny task-distillation corpora (W869 pilots used 12-410 pairs) where model-based filtering's edge shrinks sharply and bootstrap risk rises. The finding's own rationale concedes this and correctly ranks it below label-free IFD. Dependencies disclosed: fastText/python (degrades), good/bad seed split (real UX cost), IFD landing first. Holds up at medium impact, opt-in.

findings.title: Real per-pair grammar/completeness/relevance signals to honour the C2.1 quality spec
findings.technique: Lightweight reference-grounded quality signals: input-output relevance (embedding cosine or token overlap input<->output), completeness (truncation/ends-mid-sentence detection), and basic grammar/format checks — the components C2.1 names but the heuristic omits.
findings.source: KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md C2.1; Cleanlab automated data curation for LM fine-tuning (ACL'24, cleanlab.ai/research)
findings.current_state: scoreCandidateLocal scores the OUTPUT in isolation plus optional token-overlap with a SEED reference (rarely present at curate time). It never checks whether the output answers the INPUT (relevance), whether it was truncated (completeness), or grammar/format. A fluent-but-off-topic answer scores well.
findings.proposed_change: Extend the quality stage: (a) relevance = tokenOverlap(input, output) or embedding cosine (reuse dedup embeddings); (b) completeness = penalize outputs ending without terminal punctuation / mid-list / at the MAX_ROW_CHARS truncation boundary; (c) keep CoT/refusal. Surface the component breakdown in the report so Data Health can explain WHY a pair scored low.
findings.rationale: The plan promised five quality components; only two ship. These are cheap, deterministic, no-new-dep additions that materially improve drop decisions (catching off-topic and truncated outputs the current scorer passes) and give the planned Data Health 'why dropped' UX something real to show.

verdict.reason:
Could not refute — the gap is real and verified in code. C2.1 (KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md:56) names five quality components: length + relevance + completeness + grammar + confidence. scoreCandidateLocal (src/distill-preference.js:220-236) implements length (lines 228-231), CoT/refusal (224-227, ~confidence proxy), a 'structured' list bonus (weak format), and ref_overlap ONLY against an optional seedOutput (233-234). Crucially, curatePairs calls scoreCandidateLocal(_pairOutput(p)) with NO seedOutput (src/data-curate.js:297), so at curate time even the token-overlap signal never fires, and it overlaps with a REFERENCE not the INPUT — there is genuinely zero input-vs-output relevance check. No completeness/truncation check exists. So a fluent-but-off-topic or truncated answer passes (would score ~0.4-0.55, above minQuality 0.35).

The finding's completeness premise is concretely grounded: MAX_ROW_CHARS (=32000) IS real, and docs/file ingestion TRUNCATES output via .slice(0, MAX_ROW_CHARS) (src/data-ingest.js:597, 981) — those mid-sentence-truncated chunks currently only lose 0.1 (very_long) and survive curate. The 'MAX_ROW_CHARS truncation boundary' is not fictional.

This is NOT a false-positive flag: the finding's own already_partially_present:true is correct (length + a weak format bonus ship; relevance/completeness/grammar don't). No committed test pins scoreCandidateLocal's numeric scores (grep of tests/ = no matches), so extending it is low-risk.

Calibration caveats (kept impact at medium, not down to low): (1) the embedding-cosine variant of relevance is NOT free as implied — embeddings live only in the python dedup worker, not in JS at curate time, so only the deterministic tokenOverlap(input,output) variant is the no-dep option; tokenOverlap relevance is itself a crude, gameable signal. (2) Grammar/format is the weakest sub-component (regex/English-only, brittle). (3) The headline payoff 'give Data Health why-dropped UX something real' is partly latent: the audit confirms the Data Health / account UI panel is MISSING (data-engine.audit.json), so half the value waits on unbuilt UI. (4) This finding addresses the same root weakness as two sibling findings that are higher-leverage — the nomic-embedder 'bug' fix (0.5d, real semantic relevance/dedup) and the IFD difficulty score (3d, model-based) — so the marginal value over those is modest. (5) Effort 1.5d is fair for the tokenOverlap+completeness+per-pair-reason-in-report core (the quality stage currently only counts quality_filtered, so emitting per-pair component breakdown is a real but small extension); the embedding-cosine option would push it higher. Users who import messy CSV/log/docs corpora (kolm's ingest paths) would benefit from dropping off-topic and truncated pairs, so users would care — moderately.

Relevant files (absolute): C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/distill-preference.js (scoreCandidateLocal:220, tokenOverlap:209), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/data-curate.js (quality stage:291-303), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/data-ingest.js (MAX_ROW_CHARS:182, truncation:597/981, drop_long:250), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md (C2.1:56).

findings.title: Cleanlab-style label/answer-error detection (flag mislabeled or contradictory pairs, do not just dedup)
findings.technique: Confident learning: use model self-confidence (or cross-pair agreement) to surface examples whose labeled answer is likely WRONG — distinct from duplicates. Cleanlab's ACL'24 work targets LM fine-tuning data.
findings.source: Cleanlab confident learning arXiv:1911.00068; 'Automated Data Curation for Robust LM Fine-Tuning' (ACL'24, cleanlab.ai/research); github.com/cleanlab/cleanlab
findings.current_state: CURATE has no error/outlier detection. A pair with a confidently-wrong answer (mislabeled CSV row, stale doc answer, contradictory log response) passes every gate as long as it is fluent, non-dup, non-CoT, PII-clean. Imported helicone/litellm/openai-finetune logs are exactly where bad labels live (a 4xx error response captured as the 'answer').
findings.proposed_change: Add an outlier/error pass: for clustered pairs, flag answers that disagree with cluster consensus (low agreement) and answers the student model finds high-loss-but-low-IFD (likely noise, not difficulty). Surface as a review queue (wire the existing /v1/label-queue route per plan F6.7) rather than auto-dropping, since false positives are costly.
findings.rationale: Garbage-label-in is a real failure mode for the log-import ingest paths kolm just built, and no current stage catches it. But true confident-learning needs a trained classifier / probability estimates, so a faithful implementation is heavier and false-positive-prone — hence medium impact, lower confidence. A cluster-consensus heuristic is a pragmatic first cut.

verdict.reason:
NOT REFUTED — the gap is real and not present. Verified directly in C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/data-curate.js: CURATE has exactly 5 gates (quality, dedup, cluster, cot, pii) and none detects mislabeled or contradictory answers. The "quality" gate calls scoreCandidateLocal (src/distill-preference.js:220) which is a per-pair fluency/format heuristic (CoT penalty, refusal penalty, length, list-structure, optional ref-overlap) scoring each pair IN ISOLATION — no cross-pair agreement, no model confidence, no consensus. A fluent-but-wrong answer (e.g. a captured 4xx error string, a stale doc answer) scores ~0.5+ and survives every gate. A repo-wide grep for consensus/outlier/mislabel/confident-learning/label-error across src/ returns no curation logic (only one unrelated "outlier" comment in active-learning.js about bucket sizes).

The log-import failure mode is genuine: src/data-ingest.js exposes ingestFrom format adapters that normalize external logs (helicone/litellm/openai-finetune-style) into raw-pairs.jsonl, which is exactly where bad labels land, and nothing downstream catches them.

Two corrections to the finding, neither of which refutes it: (1) the cited file workers/data/scripts/score_ifd.py does NOT exist — there is no IFD script anywhere in the repo, and the workers/data/scripts/ directory itself does not exist (data scripts live under workers/distill/scripts/). So a faithful confident-learning implementation must BUILD the IFD scorer from scratch, making it heavier than implied. (2) The /v1/label-queue routes ALREADY exist and are wired (src/router.js ~18781-18875), fed by the active-learning labeling queue — but they are NOT fed by any error-detection pass. The plan's F6.7 ("wire existing /v1/label-queue routes" for human review) is real and confirmed unbuilt at KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md:119.

Effort: bumped 3→4 days. The pragmatic cluster-consensus heuristic alone is ~2-3 days (reuses _bucketKeyFor clustering + the dedup ngram embedder), but the finding also proposes the high-loss-low-IFD signal which requires a student model + the non-existent score_ifd.py, plus surfacing into the review queue with its own UI/route shape — realistically 4+ days for the full proposal as written, and false-positive-prone (the finding's own confidence 0.58 reflects this).

Impact: medium is accurate — garbage-label-in is a real degradation for the just-built log-import paths, but it is a quality refinement (review-queue, not auto-drop), not a ship blocker. Users running distill on imported production logs would care; users on hand-curated seed data would not.

findings.title: Provenance-aware data mixing / reweighting at TRAIN handoff (lightweight DoReMi intuition)
findings.technique: Data mixing/reweighting: rather than uniform sampling, weight clusters/sources to balance worst-case coverage (DoReMi minimax over domains; mixing-laws to predict good proportions).
findings.source: DoReMi arXiv:2305.10429; 'Scaling Laws for Optimal Data Mixtures' arXiv:2507.09404
findings.current_state: curated-pairs.jsonl is consumed downstream as a flat uniform set; cluster_id and source_type provenance are recorded but never used to reweight or rebalance sampling. Over-represented clusters dominate the loss exactly as the raw histogram dictates.
findings.proposed_change: At the curate->train handoff, optionally upsample under-represented clusters / cap over-represented ones toward a target distribution (uniform-over-clusters or production-matched), using cluster_id and the balance report. Cheap, training-free reweighting — full DoReMi proxy-model optimization is overkill for task-distillation corpora.
findings.rationale: Full DoReMi is a pretraining-scale technique with limited ROI on small SFT/distill sets, so only the cheap reweighting intuition transfers. Listed for completeness and because the provenance to do it cheaply already exists, but it ranks last: lower confidence, low expected impact relative to fixing quality scoring and semantic dedup first.

verdict.reason: Could not fully refute the gap, but the finding materially understates existing capability. VERIFIED PREMISE: CURATE (src/data-curate.js, lines 318-328) tags every survivor with cluster_id and builds a coverage histogram, but the curate->train handoff consumes curated-pairs.jsonl as a flat/uniform set. pipeline-train.js (lines 56-78) just spawns the distill worker with --spec <flat pairs>; training-planner.js has zero references to cluster_id/coverage/reweight/balance. So cluster_id-keyed reweighting at the train handoff genuinely is NOT implemented. Note: src/data-engine.js (one of the two cited files) does NOT exist; the engine is split across src/data-{ingest,curate,augment,evaluate,feedback}.js. STRONGEST REFUTATION (makes the finding's already_partially_present:false WRONG): the exact DoReMi-intuition reweighting machinery the finding proposes is ALREADY SHIPPED in two modules, just keyed on language instead of cluster. src/lang-balanced-sampler.js sampleBalanced() implements uniform / sqrt_inverse / log_inverse / traffic_weighted per-source reweighting with budget allocation and honest-finite-supply sampling (no fabrication). src/lingual-mixture.js buildMixture() adds a stateful mixture iterator + autoBalanceWeights() that floors underrepresented buckets at 0.05 (precisely 'upsample under-represented / cap over-represented toward a target distribution'). Both are wired into router.js. EFFORT: 2 days is OVERESTIMATED -- the weighting/budgeting/sampling primitives exist; the real work is repointing the partition key from detected-language to the already-recorded cluster_id and adding a curate->train wire, closer to 0.5-1 day. IMPACT/CONFIDENCE: 'low' and 0.5 are accurate; the finding's own rationale concedes full DoReMi is pretraining-scale with limited ROI on small SFT/distill corpora, and ranks it last behind quality-scoring and semantic-dedup fixes. AUGMENT's gapFill (data-augment.js:152) already consumes the coverage map but only to GENERATE new synthetic prompts for thin clusters -- a different lever than reweighting existing pairs, so it does not close this gap either. Net: the specific cluster-keyed train-handoff reweighting is a real, small, low-value gap; the finding is real but should be downgraded to already_partially_present and lower effort because the reweighting engine itself is already built. Users (small-corpus task-distillation) would barely notice.

gaps: NO automated test coverage for the autopilot 'intelligence layer': quality-predictor (predictKScore/backfillFromRuns), compile-simulator (simulateCompile/decideFromDeltaK), failure-analyst (analyzeFailures), cost-optimizer (rankStrategies), and the autopilot-lifecycle capstone (tickAutopilotFull/objectToDeploy). Grep of tests/ for these symbols returns nothing. All are HTTP- and CLI-wired and pass manual smoke tests, but no regression net exists.

The auto-deploy guardrail (autopilot-lifecycle._evaluateDeploy) is the highest blast-radius path (can autonomously promote a model) and has zero automated tests for the 5-condition gate or the 48h grace/objection clock. The grace clock is intentionally test-backdatable (_eventTs prefers fb.proposed_at) but no test exercises it.

quality-predictor heuristic calibration (CAL_SLOPE 0.92 / CAL_INTERCEPT 0.07) is hand-fit to a SINGLE real run (Trinity-500). Absolute K away from Trinity-like vectors is an acknowledged extrapolation; confidence is correctly capped LOW (<=0.45) but nothing validates it against a second anchor.

The learned (GBM) prediction path has never been exercised end-to-end with >=1000 rows in any test; only the heuristic path is smoke-verified. The _toMetaFeatures proxy-slot projection is internally consistent but semantically arbitrary, so learned-path accuracy is unverified.

kolm-meta-trainer is by design a toy depth-1 stump GBM with no regularization/subsampling; the documented production path (apps/trainer/meta_xgb.py worker shell) is referenced but not active - acceptable documented tech-debt, but the 'meta-distillation' headline is backed only by the toy today.

cost-optimizer strategy feature-deltas are characteristic priors, not measured outcomes; ranking is only as calibrated as the heuristic predictor. Negative ΔK is clamped to 0, which can mask a strategy that would actually regress quality.

bakeoff's real per-model path depends on KOLM_LLM_PROVIDER/KOLM_LLM_KEY; without them per-model contestants emit {error:'llm_not_configured'}. Coverage relies on opts.stubModel; real-LLM ranking accuracy is not asserted.
audit.summary: The Autopilot surface is broad and mostly real. The W775 daemon core (enable/disable/status/tick) and savings join are production-grade and well-tested (24 hermetic tests incl. HTTP-route integration, all green). The W720 self-improvement orchestrator + compareAndDecide gate, the W832 toy stump-GBM meta-trainer, and the W777/W822 A/B router each have dedicated passing tests (I ran the four wave suites: 73/73 green). The newer 'intelligence' layer the daemon composes - quality-predictor (qp-v1), compile-simulator (cs-v1), failure-analyst (fa-v1), cost-optimizer (co-v1), and the autopilot-lifecycle capstone tickAutopilotFull (apl-v1) - is fully wired into HTTP routes and the CLI and behaves correctly under my smoke tests, but has NO automated test coverage (no test references predictKScore/simulateCompile/analyzeFailures/rankStrategies/tickAutopilotFull). Honesty contracts are strong throughout: cold-start heuristic predictor with LOW capped confidence, propose-only-default deploy guardrail (5 conditions + 48h grace), drift-red holds, and tenant fences everywhere. Top risks: (1) the autonomous-deploy guardrail is the highest blast-radius path and is untested; (2) the whole intelligence layer lacks a regression net; (3) the predictor is hand-calibrated to a single Trinity anchor and the GBM is deliberately a toy. Full result written to C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/audit/w920/autopilot.audit.json.

findings.title: Traffic split is deterministic 50/50 with no learning -- adopt Thompson-sampling bandit allocation to cut regret while the test runs
findings.technique: Thompson sampling / multi-armed bandit adaptive traffic allocation
findings.source: Agrawal & Goyal 'Analysis of Thompson Sampling for the Multi-armed Bandit' PMLR v23 / arXiv:1111.1797; IBM Research KDD 2024 tutorial 'Multi-Armed Bandit Applications for LLMs'; 'LLM Routing with Dueling Feedback' arXiv:2510.00841
findings.current_state: src/ab-router.js assignArm()/pickVariant() use fnv1a(request_hash) % 1000 against a fixed split threshold. The split is static for the life of the test; every losing-arm assignment is wasted quality/cost until a human or autoRollback flips it. No exploration/exploitation -- pure fixed allocation.
findings.proposed_change: Add an optional bandit allocation mode to the W777 router: maintain a Beta (or Gaussian-on-kscore) posterior per arm from recorded outcomes, and on assignArm sample each posterior and route to the argmax (Thompson sampling). Keep deterministic split as default (sticky, reproducible), gate bandit mode behind a per-test flag so the 21 W777 tests are unaffected. recordOutcome already persists the per-arm kscores needed. Optionally add an epsilon floor so an unlucky early arm is never starved. Composes with auto-deploy: a converged bandit is a stronger promote signal.
findings.rationale: Fixed 50/50 is the textbook thing bandits beat -- adaptive allocation cuts cumulative regret (lost quality/cost on the losing arm) while a test is live, matching the autopilot's 'every dollar to the highest-yield move' thesis. The posterior also gives the deploy guardrail a cleaner 'P(arm B is best)' than a peeked p-value.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED, NOT REFUTED. The finding accurately describes the actual code. In src/ab-router.js, both assignArm() (lines 270-283) and pickVariant() (lines 909-938) compute a static split via fnv1a(request_hash) % HASH_BUCKETS(=1000) compared to Math.floor(split * 1000) — a fixed, deterministic allocation with zero exploration/exploitation. The split is set once at createAbTest()/setSplit() and never adapts. recordOutcome() (line 294) does persist per-arm kscores to the event-store, exactly as the finding states, so the data needed for a posterior exists. The promote/rollback path uses src/stat-sig.js welchT()/gate() — a frequentist Welch t-test, NOT a Beta/Gaussian posterior. No Thompson sampling, Beta posterior, epsilon-greedy, or adaptive allocation exists anywhere in the A/B traffic path. The only bandit/Thompson references in src/ and docs are roadmap/research aspirations: W9206 "Contextual Bandit Router" and W596 "Bandit Registry Ranker" (the latter is marketplace/registry artifact RANKING via src/registry.js, a different surface, not traffic allocation), plus the assistant corpus index and research markdown. quality-predictor.js mentions "posterior" only to clarify its CI is explicitly NOT a posterior interval. So alreadyPresent=false is solid.

CAVEATS on the finding's estimates: (1) Effort: 4 days is plausible but slightly optimistic. There are TWO parallel splitters (W777 SQLite-backed assignArm + W822 jsonl-backed pickVariant) both using fnv1a bucketing, plus HTTP routes (src/ab-routes.js) and kolm's standing CLI/TUI surface-parity mandate, plus new tests, plus the requirement to keep deterministic as default behind a flag. Realistically 4-6 days. The finding says "21 W777 tests" but the file actually has 22 test() blocks / 33 assertions — immaterial. (2) Impact "high" is mildly generous: kolm's A/B volume is low-traffic artifact comparisons (default sample_target 1000), so absolute regret savings are modest; medium-high is fairer. (3) Real unstated tension: Thompson sampling conflicts with the explicit sticky/reproducible assignment contract downstream routing relies on (a returning request_hash could flip arms as the posterior shifts), and adaptive allocation deliberately unbalances per-arm n, which complicates the existing balanced-sample Welch gate. The proposed epsilon floor is the right mitigation and is itself extra scope. None of these refute the finding — they refine it. The finding is correct, on-thesis for an autopilot surface, and genuinely absent from the code.

findings.title: Quality predictor CI is a hand-drawn band, not a calibrated interval -- wrap predictKScore in split-conformal prediction for distribution-free coverage
findings.technique: Split / inductive conformal prediction for calibrated regression intervals
findings.source: Angelopoulos & Bates 'A Gentle Introduction to Conformal Prediction' arXiv:2107.07511; Self-Calibrating Conformal Prediction (Venn-Abers, NeurIPS 2024)
findings.current_state: src/quality-predictor.js header is explicit: 'The CI is a transparent confidence-scaled band, NOT a posterior interval.' Half-width is a literal affine function of a heuristic confidence (half = 0.26 - 0.32*confidence). compile-simulator and cost-optimizer ride this band, so every compile/skip and ship/no-ship decision inherits an interval with NO coverage guarantee.
findings.proposed_change: Once backfillFromRuns has >= a few dozen (features -> observed K) rows, hold out a calibration split, compute the residual quantile |observed - predicted| at level 1-alpha, and return [pred - q, pred + q] as a conformal interval with real (e.g. 90%) marginal coverage. Falls back to the heuristic band below the calibration-size floor (same heuristic->learned flip pattern). ~40 lines of pure JS: sort residuals, take the ceil((n+1)(1-alpha))/n quantile. Turns 'confidence:0.18' into 'this interval covers true K 90% of the time' that the deploy guardrail can trust.
findings.rationale: The module already collects the exact (features, observed K) rows conformal needs (backfillFromRuns) and already has a heuristic->learned flip seam to graft a calibration-size gate onto. Conformal is model-agnostic, needs no GBM retraining, and replaces an admittedly-fabricated band with a guaranteed one -- directly raising trust in the autonomous compile/deploy gates.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, NOT ALREADY PRESENT. The finding's description of src/quality-predictor.js is accurate: the CI is a literal affine confidence-scaled band (_predictHeuristic: half = 0.26 - 0.32*confidence, line 322; learned path half = 0.155 - 0.11*confidence, line 365), and the header (lines 48-50) explicitly states "The CI is a transparent confidence-scaled band, NOT a posterior interval." A repo-wide search for "conformal" returns ONLY docs/research/audit files, never the implementation — no split/inductive conformal exists anywhere. backfillFromRuns DOES persist (features -> observed K) rows (lines 441-446, with the source data-quality vector preserved as _qp_features), so the raw material conformal needs is genuinely collected. The heuristic->learned flip seam at MIN_ROWS_FOR_META exists to graft a calibration gate onto. So the technique is real, model-agnostic, and applicable.

CAVEATS THAT LOWER THE FINDING'S CLAIMS:
1. IMPACT OVERSTATED (high -> medium). The finding claims "every compile/skip and ship/no-ship decision inherits an interval with NO coverage guarantee." But I checked both dependents (src/compile-simulator.js and src/cost-optimizer.js — both exist; Glob missed them but Grep + Read confirm). They consume the POINT ESTIMATE (kscore_predicted) and the SCALAR confidence, NOT the ci band. compile-simulator's decision uses delta_k vs threshold and propagates min(confidence) (lines 201-212); cost-optimizer's ship/no-ship reaches_target uses basePred.kscore_predicted + predicted_delta_k >= target (lines 298-300) — the point estimate. The ci band is logged/returned but does not gate any guardrail today. Calibrating the interval only delivers the promised trust benefit once a guardrail is REWIRED to consume it (e.g. require ci_lo >= 0.85), which the finding does not scope.

2. DATA-AVAILABILITY PREREQUISITE UNSTATED. The module header (lines 14-18, 45-47) admits only ONE real end-to-end measured run exists (Trinity-500), and the learned/GBM path only flips at MIN_ROWS_FOR_META=1000 (kolm-meta-trainer.js:61). Conformal coverage is an asymptotic/exchangeability guarantee; at the finding's proposed "a few dozen" rows sourced from essentially one genuine run, the guarantee is largely vacuous and the calibration set is not exchangeable with future drifting autopilot runs. The finding invents a NEW calibration-size floor ("a few dozen") distinct from the 1000-row GBM floor, creating a third regime (heuristic / conformal-band / learned-GBM) the design must reconcile.

3. EFFORT SLIGHTLY UNDER (3 -> ~4 days). The quantile math is ~40 lines as claimed, but the qp-v1 contract is described as "frozen," there's a train/calibrate split to introduce, a new threshold regime + its confidence semantics, smoke-test updates (scripts/quality-predictor-smoke.mjs currently asserts the band shape), and dependent-side handling.

Net: a legitimate, well-sourced upgrade on a self-admitted fabricated band, correctly flagged as not-yet-present and partially-scaffolded — but it is a premature optimization on a cold-start surface with one real data point, and the interval it would calibrate is not currently consumed by any decision gate, so "high" impact is not warranted today.

findings.title: No learning-curve extrapolation -- predict whether MORE data of the same kind will clear the gate before spending on it
findings.technique: Power-law learning-curve fitting + extrapolation (forecast data needed to hit target K)
findings.source: 'When Scaling Meets LLM Finetuning' ICLR 2024 (openreview 5HCnKDeTws); 'A Hitchhiker's Guide to Scaling Law Estimation' arXiv:2410.11840; JMLR 26(2025) learning-curve extrapolation survey (23-0292)
findings.current_state: The cost-optimizer's 'ingest-more' strategy applies a fixed +500 n_pairs delta and the predictor scores it with a log-saturating pairs sub-score (_pairsSubScore) that is a hand-tuned universal monotone curve, NOT fit to this corpus's own (n_pairs -> K) history. No per-namespace learning curve, so the optimizer cannot answer 'how many MORE pairs to reach target K' nor detect diminishing returns / sub-scaling.
findings.proposed_change: From kscore-timeseries.js points + backfill rows, fit a per-namespace saturating power law K(n)=K_inf - a*n^-b (3-param least squares, pure JS). Use it to (a) replace _pairsSubScore's universal constant with the corpus-specific curve, and (b) add forecast_pairs_to_target(target_K) that the cost-optimizer surfaces so 'ingest-more' is priced against the ACTUAL marginal pairs needed and flagged when the curve has saturated (recommend a different strategy).
findings.rationale: kolm already records the K timeseries and per-run feature/K rows -- the only missing piece is a 3-param curve fit. Converts the optimizer from 'apply a fixed +500 prior' to 'spend exactly to target, stop when the curve flattens', the biggest lever on whether the autopilot wastes ingest budget. Medium confidence because per-namespace curves are noisy until several runs accumulate; gate behind a min-points floor like the learned-predictor flip.

verdict.reason:
PARTIALLY REAL, but the proposed implementation rests on a data substrate that does not exist, and impact is overstated. Verified against actual code.

CONFIRMED true: (1) src/quality-predictor.js _pairsSubScore (lines 126-132) is a universal hand-tuned log-saturating curve anchored at n=2200, NOT fit to any corpus's own (n_pairs->K) history. (2) src/cost-optimizer.js uses a fixed +500 n_pairs delta for 'ingest-more' (STRATEGIES['ingest-more'].delta, lines 114-119). (3) No power-law learning-curve fit, no forecast_pairs_to_target, no diminishing-returns detection exists anywhere in src/ (grep for forecast|learning.curve|power.law|K_inf|marginal.pairs returns nothing relevant). (4) src/autopilot-lifecycle.js:349 does consume rankStrategies and acts on plan.recommended, so the optimizer is on a live path. So the diagnosis 'no learning-curve extrapolation' is genuinely correct and not already implemented.

WHERE THE FINDING IS MATERIALLY WRONG (refutation grounds):
1. DATA SOURCE CLAIM IS FALSE. The finding says fit K(n) 'From kscore-timeseries.js points + backfill rows.' But kscore-timeseries.js points carry ONLY {ts, kscore, artifact_id, run_id} (recordKScore, lines 83-88) -- there is NO n_pairs axis. You cannot fit K(n) from the timeseries at all without joining to run features by run_id. The only paired (n_pairs, K) history is the meta-trainer backfill rows (_qp_features carrying n_pairs + observed.kscore), so the finding's data plan is half-wrong.
2. 'PER-NAMESPACE' IS INFEASIBLE AS DESCRIBED. The meta-trainer row store (src/kolm-meta-trainer.js appendTrainingRow / readTrainingRows) is TENANT-fenced only -- there is ZERO namespace field on training rows (grep 'namespace' in kolm-meta-trainer.js returns nothing). A per-namespace curve cannot be cut from the existing store without a schema change, directly contradicting the rationale's 'the only missing piece is a 3-param curve fit.'
3. COLD-START FLOOR GUTS THE VALUE. MIN_ROWS_FOR_META defaults to 1000 (kolm-meta-trainer.js:61). The predictor itself only flips heuristic->learned at ~1000 runs. A 3-param power-law over noisy per-namespace (n,K) points needs many distinct n values; with the product's single measured anchor run (Trinity-500 ~410 pairs) and a cold-start posture, essentially every real user is far below any sane min-points floor, so forecast_pairs_to_target would silently fall back to the same heuristic. The 'biggest lever' delivers nothing for the users who actually exist today.

EFFORT: 4 days is low. The pure-JS least-squares fit is easy, but to actually deliver the proposal you must add a namespace field + thread it through TWO backfill paths (quality-predictor.backfillFromRuns and kscore-timeseries.backfillKScoreSeries), join n_pairs to K (timeseries has no n_pairs), gate behind a min-points floor, and re-wire _pairsSubScore to optionally consume a corpus curve WITHOUT breaking the calibrated Trinity cold-start anchor. Realistically ~7 days.

IMPACT: 'high / biggest lever' is overblown. It assumes mature per-namespace (n,K) history the product does not have for any user. For the cold-start majority it changes nothing; the existing Caveats in cost-optimizer.js already say to 'treat absolute deltaK as directional.' Real but low-priority; the genuine gap is the conceptual one (no extrapolation), not a near-shippable 4-day curve fit.

findings.title: Re-distill loop has no catastrophic-forgetting / retention guard -- add a held-out retention eval + self-synthesized replay before promote
findings.technique: Replay-based continual learning (self-synthesized rehearsal) + retention/regression holdout gate
findings.source: Huang et al. 'Mitigating Catastrophic Forgetting with Self-Synthesized Rehearsal (SSR)' arXiv:2403.01244; 'Self-Evolving LLMs via Continual Instruction Tuning' arXiv:2509.18133; SDFT self-distillation fix (InfoWorld 2025)
findings.current_state: compareAndDecide (src/improvement-orchestrator.js) gates promote on delta>=min_kscore_delta AND regressions<=max_regression_classes, where regressions is the candidate's per-class set minus what the base already failed -- read from the eval artifact. But each round re-distills primarily on NEW telemetry-seeded data (telemetry_seed in run-meta); nothing forces a replay sample of prior on-policy data, and there is no dedicated retention benchmark the candidate must NOT regress on. The only guard is whatever the eval suite happens to cover.
findings.proposed_change: Two-part: (1) carry a small replay buffer of prior promoted-round pairs (or a self-synthesized rehearsal set) into orchestrateImprovement's candidate set so each round trains on new+replay (SSR); (2) add a frozen RETENTION holdout (prior round's eval set) and make compareAndDecide / the deploy guardrail fail-closed if retention K drops more than a small epsilon, not just the live regression-class count. Makes the loop monotone-or-hold rather than 'improve on new, silently forget old'.
findings.rationale: An autonomous loop that retrains every tick is the EXACT setting where catastrophic forgetting bites, and kolm currently has no explicit retention guard or replay -- it trusts the eval suite to incidentally cover old behavior. The regression-class machinery is half the gate already; adding a retention holdout + replay buffer is the standard production fix and the difference between a loop that compounds quality and one that can quietly regress.

verdict.reason:
COULD NOT REFUTE -- the gap is real and accurately described. Verified against actual code at C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/improvement-orchestrator.js, src/autopilot-lifecycle.js, src/distill-pipeline.js.

(1) NOT already implemented. compareAndDecide (improvement-orchestrator.js:276-410) gates promote on delta>=min_kscore_delta AND regressions.length<=max_regression_classes, where regressions = candidateRegressions minus baseRegressions read via _readKScoreFromArtifact from manifest.regression_classes|regressions|eval_results.regressions. There is NO frozen prior-round retention holdout compared candidate-vs-base with a fail-closed epsilon, and NO forced replay of prior promoted-round pairs into the candidate corpus. The re-distill corpus comes from prepareDistillCorpus({split:'train'}) over the namespace (distill-pipeline.js:568-573) or detectUnderperformingCaptures (new telemetry); telemetry_seed in run-meta is only capture_ids, not a replay buffer.

Crucially, the finding UNDER-states the gap: I grepped all of src/ and the regression_classes/regressions key is ONLY EVER READ (autopilot-lifecycle.js + improvement-orchestrator.js), never WRITTEN by any eval producer. kscore-bench.js emits a `by_class` breakdown but that is never mapped to regression_classes, so compareAndDecide's regressions array defaults to [] at runtime unless a caller injects it. The 'regression-class machinery is half the gate already' is optimistic -- the slot is inert scaffolding. So already_partially_present is better read as 'present-as-unfed-scaffold', i.e. effectively not present.

The nearby holdout modules are NOT this feature: external-holdout.js + tenant-holdout.js are compile/verify-time provenance layers (load a hash-pinned corpus, score the recipe, stamp a manifest block) -- not a candidate-vs-prior-round retention gate, and not wired into compareAndDecide or tickAutopilotFull. drift-supersession.js detects K drift over time to TRIGGER re-distill (not guard a candidate against forgetting). replay.js replays captured traces against an artifact for eval (not a training replay buffer).

(2) Effort 6 days realistic, slightly tight. Part-2 (frozen retention holdout + epsilon fail-closed in compareAndDecide/_evaluateDeploy) has a clean seam and is a few days. Part-1 (SSR replay buffer into worker training data) needs per-artifact training-pair snapshots that are NOT currently persisted as a queryable source -- adds work. Unstated prerequisite: the retention gate needs the eval pipeline to actually emit per-class/per-set scores into the manifest in the shape the gate reads -- today it doesn't, so this finding partially subsumes finishing the regression_classes plumbing.

(3) Impact: 'critical' is slightly overblown for kolm's CURRENT reality; 'high' is fair. The autonomous EXECUTE path is opt-in (--auto), propose-only by default, 48h grace, human-objection veto, and eval_pass===true (autopilot-lifecycle.js:140-291) -- silent forgetting cannot ship unattended. kolm ships narrow single-domain Trinity recipes where forgetting risk is lower than broad multi-task continual tuning. But it is a genuine correctness gap in the loop's monotone-improve claim, so high.

(4) Users care: yes for the autonomous-compounding-quality value prop and regulated buyers needing a non-regression guarantee; less so given today's heavily human-gated default. Net: real gap, do not refute; keep effort 6, downgrade impact critical->high.

findings.title: Cost-optimizer strategy delta-K priors are hardcoded and never updated from observed outcomes -- make them an online (contextual-bandit) posterior
findings.technique: Online / contextual-bandit update of per-strategy effect estimates (learn delta-K from realized runs)
findings.source: IBM Research KDD 2024 'Multi-Armed Bandit Applications for LLMs'; 'Large Language Model-Enhanced Multi-Armed Bandits' arXiv:2502.01118
findings.current_state: src/cost-optimizer.js STRATEGIES is Object.freeze'd with literal deltas (dedup dup_fraction -0.10; ingest-more n_pairs +500; gap-fill coverage +0.15; preference avg_quality +0.08; evol avg_quality +0.05/coverage +0.05). Header is explicit these are 'characteristic priors, not measured outcomes'. After a strategy runs, realized delta-K is never fed back, so the autopilot can keep recommending an empirically-underperforming strategy for this corpus.
findings.proposed_change: Per (tenant, namespace, strategy) maintain a running posterior over realized delta-K (the deploy/flywheel loop already records candidate K vs base K), and at rank time blend the frozen prior with the observed posterior (shrinkage toward the prior when n is small). Treat the 5 strategies as bandit arms whose reward is delta-K-per-dollar; recommend by posterior-sampled or UCB yield rather than the static prior. Prior stays the cold-start default (n=0 limit), so existing behavior is preserved.
findings.rationale: The whole pitch is 'spend the next dollar where it buys the most K' -- but the optimizer never learns which strategy actually delivered. The realized-vs-predicted delta-K signal is already produced by compareAndDecide / the flywheel; wiring it back as a per-strategy posterior closes the loop and makes the optimizer self-correcting per corpus, the autonomous-agent ideal.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED against actual code. The finding's core facts are correct. src/cost-optimizer.js lines 106-141: STRATEGIES is Object.freeze'd with literal feature deltas exactly as described (dedup dup_fraction -0.10/n_pairs -20; ingest-more n_pairs +500; gap-fill coverage +0.15/n_pairs +150; preference avg_quality +0.08; evol avg_quality +0.05/coverage +0.05). The header lines 50-56 explicitly state "The feature deltas are characteristic priors per strategy, not measured outcomes of a specific corpus." rankStrategies (lines 244-354) always recomputes predicted ΔK by projecting the frozen delta through predictKScore and never blends in any realized per-strategy outcome. Grep for bandit/posterior/thompson/UCB/realized-delta across all of src/ found ZERO implementation — the only "posterior" hits are explicit disclaimers in quality-predictor.js (lines 48,321) and kolm-meta-trainer.js (lines 18,443) stating those bands are NOT posteriors. So the loop is genuinely open: a per-strategy effect estimate is never corrected from outcomes.

NOT already present, but there IS an adjacent learning mechanism the finding under-acknowledges: the quality-predictor flips heuristic->learned via the W832 stump-GBM (backfillFromRuns -> appendTrainingRow -> trainKolmMeta in quality-predictor.js + kolm-meta-trainer.js). That learns the (features->K) mapping from realized runs. So absolute K prediction DOES self-correct once ~1000 rows accumulate; what stays frozen is the per-strategy effect prior (how much a given strategy moves each feature). The finding targets a real, distinct gap.

EFFORT (5 days) is OPTIMISTIC. The finding asserts "the realized-vs-predicted delta-K signal is already produced by compareAndDecide / the flywheel" — this is only partly true. The flywheel (autopilot-lifecycle.js lines 406-422) records simulate.k_proposed, which is a PREDICTED K, not a realized post-run K; kscore-timeseries.js recordKScore stores {ts,kscore,artifact_id,run_id} with NO strategy tag. compareAndDecide compares base vs candidate artifact K but is never keyed to which cost-optimizer strategy produced the candidate. So before any posterior can be built you must first: (a) tag each run with the applied strategy, (b) capture realized base-K and realized post-run-K (requires a real run + eval to complete), (c) build the per-(tenant,namespace,strategy) posterior store with shrinkage-to-prior, (d) blend at rank time, (e) tests. Realistic effort is ~7-9 days given the missing data plumbing.

IMPACT (high) is OVERBLOWN. Realized delta-K accrues per (tenant, namespace, strategy) only when that exact strategy runs AND a candidate is evaluated — a very slow-growing n for any single corpus. For most users the posterior would sit at the cold-start prior for a long time (the finding itself concedes prior = n=0 default), so observable behavior change is rare in practice. Medium is the accurate impact. The diagnosis is correct and aligns with the autonomous-agent ideal, so users (especially the autonomous-autopilot persona) would care directionally, but the day-to-day payoff is modest until many runs accumulate. Relevant files: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/cost-optimizer.js, src/autopilot-lifecycle.js, src/kscore-timeseries.js, src/quality-predictor.js, src/compile-simulator.js.

findings.title: Ingest selection is a coarse +N-pairs prior -- add influence-based / forward-only data valuation to pick WHICH pairs to acquire
findings.technique: Data valuation via influence functions (forward-only / closed-form) for fine-tuning data selection
findings.source: 'What is Your Data Worth to GPT? LLM-Scale Data Valuation with Influence Functions (LoGra)' arXiv:2405.13954; 'Efficient Forward-Only Data Valuation (For-Value)' arXiv:2508.10180; 'Influence Distillation' arXiv:2505.19051
findings.current_state: The autopilot's data feature vector (autopilot-lifecycle._deriveFeatures + quality-predictor) treats data as aggregate counts: n_pairs, dup_fraction (input-hash dedup), teacher_diversity, coverage_score. No per-pair value estimate -- 'ingest-more' just assumes 500 generic new pairs lift K by a fixed prior. dedup is exact-input-hash only, so near-duplicate / low-value pairs are not pruned and high-influence pairs are not preferentially acquired.
findings.proposed_change: Add a lightweight forward-only data-valuation pass (For-Value style: per-pair score from hidden-representation alignment + prediction error using the current student in a single forward pass via the existing worker shell, no gradient back-prop) to rank candidate ingest pairs and prune low-influence ones before a compile. Surface a 'select-high-value' optimizer strategy (or refine 'ingest-more' to acquire top-k by influence) so the autopilot buys quality, not volume. Worker-shell task (apps/); JS side just ranks/filters the returned scores.
findings.rationale: The data-quality frontier; would meaningfully raise K-per-dollar, but the highest-fidelity variants need a forward pass over the student (worker/GPU), so it is heavier and lower-confidence than the statistical-core fixes. Next data-engine frontier once the cheaper gates (conformal, learning-curve, bandit) land; medium because real but expensive and partly research-stage at LLM scale.

verdict.reason:
PARTIALLY REFUTED on premise, but the specific technique is genuinely not implemented.

VERIFIED-ACCURATE parts of the finding:
- src/cost-optimizer.js:114-119: `ingest-more` really is a fixed coarse prior `delta:{n_pairs:+500}`, new_pairs:500. The module even self-admits (lines 54-56) the deltas are "characteristic priors per strategy, not measured outcomes of a specific corpus."
- src/autopilot-lifecycle.js:101-121 `_deriveFeatures`: dup_fraction is computed by EXACT input-string equality (seen.has(input)) — exact-input-hash dedup only, no near-dup/semantic pruning. Features are aggregate counts (n_pairs, dup_fraction, teacher_diversity); coverage_score/avg_quality come from a curate run, not per-pair.
- No influence-function / For-Value / forward-only hidden-representation valuation exists anywhere. The trainer worker (apps/trainer/*.py) has hidden-state access (embedding.py, moe.py use output_hidden_states/last_hidden_state) but nothing scores individual training pairs by influence on the student. So the proposed TECHNIQUE is real and not implemented.

OVERSTATED premise ("No per-pair value estimate"): kolm already ships a substantial per-example value-scoring + acquisition-selection stack the finding ignores:
- src/capture-importance.js (W711): per-capture training-value score (token-density + entropy + MinHash novelty) driving WeightedRandomSampler oversampling.
- src/active-learning.js (W815): scoreCaptureRichness (importance/weakness/novelty/recency) + detectCoverageGaps + recommendNextCaptures returns TOP-K acquisition targets ranked by gap_score x demand — i.e., it already picks WHICH data to acquire, and the autopilot daemon (src/autopilot-daemon.js:41,437) consumes getCoverageGapsForNamespace as its readiness/ingest signal.
- src/active-learning-queue.js (W710): enqueues high-entropy/high-uncertainty routing decisions as the highest-value next-distill examples, priority-by-entropy.
The existing selection is statistical (entropy/Jaccard/MinHash/TF-IDF), NOT model-influence-based — so the finding's specific For-Value forward-pass valuation is the novel delta, but its claim that data is treated as aggregate counts with no per-pair value estimate is materially incomplete.

EFFORT: 9 days is optimistic. A real For-Value pass needs to load the current student on GPU in the worker shell, compute per-pair hidden-rep alignment + prediction error over a candidate pool, cross the JS<->GPU-worker boundary with a new task type, return + rank/filter scores JS-side, add the select-high-value optimizer strategy, wire cost, honest envelopes, and tests. Realistically ~14 days, not 9.

IMPACT: medium is fair (not overblown), but tempered by heavy overlap with the already-shipped active-learning stack (which already captures much of the cheap selection value) and by the dependency that the highest-fidelity variant requires a GPU forward pass over an actual student artifact, so it only helps once a student exists.

Net: isReal=true (technique not implemented), alreadyPresent=false for the influence-function technique itself, but reviewers should note kolm already has statistical per-example value scoring + top-k acquisition selection, so this is an incremental upgrade of a shipped capability rather than filling a hole.

findings.title: No cheap-proxy candidate screening before full compile -- add successive-halving (Hyperband/ASHA) over small-data trial runs
findings.technique: Successive halving / Hyperband / ASHA for resource-efficient configuration selection
findings.source: Li et al. 'Hyperband' JMLR 2018; ASHA (Li et al. 'A System for Massively Parallel Hyperparameter Tuning' MLSys 2020)
findings.current_state: The compile-simulator decides compile-vs-skip on a SINGLE proposed feature delta via two predictor point-estimates. When the autopilot does compile, orchestrateImprovement kicks ONE distill config (student_base, curriculum, council flags) -- no mechanism to cheaply screen several candidate configs/recipes on a small data slice and promote only the survivor to a full run. Config choice is effectively fixed by subOpts defaults.
findings.proposed_change: When the cost-optimizer surfaces multiple plausible moves (or multiple student bases / recipes), run them as Hyperband/ASHA rungs: a small-budget trial (few hundred pairs, short LoRA) scored by the existing eval, early-stop the bottom half, escalate survivors to larger budgets, full-compile only the winner. The pipeline already supports pairs_override and partial runs; this is an orchestration loop in improvement-orchestrator reusing compareAndDecide for rung comparisons. Bounds wasted GPU on losing configs.
findings.rationale: Real and well-proven (Hyperband/ASHA are MLOps staples), but kolm's current single-config compile path is adequate for the narrow Trinity-style recipes it ships, so payoff only materializes with multiple competing configs/recipes. Medium impact, medium confidence -- a natural follow-on to the contextual-bandit cost-optimizer rather than a day-one need.

verdict.reason: Could NOT refute the gap; the finding accurately describes kolm's actual code. compile-simulator.js (simulateCompile, L170-235) decides compile-vs-skip on a SINGLE proposed feature delta via two predictKScore point estimates; improvement-orchestrator.js orchestrateImprovement (L99-252) kicks exactly ONE distill config with student_base/use_curriculum/use_council/pairs_override taken from subOpts defaults (L151-154, 212-219); cost-optimizer.js rankStrategies (L282-310) ranks 5 strategies by predicted dK/dollar but ONLY as a forward projection through the heuristic quality predictor, not via real small-budget trial runs scored by eval; autopilot-lifecycle.js feeds the single recommended strategy delta to the simulator (L360-373). NOT already present: no successive-halving/Hyperband/ASHA/rung-escalation logic exists in src/ or tests/. The terms appear only in a wishlist research doc (docs/research/kolm-billion-dollar-distillation-lab-2026-05-24.md L41446, 85293-85295 -- arXiv links, unimplemented); 'rung' elsewhere is unrelated runtime-routing/multi-region failover ladders. The closest analog, quantize-bakeoff.js, runs candidate QUANTIZE profiles (not distill configs/recipes) and runs ALL candidates to full completion with a Pareto-frontier accept -- there is NO rung-based early-stop of the bottom half on a small budget then escalation -- so no partial credit. EFFORT: 7d is plausible-to-optimistic. The rung loop reusing compareAndDecide is a few days, but pairs_override only injects pairs into a single fire-and-forget run; there is no existing concurrent multi-config trial harness with poll/kill-loser/cancellation, pushing realistic effort to ~10d. IMPACT: medium is accurate but at the low end -- the author's own rationale concedes payoff only materializes with multiple competing configs/recipes, and kolm ships narrow Trinity-style recipes with effectively fixed config defaults, so this is a natural follow-on to a contextual-bandit cost-optimizer, not a day-one need. UNSTATED PREREQUISITES: (1) a low-variance eval trustworthy at few-hundred-pair budgets (the predictor is heuristic in cold-start, so small-slice scores may be noisy), (2) a concurrent multi-run job manager with cancellation (current orchestrateImprovement is explicitly single fire-and-forget non-blocking), (3) a real population of competing configs/recipes to screen, which the product does not yet generate. kolm's current narrow-recipe users would not feel this today; it pays off once multi-base/multi-recipe selection becomes a product surface.

findings.title: Failure-analyst clusters by hash bucket and emits canonical-answer scaffolds it cannot fill -- cluster by embedding and close the teacher loop
findings.technique: Embedding-based failure clustering + teacher-generated corrective pairs (active-failure remediation)
findings.source: Standard active-learning / hard-example mining practice; aligns with drift-detect.js which already does embedding-distribution comparison (W813); 'Selection of LLM Fine-Tuning Data based on Orthogonal Rules' arXiv:2410.04715
findings.current_state: src/failure-analyst.js clusters failing items via active-learning's _bucketKey (a deterministic hash bucket), ranks by fail_rate, and for clusters WITHOUT a reference answer emits a templated [NEEDS_CANONICAL_ANSWER] scaffold -- it performs NO live teacher call ('We never call it here'). So the worst-case failure clusters produce placeholder pairs a human/COLLECT step must later fill, breaking the autonomous loop exactly where it matters most.
findings.proposed_change: (1) Cluster failing items by embedding similarity (reuse the embedding path drift-detect.js already relies on) instead of a flat hash bucket, so semantically-related failures group correctly. (2) For scaffold clusters, optionally invoke the configured teacher (teacher_base is already threaded) to synthesize the canonical answer, turning [NEEDS_CANONICAL_ANSWER] into a real corrective fix pair -- gated by a budget/opt-in flag. Closes the EVALUATE->fix->TRAIN leg without a human in the loop.
findings.rationale: The module is structurally complete and provenance-clean, but its two honest limitations (hash-bucket clustering + scaffold-only fixes) are exactly where the autonomous loop hands back to a human. Embedding clustering is already in the codebase (drift-detect) and the teacher is already threaded; wiring them makes failure remediation genuinely autonomous. Medium because safety of auto-synthesized answers needs the eval gate to catch bad fixes.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED accurate against the code. src/failure-analyst.js clusters failing eval items via _bucketKey imported from active-learning.js (a 3-gram-prefix sha256 hash bucket, 32 fixed buckets), ranks by fail_rate (lines 201-214), and for clusters lacking a reference answer emits templated [NEEDS_CANONICAL_ANSWER] / [NEEDS_TEACHER_SYNTHESIS] scaffolds with NO live teacher call (the module header lines 27-33 and _fixPairForItem lines 234-242 state this explicitly). teacher_base IS threaded in from autopilot-lifecycle.js:396 but is only stringified into the rationale, never invoked. So the autonomous EVALUATE->fix->TRAIN leg genuinely hands back to a human exactly where worst-case clusters lack references. The repo's own audit corroborates: data-engine.audit.json line 176 says "real nomic-embed semantic dedup and k-means+teacher labeling are unbuilt" and line 180 notes the teacher-synthesis path is untested against a live teacher. NOT already implemented.

Both proposed building blocks exist and are reusable, which strengthens (not refutes) the finding: src/embedding.js provides a real embed(text)->256-dim L2-normalized vector + cosine(); src/llm-call.js (live LLM path) and src/teacher-bridge.mjs provide a teacher-call path. One inaccuracy in the finding: it says to "reuse the embedding path drift-detect.js relies on" — drift-detect.js does NOT embed text, it takes pre-computed number[][] and bins them via a seeded scalar projection; the actual text->vector path is src/embedding.js. Capability still present, just mislocated.

Effort 4 days is realistic-to-slightly-optimistic: embedding+cosine clustering swap is ~1 day, but the teacher-fill leg needs budget gating, the eval-regate safety loop, error handling, provenance, and tests. Impact "medium" is fair and honestly downgraded — the finding itself flags that auto-synthesized answers must pass the EVALUATE gate before entering TRAIN (an unstated hard prerequisite, not optional, or the loop self-poisons). Additional caveat: src/embedding.js is itself a hash-ngram stand-in ("Swap for a real embedding model when scaling"), so embedding clustering improves on the hash bucket but is not true semantic clustering until a real embedder lands. Users running the autopilot would care because failure remediation is the leg that currently cannot run unattended.

findings.title: Meta-trainer is an explicitly-documented toy stump-GBM -- promote to a real worker-shell GBM (XGBoost/LightGBM) for the learned predictor path
findings.technique: Production gradient boosting (XGBoost/LightGBM) via worker shell, replacing depth-1 stump ensemble
findings.source: Chen & Guestrin 'XGBoost' KDD 2016; the module's own header naming apps/trainer/meta_xgb.py as the intended production path
findings.current_state: src/kolm-meta-trainer.js is N_TREES=50 depth-1 stumps, no column/row subsampling, no regularization; header states 'production-scale meta-distillation should move to apps/trainer/meta_xgb.py via a worker shell.' confidence is heuristic n/1000, not a posterior. quality-predictor's learned path defers to this GBM once n_rows>=1000, so the learned predictor inherits stump-level accuracy and an uncalibrated confidence.
