findings.proposed_change: Implement the already-named apps/trainer/meta_xgb.py worker shell (same W786 worker-shell pattern as the multimodal/redact workers): train a real depth-3+ regularized GBM on the same rows.jsonl, write back the model + a held-out RMSE so confidence becomes data-derived. quality-predictor's learned path calls the worker when available, falls back to the JS stump GBM when not -- zero change to META_FEATURES. Pairs with the conformal-interval finding (GBM residuals feed the calibration split).
findings.rationale: Documented tech-debt with a named target path, so execution not discovery. Lower priority than the statistical-core fixes because at current data volumes the stump GBM is rarely even reached (n>=1000 rows), but it is the right home for the conformal residual machinery and removes the 'TOY' caveat that undercuts every learned prediction the autopilot makes.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED, NOT REFUTED. The finding's premise is accurate against actual code. src/kolm-meta-trainer.js is exactly what's claimed: a depth-1 stump GBM (N_TREES=50, LR=0.1, max_depth=1, no row/column subsampling, no regularization) whose own header states 'production-scale meta-distillation should move to apps/trainer/meta_xgb.py via a worker shell'; confidence is the heuristic n/1000 (line 444), not a posterior. src/quality-predictor.js's learned path (_predictLearned, lines 339-378) does defer to this stump GBM once nTrain >= MIN_ROWS_FOR_META(1000), inheriting stump accuracy and that uncalibrated confidence.

NOT already present: apps/trainer/meta_xgb.py does NOT exist anywhere on disk (confirmed by glob + find). The only 'meta_xgb' hits are comment references in kolm-meta-trainer.js, quality-predictor.js, cli/kolm.js, and public/docs/cli/meta.html. xgboost/lightgbm appear in NO requirements/toml/cfg. So this is genuine unbuilt work, not a shipped feature mis-flagged as missing. The W786/W464 worker-shell pattern the finding wants to reuse IS real and proven (workers/multimodal-redact-audio/redact-audio.mjs: heavy Python deps isolated, Node thin shell, spawn external cmd, honesty-on-missing-tool envelope, doctor mode) - so the proposed approach is sound and idiomatic for this repo.

CAVEATS on the finding's own numbers:
1) Effort 5 days is optimistic. A real end-to-end build needs: the Python meta_xgb.py (train + held-out RMSE + serialized model), a new Node worker shell (spawn/doctor/honesty-on-missing/W786 envelope), wiring _predictLearned to prefer-worker-then-fall-back, AND a model-handoff contract (the JS infer path currently reads its OWN JSON model format; an XGBoost artifact is a different thing - the worker must either serve predictions or emit a JS-replayable model). The audit (autopilot.audit.json gap) also notes the ENTIRE learned path has zero automated test coverage today, so tests are net-new too. 5 days covers a happy-path shell, not the full tested path; realistic is higher.
2) Impact 'medium' is defensible but the practical product impact is LOW at current data volumes - the finding itself concedes 'the stump GBM is rarely even reached (n>=1000 rows)', and there are no rows.jsonl files anywhere, so no run has ever crossed the threshold even in tests. Every real autopilot decision today runs through the heuristic path (confidence capped LOW <=0.45), not the GBM. The audit explicitly classifies this as 'acceptable documented tech-debt, not a bug.' So the upgrade is correct and well-targeted (it's the right home for conformal residuals and removes the 'TOY' caveat) but is not urgent.
3) Unstated dependency: introduces a NEW heavy Python ML dependency the repo does not currently carry, plus the Python<->JS model artifact contract noted above.

Net: real upgrade, correctly framed as documented tech-debt with a named target path, lower-than-headline practical impact, effort underestimated.

findings.title: Deploy guardrail trusts a single eval_pass boolean and one drift snapshot -- add a multi-look canary / shadow-eval window before EXECUTE
findings.technique: Champion/challenger shadow deployment + canary monitoring before promotion
findings.source: DataRobot/Snowflake/H2O champion-challenger MLOps docs (2024); Wallaroo shadow-deployment guidance; 'Multi-Criteria Automated MLOps Pipeline in Response to Data Distribution Shifts' arXiv:2512.11541
findings.current_state: src/autopilot-lifecycle.js _evaluateDeploy auto path requires promote + regressions<=max + eval_pass===true (a caller-supplied boolean, fail-closed) + one drift-green snapshot + 48h grace. The eval is a single point-in-time pass flag and drift is checked once; there is no shadow/canary window where the candidate serves a slice of live traffic and is monitored before becoming champion. Audit notes this highest-blast-radius path has NO automated test.
findings.proposed_change: Insert a canary/shadow stage between DEPLOY_PROPOSED and DEPLOY_EXECUTED: route a small live-traffic slice to the candidate (reuse the W777/W822 router already present) for a bounded window, record kscore/fallback-rate outcomes, and require the anytime-valid gate (finding #1) to confirm non-regression on LIVE traffic before EXECUTE. The 48h grace becomes a real monitored canary, not a passive timer. Makes the highest-blast-radius path defend on observed live behavior, not a one-shot assertion.
findings.rationale: The propose-only + grace guardrail is already strong, but champion/challenger + canary is the industry-standard final gate and kolm has all the parts (router for the split, drift detector, the proposed anytime-valid gate) -- it just doesn't compose them into a monitored canary. Highest blast radius per the audit, and it has no automated test; making it depend on observed live outcomes is the most defensible upgrade. Ranked below the core stats fixes because it depends on finding #1 landing first.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED in code, NOT refuted. src/autopilot-lifecycle.js _evaluateDeploy (lines 140-291) confirms the finding's current_state exactly: the --auto EXECUTE path gates on (3) eval_pass===true — a single caller-supplied boolean, fail-closed at line 202 — and (4) _isDriftRed checked ONCE (line 206, one point-in-time snapshot), then a 48h passive timer grace, then jumps straight to compareAndDecide({auto_promote:true}). There is NO canary/shadow window: grep for canary|shadow|champion|challenger|CanaryDeploy|ab-router|stat-sig|welchT|readSamples in autopilot-lifecycle.js returns zero matches. The candidate never serves a live-traffic slice that is monitored before becoming champion.

already_partially_present:true is correct and the pieces DO exist separately: src/ab-router.js (W777 deterministic traffic split + outcome recording, readSamples), src/stat-sig.js (W778 Welch t-test gate() that reads ab.readSamples), and the W813 drift gate (_isDriftRed). They are simply not composed into the autopilot promote path.

Note: src/deploy-canary.js EXISTS but is a DIFFERENT thing — W888-D CanaryDeploy operates on physical fleet DEVICES via a DeployPipeline, watching error_rate/latency_p95 from a metricsProvider; it is not imported by autopilot-lifecycle.js and does not monitor model kscore/fallback-rate for champion/challenger model promotion. So it does not satisfy this finding.

The audit's no-test claim is accurate: no test file references tickAutopilotFull/_evaluateDeploy/autopilot-lifecycle/objectToDeploy (searched tests/ and test/), yet the path is production-reachable via router.js (lines 24023-24062). This is genuinely the highest-blast-radius autonomous path and is untested.

Caveats on the estimate/impact: (1) effort 6 days is optimistic. The module explicitly states 'no long-lived process — cron re-evaluates,' so a monitored canary cannot be a simple sleep; it needs a persisted canary-state machine that starts a slice, accumulates live samples across stateless ticks, and decides — plus real router wiring to actually route a candidate slice, plus the anytime-valid gate. More realistically 8-12 days. (2) Impact is overstated as 'high'; the path is PROPOSE-ONLY by default, requires explicit --auto opt-in, and already has a 48h human-veto (objectToDeploy) window, so blast radius in practice is gated — medium-to-high is fairer. (3) Hard unstated-but-acknowledged dependency: finding #1 (anytime-valid sequential gate) must land first, otherwise a multi-look canary using a fixed-alpha Welch test (current stat-sig.js gate) inflates false-promote rate via repeated peeking.

audit.summary: The CLI is mature and substantially exceeds the 2026-05-14 baseline (28 verbs -> 150+ in the completion registry). Most of the top-10 SOTA gaps from that audit are now CLOSED: EXIT.* constants defined + used at 100+ call sites; shell completion for bash/zsh/fish; `kolm upgrade` (check-only) + `kolm update` (self-install) with --json/EXAMPLES; --json on doctor/what/next/whoami/org and many more; error-with-next-step wrapper (kolm-ux.js errorWithNextStep + kolm.js withErrorContext); Levenshtein suggestVerb 'did you mean' wired into dispatch default; rich per-command help (146 maybeHelp sites, 57 EXAMPLES sections, exit-code docs). Accessibility contract (NO_COLOR/NO_UNICODE/--plain/--no-color/TTY/KOLM_NO_PROGRESS) implemented and honored. Primary `kolm tui` is a 14-view alt-screen TUI (cmdTui) with vim-style nav and a non-TTY guard. Ran 71 CLI/TUI tests across 8 suites: all pass. Two real gaps remain: (1) unknown-FLAG rejection (rejectUnknownFlags) is wired into only ONE verb (cmdCompute), so bogus flags are silently ignored on ~150 commands (gap #10 mostly open); (2) cli/kolm-tui.mjs is orphaned legacy wave-122 code — not reachable from `kolm tui`, with a mock/stub inference path and an advertised-but-unwired `--cloud` flag.

checks.name: All three CLI files syntax-check clean (node --check)
checks.evidence: node --check passed for all three (KOLM.JS/TUI/UX SYNTAX OK).

checks.name: EXIT code constants defined + used (gap #4)
checks.evidence: EXIT={OK:0,BAD_ARGS:1,GATE_FAIL:2,MISSING_PREREQ:3,EXECUTION:4,NOT_FOUND:5,USAGE:64}; runtime: unknown-verb exit=1, run-missing-file exit=5, completion-bad-shell exit=1.

checks.name: Shell completion bash/zsh/fish (gap #6)
checks.evidence: completion bash emits _kolm_complete() with per-verb subcommand cases; zsh #compdef kolm; fish complete -c kolm; bad shell errors exit=1.

checks.name: kolm upgrade + kolm update (gap #9)
checks.evidence: upgrade --help documents [--json] {current,latest,status} with EXAMPLES, 5s timeout against canonical install source; update has --dry-run.

checks.name: --json on core verbs (gap #1)
checks.evidence: doctor/what/next/whoami all emit JSON; next --json -> {ok,recommendations:[{action,command,why,rank}]}.

checks.name: Error-with-next-step + contextual hints (gap #7)
checks.evidence: errorWithNextStep prints Run:/See:/Fix:/Try: hints to stderr w/ exitCode; withErrorContext prefixes [kolm <verb>] + errorHint() for 401->login, ENOENT .kolm->inspect.

checks.name: Unknown-verb suggestion (Levenshtein) (gap #10 partial)
checks.evidence: main() default calls suggestVerb; 'kolm complie'->compile; wave910-cli-fuzzy F.1-F.7 pass; exits EXIT.BAD_ARGS.

checks.name: Accessibility contract
checks.evidence: supportsColor false for NO_COLOR/dumb/--no-color/non-TTY; ASCII fallbacks; main() fails fast on --plain --json (kolm.js:46602).

checks.name: Per-command help USAGE/OPTIONS/EXAMPLES + exit-code docs (gap #5)
checks.evidence: 146 maybeHelp sites, 57 EXAMPLES sections; compile --help shows OPTIONS(cloud/spec) + 'EXIT CODES: 0=pass, 2=gate fail'.

checks.name: Primary kolm tui — 14-view alt-screen (cmdTui)
checks.evidence: TUI_VIEWS: live-calls/artifacts/compile/spend/privacy/opportunities/datasets/builds/bakeoffs/devices/etc; vim modes; compile wizard; wave910-tui-smoke F4.1-F4.7 pass.

checks.name: TUI non-TTY guard (no CI hang)
checks.evidence: if(!process.stdout.isTTY){print hint; exit(MISSING_PREREQ)} with non-interactive alternatives.

checks.name: kolm chat REPL launches + :exit clean
checks.evidence: echo :exit | kolm chat -> header printed, exit 0; wave144-tui-chat covers it.

checks.name: kolm doctor diagnostics (honest, actionable)
checks.evidence: 40+ checks; each warn carries 'needed for:/install:'; doctor --fix --dry-run previews; wave298+wave888a tests.

checks.name: NL DX surface (ask/do/what/next/nl)
checks.evidence: do --dry-run -> 'would run: kolm build, confidence 0.95'; ask gives harness disambiguation; --no-assistant opts out; wave888p tests pass.

checks.name: CLI/TUI test coverage
checks.evidence: wave888g+wave202+wave910-fuzzy+wave910-tui = 46/46; wave888p+888i+487+918 = 25/25; dedicated completion-install/doctor-loop/nl-routing/startup-perf/altscreen suites.

checks.name: Unknown-FLAG rejection wired into only 1 verb (gap #10 still mostly OPEN)
checks.evidence: rejectUnknownFlags comment: 'Wire into new commands as we go — proven first in cmdCompute'; grep shows ONE caller. `kolm whoami --bogus-flag` runs clean. (Separate one-off check exists only for hf modelcard at :54553.)

checks.name: --dry-run ignored on bare-task cloud compile (minor)
checks.evidence: --dry-run honored on --data/--describe/--target + install/doctor/update/improve/do, but `compile "<task>" --dry-run` (no --data) fell through and POSTed /v1/compile (failed no_seeds_provided) instead of printing a plan.

checks.name: cli/kolm-tui.mjs orphaned legacy w/ stub inference + unwired --cloud
checks.evidence: Not reachable from `kolm tui` (routes to cmdTui); only refs are its entry guard + wave-122 plan + orphan-scan data. :run uses mockInfer() ('(local mock) ... Plug in the cloud with kolm tui --cloud') but runTui never reads opts.cloud; 'Wave 123 will wire it to /v1/wrap/verified' TODO never delivered.

checks.name: CLI cold-start performance
checks.evidence: version ~600-800ms cold incl node boot; guard test wave888i-cli-startup-perf passes.

checks.name: ExperimentalWarning noise on some verbs (cosmetic)
checks.evidence: doctor --json + do --dry-run print '(node:NNN) ExperimentalWarning: SQLite/WASI' to stderr on Node 24; stdout JSON uncorrupted, but noisy.

gaps: Unknown-FLAG rejection (rejectUnknownFlags, cli/kolm.js:29942) is wired into only 1 of ~150 verbs (cmdCompute, :31632). 2026-05-14 gap #10 remains mostly open: bogus/typoed flags are silently ignored on nearly every command (verified `kolm whoami --bogus-flag` runs clean). A pre-dispatch flag validator or per-verb allowlists would close it.

`kolm compile "<task>" --dry-run` with no --data ignores --dry-run and runs the real cloud compile (observed POST /v1/compile failing no_seeds_provided instead of printing a plan). Dry-run is only honored on the data-ingestion/--target paths. Minor inconsistency vs the documented --dry-run contract.

cli/kolm-tui.mjs is orphaned legacy (wave 122) dead code: not reachable from `kolm tui`, contains a mock/stub inference path, and advertises `kolm tui --cloud` -> /v1/wrap/verified that was never wired (the 'Wave 123 will wire it' TODO at line 265 is unfulfilled). Recommend deleting it or documenting it as a standalone `node cli/kolm-tui.mjs` toy. It is knowingly carried (kolm-ux.js header lists the trio as exempt CLI shims).
ExperimentalWarning lines (SQLite/WASI) leak to stderr on Node 24 for some verbs — cosmetic, does not break --json on stdout, but noisy; could be suppressed with --no-warnings.

cli/kolm.js is a 54,645-line / 2.4MB single file — maintainability/tech-debt concern (verb handlers, help registry, completion registry, and two embedded flag() helpers all in one module); not a behavioral defect.

findings.title: Global error catch is text-only and ignores --json (dual-consumer gap)
findings.technique: Dual-consumer structured error envelope: same binary emits lush human text OR a machine-readable error object (RFC 9457-style {type/code, title, detail, status, suggested_fix, code_actions[]}) selected by --json / TTY detection.
findings.source: https://zircote.com/blog/2026/04/cli-error-messages-are-a-dual-consumer-problem/ ; AWS CLI structured error format https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/v1/userguide/cli-usage-error-format.html
findings.current_state: main()'s global catch (cli/kolm.js ~47525) always does console.error('error:', e.message) + process.exit, regardless of --json. jsonErrorEnvelope() exists (cli/kolm.js:40876, code/exit/hint/next fields) but is wired into only ~6 call sites (grep count 6). So `kolm <anything> --json` that throws emits unparseable text on stderr, breaking agents/scripts mid-pipeline.
findings.proposed_change: Detect --json (and KOLM_OUTPUT=json) in main(); in the global catch emit jsonErrorEnvelope({error,code,exit,hint,next}) to stdout instead of plain stderr text when JSON mode is on. Standardize the envelope on the KOLM_E_* code taxonomy already started (KOLM_E_ARTIFACT_NOT_FOUND etc.) and add a stable suggested_fix/next[] array on every throw path via withErrorContext.
findings.rationale: Agents and CI consuming --json are the fastest-growing CLI audience (per 'CLI is the new MCP'). A pipeline that gets clean JSON on success but raw text on failure cannot branch on errors. The fix is centralized in one catch block plus reusing an existing helper, so the blast radius is tiny relative to the reliability payoff.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, could not refute the core claim. The global catch in main() at cli/kolm.js:47524-47530 does `console.error('error:', e.message)` + process.exit unconditionally, with no --json branch. A SECOND catch the finding missed — the NL pre-dispatch catch at cli/kolm.js:46636-46639 — has the identical plain-text behavior. main() already knows about --json (it checks process.argv.includes('--json') for the mutual-exclusion guard at line 46602), so honoring it in the catch is trivial. jsonErrorEnvelope() exists at cli/kolm.js:40876 with fields {ok,error,code,exit,hint,next}. Finding said wired into ~6 sites; actual grep count is 5 (lines 40903, 41034, 41051, 41134, 41151), all in the intent dispatcher (do/explain/eval). Critically, AGENT_GUIDE.md line 57 documents this as a STABLE CONTRACT for agents: "Every verb that supports --json returns { ok: true, ... } on success or this exact shape on failure." The runtime does not honor that promise on the fallback path, so the doc over-promises.

alreadyPresent=false: the envelope helper and the documented contract exist, but the GLOBAL wiring the finding asks for is absent. Confirmed by tests/wave353-agent-guide.test.js — every assertion only checks the GUIDE text mentions the fields, none verify the runtime emits JSON on error. No KOLM_OUTPUT env var exists anywhere in the repo (the proposed KOLM_OUTPUT=json detection is net-new, not "already started").

CAVEATS that temper the finding:
1. Impact "critical" is overblown -> adjusted to medium. High-traffic --json verbs (e.g. cmdWhoami at 5641) ALREADY self-handle their EXPECTED error paths with structured JSON (catching API failures internally, emitting {logged_in:false, error, error_code, error_type, hint}). So the documented common failure modes mostly already return JSON. The gap bites on UNEXPECTED throws that bubble to the global catch. Exit codes ARE honored on every path (EXIT.* preserved via withErrorContext), so an agent can still branch on exit status even when stderr is text. That meaningfully reduces "cannot branch on errors" severity.
2. The KOLM_E_* taxonomy is real but thin (~8 codes: KOLM_E_UNKNOWN, KOLM_E_DO_NO_INPUT, KOLM_E_ARTIFACT_NOT_FOUND, KOLM_E_LOAD_FAILED, KOLM_E_EVAL_FAILED, KOLM_E_BUNDLE_RUN_FAILED, KOLM_E_RUN_FAILED, KOLM_E_DUPLICATE_ID, KOLM_E_UNKNOWN_DEVICE) — scattered, no central registry. Standardizing it across "every throw path via withErrorContext" plus the existing ad-hoc per-verb shapes (error_code/error_type) is a contract-touching refactor, not a one-block change.
3. RFC 9457 fields (type/status/suggested_fix/code_actions[]) are NOT present; current envelope is {ok,error,code,exit,hint,next}. The richer envelope the finding proposes is partly net-new.
4. Effort: the narrow centralized fix (detect --json in BOTH catch blocks, emit jsonErrorEnvelope) is ~0.5 day. But the finding's stated full scope (standardize taxonomy on every throw, add suggested_fix/next[] everywhere, RFC 9457 shape) is realistically 2.5-3 days and risks divergence from the per-verb shapes already in the wild. 2 days is slightly optimistic for the full scope; adjusted to 2.5.

Net: a genuine dual-consumer gap that contradicts a documented stable contract; worth fixing, but medium (not critical) since exit codes and the popular-verb expected-error paths already give agents something to branch on.

findings.title: Shell completion is fully static; no dynamic completion of artifact IDs, namespaces, model/target names
findings.technique: Runtime/dynamic completion via a hidden `__complete` subcommand (the carapace/Cobra/clap_complete pattern): the shell function shells back into the binary, which returns live candidates (artifact IDs, namespaces, group slugs, --target enum values, recipe names). Optionally publish a Fig-style declarative spec.
findings.source: https://carapace-sh.github.io/carapace-bin/ ; https://github.com/withfig/autocomplete ; https://cli.github.com/manual/gh_extension (gh ships dynamic completion of repos/PRs)
findings.current_state: emitBashCompletion/emitZshCompletion/fish (cli/kolm.js:33059+) generate purely static lists from hardcoded COMPLETION_VERBS / COMPLETION_SUBS. Verb + first-subcommand only; no value completion for artifact paths, namespaces, --target gguf-q4km|mlx|onnx enums, group slugs, or recipe names. Bash branch only handles COMP_CWORD 1 and 2.
findings.proposed_change: Add a hidden `kolm __complete <line>` that prints newline-delimited candidates; rewrite the bash/zsh/fish emitters to delegate to it for arg positions (after inspect/run/fix complete *.kolm in ~/.kolm/artifacts and cwd; after --target complete the export enum; after --namespace complete known namespaces). Keep current static fallback when offline.
findings.rationale: Static verb completion is table-stakes; the DX leap competitors made (gh, carapace-backed tools) is completing the values a user actually types — artifact IDs and enum flags. kolm already has all the data (artifacts dir, COMPLETION_SUBS, export target list) so a thin __complete shim unlocks it without a new dependency.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED REAL, NOT REFUTED. The current_state is fully accurate. emitBashCompletion (cli/kolm.js:33059), emitZshCompletion (:33088), and emitFishCompletion (:33121) all generate purely static scripts from the hardcoded COMPLETION_VERBS / COMPLETION_SUBS tables. Bash only handles COMP_CWORD 1 and 2 (verbatim: `if [ "$COMP_CWORD" -eq 1 ]` / `-eq 2`); zsh only CURRENT 2 and 3; fish only top-level verbs + second-level subcommands. cmdCompletion (:34817) and the dispatch table (:41295) have NO __complete entry. A repo-wide search for __complete / dynamicComplet / completeCandidates returns ZERO hits in product code (the only match is the audit finding file itself). So there is no dynamic value completion of artifact IDs, namespaces, --target enums, group slugs, or recipe names. NOT already implemented.

Caveats on the finding's own claims: (1) Effort is mildly optimistic. The finding asserts "kolm already has all the data," but that is only true for the verb/sub tables. The export --target enum (gguf | onnx | safetensors | coreml | mlx | executorch | tensorrt and quant tiers q4_k_m|q5_k_m|q8_0|f16|int4|int8) lives scattered in help-text strings (cli/kolm.js:996-1021, 1471-1482), NOT a reusable constant — it must be extracted/centralized first. Combined with COMP_LINE/COMP_POINT parsing in bash, $words/$CURRENT handoff in zsh, fish commandline wiring, an offline static fallback, and tests across three shells, 3.5 days is more realistic than 3. (2) Impact is overblown as "high." kolm is a single-binary CLI, not gh/kubectl with thousands of users; artifacts live in one known dir and users typically juggle a handful. Dynamic value completion is a genuine DX polish item but not a value-unlocking gap or competitive blocker — medium. (3) Unstated dependencies: a __complete shim runs on every TAB, so kolm's CLI cold-start cost is a hard latency constraint; and the existing static-emit/install path is test-locked (tests/wave308-kolm-completion-install.test.js asserts idempotent eval-line install for bash/zsh), so any rewrite must preserve that contract.

findings.title: rejectUnknownFlags wired into only 1 of ~150 verbs — bogus flags silently ignored
findings.technique: Per-command allowed-flag validation with 'did you mean' on the rejected flag (rustc/clap unknown-argument diagnostics). Pair each verb with its allow-list, reject unknowns with EXIT.BAD_ARGS.
findings.source: https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/diagnostics.html (suggestions for unknown flags); clap unknown-argument errors
findings.current_state: rejectUnknownFlags (cli/kolm.js:29942) is sound (already does prefix-based 'did you mean') but has exactly ONE caller — cmdCompute (cli/kolm.js:31632). Its own comment says 'Wire into new commands as we go — proven first in cmdCompute'. `kolm whoami --bogus-flag` runs clean; a typo'd flag like --jsno is silently dropped on ~150 commands, so users get wrong behavior with no signal.
findings.proposed_change: Define a per-verb ALLOWED_FLAGS map (or attach .allowedFlags to each cmd in the dispatch table) and call rejectUnknownFlags centrally from withErrorContext / _dispatchVerb. Start with high-traffic verbs (compile, run, distill, doctor, export, deploy, whoami, config) where a dropped flag is most damaging; global flags (--json/--no-color/--plain/--help/-h) are always allowed.
findings.rationale: Silently ignoring an unknown flag is the single most common CLI footgun — the user thinks --dry-run is honored, it isn't, and they ship. The helper and the suggestion logic already exist; this is purely a wiring plus allow-list-curation task, not new infrastructure. Centralizing it in the dispatcher avoids touching 150 handlers.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, not already implemented. rejectUnknownFlags (cli/kolm.js:29942) is sound and does prefix-based 'did you mean', but has exactly ONE caller — cmdCompute at line 31632, whose own comment says it's 'proven first in cmdCompute' (audit gap #10). Confirmed by execution: `kolm whoami --bogus-flag` exits 0 silently; `kolm doctor --jsno` exits 0 silently (typo'd --json dropped); only `kolm compute pick --budjet 10` rejects correctly with 'did you mean --budget?'. The ~144-entry dispatch table (_dispatchVerb, line 41252) routes everything through withErrorContext (line 142) with zero flag validation. Two scattered partials don't cover the gap: `kolm hf modelcard` has its own ad-hoc rejection (line 54553) and cmdNl intentionally skips unknown flags (line 26637, by design for NL input).

EFFORT UNDERESTIMATED (3 -> 5 days): The proposal's premise that this is 'purely wiring + allow-list curation, centralized in the dispatcher' is the weak point. There is NO central flag registry — ~1076 inline flag reads (args.includes/flag()/pickFlag()) scattered across ~144 verbs and ~992 sub-verb branches. Flags differ per sub-verb (e.g. `group create` vs `group list`), so the _dispatchVerb choke point — which only receives (verb,args) — cannot validate without hand-curated per-SUB-verb allow-lists. 3 days covers maybe the 8 named high-traffic verbs (each of which still has sub-verbs); a real sweep is larger.

IMPACT SLIGHTLY OVERBLOWN (high -> medium): 'single most common CLI footgun' is rhetoric. The highest-damage verbs (compile/export/run) already fail on missing required positional args, catching some typos. But a dropped --dry-run/--json/--force on an otherwise-valid invocation genuinely yields wrong behavior with no signal — a legitimate medium-impact correctness/DX issue. Users would care for destructive/boolean flags specifically.

findings.title: Orphaned legacy cli/kolm-tui.mjs with stub mock-inference and an unwired --cloud flag
findings.technique: Dead-code removal / honest-surface hygiene: no advertised flag should be unreachable, and no shipped path should silently return mock output.
findings.source: Internal: cli/kolm-tui.mjs:268-292 (mockInfer), :540-547 (entry guard), :510 (:run). gh/Stripe CLIs ship one TUI surface, not a shadow one.
findings.current_state: `kolm tui` routes to cmdTui (the real 14-view alt-screen TUI at cli/kolm.js:39782). cli/kolm-tui.mjs (547 lines) is wave-122 legacy: runTui() never reads opts.cloud, so the `kolm tui --cloud` plug advertised inside mockInfer()'s stub string is dead. :run returns deterministic mock text, never /v1/wrap/verified.
findings.proposed_change: Either (a) delete cli/kolm-tui.mjs and its orphan-scan references, or (b) if any artifact-drag UX is worth keeping, fold it into cmdTui and wire :run to the real inference path. Remove the unwired --cloud advertisement either way. Document the decision so the orphan-scan stops flagging it.
findings.rationale: An advertised-but-unwired flag and a mock path that masquerades as inference are exactly the kind of dishonest surface the project's own audits flag. Cheap to resolve, removes a maintenance trap, and tightens the 'every advertised path works' contract.

verdict.reason: Verified against actual code — every structural claim holds. cli/kolm-tui.mjs exists (547 lines). `kolm tui` dispatches to cmdTui (cli/kolm.js:46788 case 'tui' -> cmdTui; map at :41265; defined at :39782, the alt-screen TUI). The .mjs's runTui() is never imported — no static or dynamic import of kolm-tui anywhere in src/ or cli/ (only self-references plus audit/ledger/script files). mockInfer (kolm-tui.mjs:268-292) returns deterministic mock text and :run (:510-513) calls runPrompt->mockInfer, never /v1/wrap/verified. The `kolm tui --cloud` plug advertised at :288 is dead: the only real --cloud flag belongs to `kolm compile` (alias for --rent, cli/kolm.js:7924-7937); cmdTui does not parse --cloud. package.json bin maps ONLY kolm->cli/kolm.js — so docs/reference/testing-policy.md:121's claim that the .mjs is "invoked by bin/ entries" is itself inaccurate; the file is genuinely orphaned. Zero test coverage (tests/wave144-tui-chat.test.js tests src/tui-chat.js's runTuiChat, a different function). NOT already implemented: the orphan is knowingly carried (org-audit special-cases it as planned_split:next-major "TUI rewrite v1.1"; testing-policy lists it as a CLI shim), but the dead --cloud advertisement and the mock-as-inference :run path still exist verbatim — the proposed cleanup has not been done. CAVEATS on the estimates: impact is overstated. It is internal dead-code hygiene in a file with no bin entry, invisible to real `kolm` CLI users (anyone running `kolm tui` hits cmdTui, never this code) — low, not medium. Effort is also overstated: the delete path (remove file + ~3-4 references in codebase-file-ledger.json, w890-1-organization-audit.cjs special-case, testing-policy line, w920 workflow entry) is ~0.25 day, not 1 day; only the fold-into-cmdTui option would approach a day.

findings.title: No multi-step plan execution with per-step confirmation (Warp Agent-Mode pattern)
findings.technique: Agent-mode: decompose a natural-language goal into an ordered plan of CLI steps, narrate each, run with per-step approve/edit/skip, read output, and self-correct — bounded by an autonomy/permission policy (destructive steps always confirm).
findings.source: https://www.warp.dev/blog/agent-mode ; https://docs.warp.dev/agent-platform/capabilities/full-terminal-use/ ; GitHub Copilot CLI GA autopilot https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/04/github-copilot-cli-ga/
findings.current_state: cmdDo (cli/kolm.js:40895) classifies one intent -> ONE verb via _dispatchVerb; --dry-run prints a single 'would run: kolm <verb>' line. cmdAssistantNlOneShot (12783) answers a question and surfaces backticked commands but does not chain/execute a plan. There is no plan object, no per-step confirm, no read-output-and-iterate loop.
findings.proposed_change: Add `kolm do --plan` (and TUI equivalent) that returns an ordered steps[] (each {command, why, destructive:bool}), prints the plan, then executes with [y/e/s/q] per step (auto-confirm read-only, always-prompt destructive). Reuse classifyIntent for each step; gate behind --yes for non-interactive and respect KOLM_NO_INTERACTIVE. Keep single-shot cmdDo as the default.
findings.rationale: Single-intent NL routing is solid but the 2026 frontier (Warp, Copilot CLI Autopilot) is bounded multi-step orchestration with confirmation. kolm already has classifyIntent plus a verb dispatcher, so the missing piece is the plan/loop/confirm scaffold, not the brains. Scoped to opt-in so it never erodes the deterministic single-shot path.

verdict.reason:
Finding is REAL and not refuted. Verified the current_state is accurate against actual code. cmdDo (cli/kolm.js:40895) classifies one phrase -> ONE verb via _dispatchVerb; --dry-run prints a single 'would run' line. cmdAssistantNlOneShot (12783) answers a question, surfaces backticked commands, offers only the FIRST valid command for y/N/e confirm — no chaining. cmdChat (29457) + chatPlanAction (27991) execute exactly ONE command per user turn ({action, argv, label, expensive}), with confirm gated on 'expensive' — per-turn single-command, not a decomposed ordered plan. There is no generic NL-goal -> ordered steps[] -> per-step approve/edit/skip -> read-output-and-self-correct scaffold anywhere.

NOT already present, but more partial scaffolding exists than the finding credits (so already_partially_present:true is correct): (a) src/intent.js WORKFLOWS + expandToWorkflow (lines 2004-2267) already encode ORDERED static recipes (cmd+why per step) for terminal verbs, surfaced for DISPLAY in /v1/free/chat (router.js:9560,9987) — but no destructive flag, no execution, no confirm loop; (b) `kolm quickstart` already has a per-step y/s/q confirm-or-skip loop with auto-skip of done steps + KOLM_NO_INTERACTIVE + --yes (HELP text lines 669-680) — but it walks a HARD-CODED 7/6-step wizard, not an NL-decomposed plan; (c) `kolm chat` already prints exact argv + confirms destructive/expensive commands + chatRunAction; (d) `compile --auto`/autopilot (7690,16681) is a guarded self-correcting loop (cost plan->simulate->deploy guardrail w/ 5-condition+48h grace) — but it is specific to the distill/compile data-engine, not arbitrary CLI steps. The genuinely missing piece is exactly what the proposed_change names: the planner (classifyIntent today is one-phrase->one-verb), the execute/confirm/iterate loop, and a per-step destructive classifier.

Effort 5d is roughly realistic — slightly optimistic. There is reusable scaffolding (quickstart's step loop, chat's confirm + chatRunAction, WORKFLOWS recipes, expensive-gating, KOLM_NO_INTERACTIVE/--yes conventions), which lowers cost, but the hard parts (NL goal -> ordered DEPENDENT steps where step 2 consumes step 1's output e.g. dataset_id; reliable read-output-and-self-correct since not all verbs emit machine-parseable JSON; a trustworthy per-verb destructive map) are non-trivial. Adjusted to ~6 days.

Impact 'high' is overblown. This is an opt-in CLI agent-mode tracking the 2026 frontier (Warp/Copilot CLI). kolm's actual users (ML/ops engineers + AI agents calling the CLI) are served first by the deterministic, auditable single-shot path that is the product's core differentiation; a bounded multi-step orchestrator is a credible-but-secondary nice-to-have and risks eroding the determinism story if built sloppily. Medium is more accurate. Unstated prerequisites: structured/JSON output from every step verb for the self-correct loop, a verb->destructiveness map (partially present via chat 'expensive' + server confirm_required), and cross-step argument dependency resolution.

findings.title: No passive update-notifier (only explicit `kolm upgrade`)
findings.technique: Background, debounced version check in an unref'd child with cached result plus boxed nag on next run; honor NO_UPDATE_NOTIFIER / --no-update-notifier and never block startup.
findings.source: https://www.npmjs.com/package/update-notifier ; gh checks for new versions once per 24h and prints an upgrade notice (https://docs.github.com/en/github-cli/github-cli/using-github-cli-extensions)
findings.current_state: cmdUpgrade (cli/kolm.js:34942, check-only) and cmdUpdate (38581, self-install) exist but are only invoked explicitly or via the `do` planner. main() startup never proactively checks for a newer version, so users on a stale CLI never learn unless they run `kolm upgrade`.
findings.proposed_change: Add a once-per-24h cached check (timestamp in ~/.kolm) that, on the next run, prints a single boxed 'kolm X.Y is available — run kolm update' to stderr. Run the fetch in an unref'd path so it never adds latency; gate on TTY plus NO_UPDATE_NOTIFIER plus KOLM_NO_PROGRESS so CI/scripts/pipes stay silent. Reuse cmdUpgrade's existing 5s-timeout fetch against the canonical install source.
findings.rationale: Stale CLIs are a top support cost; every mature CLI (gh, npm, wrangler) nags passively. kolm has the fetch plus version-compare logic already (cmdUpgrade); this just adds a cached, opt-out-respecting trigger at startup. Low risk because it must remain silent in non-TTY contexts the accessibility contract already detects.

verdict.reason: Could not refute. Verified absent in actual code: main() (C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/cli/kolm.js:46591) dispatches directly to the verb switch with no pre/post startup version check. fetchLatestNpmVersion (line 34907) is invoked ONLY by the explicit cmdUpgrade verb (line 34946); cmdUpdate (line 38581) is the explicit self-install verb. firstRunBannerIfNeeded (line 4377) only paints a setup banner when ~/.kolm/config.json is absent and does no version check. Grep across cli/kolm.js and src/ for update-notifier / NO_UPDATE_NOTIFIER / lastUpdateCheck / cached-timestamp logic = zero matches. The finding's claimed gating env vars NO_UPDATE_NOTIFIER and KOLM_NO_PROGRESS do not exist in the codebase (the a11y gates --plain/--no-color/NO_COLOR/TTY in termSupports DO exist, so opt-out plumbing is partially present, supporting already_partially_present:true). Strongest corroboration: the repo's own audit doc docs/audit-cli-2026-05-14.md:61 lists 'Auto-update check on first-run-per-day (~80)' as an un-built to-do, while only the explicit 'kolm upgrade' (item 9) was implemented. Effort of 1 day is realistic-to-generous since the hard primitives (timeout fetch, compareVersions, readPackageVersion) already exist and only a cached-timestamp trigger + boxed nag + TTY/env gate need adding in one file (~80-150 lines). Impact 'medium' is accurate, not overblown: kolm installs from a github source with no registry auto-update, so stale CLIs are plausible and a passive nag matches gh/npm/wrangler convention. Real, not present, low risk.

findings.title: Login is paste-a-key only; no browser/OAuth (PKCE) or device-code flow
findings.technique: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code + PKCE with a localhost callback (wrangler login), or device-code flow for headless/SSH boxes — short-lived access token plus refresh, optional --scopes.
findings.source: https://blog.cloudflare.com/wrangler-oauth/ (PKCE + localhost:8976 callback) ; Stripe/gh browser login
findings.current_state: cmdLogin (cli/kolm.js:5557) prints 'paste your API key from kolm.ai/signup'; the interactive picker's login item (28881) also says 'paste an existing ks_... key'. No `kolm login --web` that opens a browser and captures a callback, and no device-code path for SSH/remote shells.
findings.proposed_change: Add `kolm login --web` (spin a localhost callback, open browser to an authorize URL, exchange code w/ PKCE) with graceful fallback to the existing paste flow when no browser / headless; add `kolm login --device` printing a short code plus verification URL for SSH boxes. Keep paste-a-key as the zero-dependency default.
findings.rationale: Copy-paste keys are the highest-friction step in first-run and a frequent source of mis-paste/leak. Browser plus device flows are the competitor norm. Lower confidence/impact here because it requires backend authorize+token endpoints (src/router.js/auth.js), so it is a cross-surface effort, not a pure CLI change — sequence it after the cheaper CLI-only wins.

verdict.reason:
Could not refute — the gap is real. cmdLogin at C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/cli/kolm.js:5557 is genuinely paste-a-key-only ("kolm login - paste your API key from kolm.ai/signup", prompts for "API key (ks_...): "), and the interactive picker's login item at cli/kolm.js:28881 confirms it ("paste an existing ks_... key"). Grep for oauth|PKCE|device_code|--web|loopback-login|authorize across cli/kolm.js found ZERO CLI login flows; every --device match is hardware deploy-target related (deploy/test-device/install-device), and every loopback match is airgap-mode network policy. So `kolm login --web` and `kolm login --device` do NOT exist.

IMPORTANT nuance the finding got slightly wrong: it asserts already_partially_present:false and implies backend authorize/token endpoints must be built from scratch. In fact C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/oauth.js DOES implement a Google+GitHub browser OAuth flow (mountOAuth, /v1/oauth/:provider/start + /callback + /providers) plus findOrCreateTenantByEmail in src/auth.js. BUT that flow is web-only: it is a confidential-client flow (uses server-side client_secret, NOT PKCE — no code_challenge/code_verifier), and its callback sets a session COOKIE then 302-redirects to /dashboard (oauth.js:182-185). It never returns a ks_ key to a CLI loopback listener and has no device-code path. So it is not usable by the CLI as-is; the finding's CLI gap stands.

Effort 5 days: reasonable. Some backend exists to reuse (provider config, token exchange, findOrCreateTenantByEmail) and the CLI already has cross-platform browser-open (cli/kolm.js:6575 start/open/xdg-open), which lowers cost — but PKCE handshake, a CLI loopback callback that mints+returns a ks_ key (vs cookie), and a new device-code endpoint + polling loop are all genuinely new. 5 days is fair, not underestimated.

Impact medium: defensible but at the low end. Paste-a-key already works, anonymous/local-only verbs need no auth, and signup already auto-saves the key. Browser/device login is competitor-norm polish (wrangler/gh/stripe) and removes first-run friction for SSH boxes, but it is not blocking. Keep medium per the finding's own 0.6 confidence and correct sequencing note (do cheaper CLI-only wins first).

findings.title: Unknown-verb resolves the single closest match but never offers ranked candidates or prefix/abbrev matching
findings.technique: git/cargo-style ambiguity handling: when no exact verb matches, accept a unique prefix (e.g. `kolm comp` -> compile if unambiguous) and when several are close, list the top-N 'did you mean' candidates rather than guessing one.
findings.source: git 'did you mean' (lists candidates when >1 close) ; cargo unique-prefix subcommand resolution ; rustc suggestion lists https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/diagnostics.html
findings.current_state: suggestVerb (cli/kolm.js:29926) returns exactly ONE best match (threshold <=2 edits or len/3) and the default branch prints a single 'did you mean: kolm <guess>?'. No unique-prefix acceptance, no multi-candidate list when several verbs are equidistant (e.g. 'dep' near deploy/depend).
findings.proposed_change: Extend suggestVerb to return the top 2-3 candidates within the threshold and print them as a short list; add unique-prefix resolution (if exactly one verb startsWith(cmd), dispatch it) gated so it never overrides an exact NL route. Emit the candidate list in the JSON error envelope's next[] for agents.
findings.rationale: The Levenshtein single-guess is already good; the incremental DX wins (prefix acceptance, multi-candidate lists) are what git/cargo users expect and are a few lines on top of the existing function. Low impact because the current single guess is usually right, but it removes the occasional confidently-wrong redirect.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED, NOT REFUTABLE. The finding's current_state is precisely accurate against the actual code. suggestVerb (cli/kolm.js:29926-29940) returns exactly ONE best Levenshtein match (threshold = max(2, floor(len/3))) and returns null otherwise. Both top-level dispatch default branches print a single guess: the table-dispatch fallback at cli/kolm.js:41320-41330 ('unknown verb' + 'did you mean: kolm <guess> ?') and the switch-default at cli/kolm.js:47512-47521 ('unknown command' + same single guess). There is NO unique-prefix subcommand resolution anywhere (no verbs.filter(v=>v.startsWith(cmd)) dispatch path — every startsWith in the file is positional-arg filtering, not verb-prefix matching) and NO multi-candidate 'did you mean' listing. The verb table / COMPLETION_VERBS list (cli/kolm.js:32813+) holds 150+ verbs, so prefix collisions the finding describes (e.g. 'comp' near compile/compute, 'de' near device/devices/demo) are plausible. The finding self-flags already_partially_present:true (the Levenshtein single-guess), which is correct.

EFFORT (1 day): realistic, even slightly generous. Collecting top-N candidates within threshold + a unique-prefix check is a few dozen lines across the two dispatch sites; the repo's standing directive to skip new lock-in tests bounds test churn.

IMPACT (low): accurate and the finding rates it low itself, correctly reasoning the single guess is usually right and this just removes the occasional confidently-wrong single redirect.

CAVEATS: (a) Prefix resolution must be gated below the existing NL fallback at cli/kolm.js:47509 (looksLikeNaturalLanguage) so a short token like 'comp' isn't swallowed by 'ask' — the finding explicitly calls out this gating, good. (b) The proposed 'emit candidate list in the JSON error envelope's next[]' is partly aspirational: these two error branches are plain console.error with no JSON envelope today, so that sub-item is net-new scaffolding, not an extension of existing output. Minor and doesn't undermine the core finding.

Default-to-refute does not apply: there is no uncertainty here — the code unambiguously does single-guess only, with no prefix or multi-candidate behavior. Real, correctly scoped, low-impact DX upgrade.

findings.title: No `--explain <code>` long-form error reference (rustc E0308 / kubectl pattern)
findings.technique: Stable error codes linked to long-form explanations surfaced via `kolm explain KOLM_E_<CODE>` (rustc --explain), so a one-line error can point to richer remediation without bloating the terminal.
findings.source: https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/diagnostics/error-codes.html (--explain)
findings.current_state: A KOLM_E_* code taxonomy has started inside jsonErrorEnvelope call sites (KOLM_E_ARTIFACT_NOT_FOUND, KOLM_E_DO_NO_INPUT, KOLM_E_EVAL_FAILED), but there is no `--explain` verb to expand a code, and `kolm explain` (cli/kolm.js:536) is for artifacts, not error codes. Codes are inconsistently applied.
findings.proposed_change: Maintain a single ERROR_CODES map {code -> {title, detail, fix, docs_url}}; surface `kolm explain KOLM_E_...` (alias of the existing explain verb when the arg matches a code), include the code in every human plus JSON error, and add a short 'run kolm explain <code>' breadcrumb to multi-cause errors. Audit the codebase so every thrown error carries a code.
findings.rationale: Codes already exist piecemeal; formalizing them plus an --explain expander turns terse errors into self-service remediation and gives agents a stable machine key (the RFC 9457 'type'). It compounds with the dual-consumer envelope finding — same taxonomy serves both.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, NOT already present for its core deliverable. Confirmed against actual code:

1. NO centralized ERROR_CODES map exists. There is a typed-error primitive `src/kolm-error.js` (KolmError class + kolmError() factory with code/detail/status/retryable/install_hint) and ~60+ distinct KOLM_E_* codes scattered across src/ (artifact-runner.js, bundle-runner.js, compile.js, device-*.js, moe.js, runners/*.js, etc.). But none of these is a single registry mapping code -> {title, detail, fix, docs_url}. No docs_url field, no human-friendly titles/fixes anywhere.

2. NO `kolm explain KOLM_E_*` expander. cmdExplain (cli/kolm.js:41027) is artifact-only — it calls resolveArtifact(positional[0]) on the first positional arg with zero code-shaped-arg detection branch (verified cli/kolm.js:41028-41045). The help text (cli/kolm.js:536) confirms `explain <art.kolm>` is for artifacts. jsonErrorEnvelope (cli/kolm.js:40876) emits code/error/exit/hint/next but no docs_url and no breadcrumb to an explainer.

So the finding's current_state is accurate: codes exist piecemeal, applied inconsistently (e.g. cmdRun falls back to KOLM_E_BUNDLE_RUN_FAILED / KOLM_E_RUN_FAILED; many CLI errors use plain console.error with no code at all — ~2918 console.error/process.exit/throw sites in cli/kolm.js), and there is no --explain verb to expand a code.

EFFORT (claimed 2 days, adjusted to 3): The `--explain` expander plus a curated map covering the ~60 existing codes is ~1.5-2 days. But the proposed_change explicitly includes "Audit the codebase so every thrown error carries a code" — kolm-error.js's own header notes ~793 untyped `throw new Error(...)` call sites still to retire, and cli/kolm.js has ~2918 error/exit sites. Taking that audit literally pushes realistic effort to 3-4 days. Note: codes are declared "frozen" in AGENT_GUIDE.md:312, so the registry must preserve existing code spellings (a constraint, not a blocker — existing envelope.js/kolm-error.js plumbing lowers risk).

IMPACT (claimed medium, adjusted to low): The existing JSON envelope already carries `hint` and `next` (curated re-runnable commands), which deliver most of the self-service remediation value inline today. A `--explain` expander is a polish/DX nicety, not a critical gap. The strongest argument for it is the agent/machine-consumer angle (kolm is agent-first per AGENT_GUIDE.md, codes already advertised as a stable frozen machine key), but that audience is already served by the stable `code` field. So real but low-impact.

Relevant files: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/cli/kolm.js (cmdExplain ~41027, jsonErrorEnvelope ~40876, help ~536), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/kolm-error.js, C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/AGENT_GUIDE.md:297-312.

findings.title: No VHS .tape scripts for reproducible terminal demos of the CLI/TUI
findings.technique: Charm VHS: script terminal sessions in human-readable .tape files run in a virtual terminal to produce crisp, reproducible GIF/MP4/WebM — usable for docs, marketing, AND as deterministic integration tests of the CLI's rendered output.
findings.source: https://github.com/charmbracelet/vhs ; https://charm.land/blog/vhs-publish/
findings.current_state: No .tape files anywhere in the repo (checked root, demo/, scripts/). Demos/marketing terminal transcripts are hand-authored (e.g. the homepage reproducible terminal card from W604), which drift from real CLI output and aren't regenerated on release.
findings.proposed_change: Add scripts/demos/*.tape for the marquee flows (quickstart, compile, tui, doctor) and a `make demos` (or release-verify gate) that re-records GIFs with VHS so the website transcripts always match shipped behavior. Bonus: use VHS output as a golden-file regression check on the TUI's rendered frames.
findings.rationale: The project repeatedly hand-curates terminal transcripts for the homepage and they go stale (a documented W604 trap). VHS makes them reproducible and a release artifact. Low impact on core DX but high leverage for marketing/docs accuracy and adds a novel TUI regression-test angle. Requires ttyd+ffmpeg, so it's a dev-toolchain add, not a runtime dependency.

verdict.reason:
COULD NOT REFUTE — finding holds. No .tape files exist anywhere (only the audit's own cli-tui.frontier.json mentions VHS). Confirmed the two existing terminal-demo paths are both hand-authored and disconnected from real CLI/TUI output: (1) C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/scripts/video/gen-demo-video.mjs hard-codes every CLI command and output line in a SCENES array fed to ffmpeg drawtext — no kolm process is ever invoked; (2) C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/scripts/build-tui-demo-cast.mjs generates public/cdn/kolm-assets/tui-demo.cast from hand-typed template-literal pane layouts and status-bar strings — its own header comment calls it the "Source-of-truth ... when the demo storyboard changes", i.e. a manual storyboard, not a capture of `kolm tui`. The W223 test (tests/wave223-tui-showcase.test.js #11) asserts the cast "proves the recording is the real TUI not a hand-typed mockup" by pattern-matching box chars / [normal] status tags — but it matches against the hand-typed mockup, giving a false fidelity signal, which actually reinforces the drift concern the finding raises. release-verify.cjs has 13 gates and none regenerate or diff a terminal demo against real CLI output. So the technique (VHS-scripted, reproducible, golden-file-capable terminal recordings) is genuinely absent.

Adjustments: impact is correctly "low" — this is marketing/docs accuracy plus a novel TUI regression angle, not core DX; kolm's actual users (solo devs, ML engineers, enterprise) run the CLI and never consume .tape files, so leverage accrues to the kolm team's own docs, not end users. Effort is underestimated: the dev environment is Windows (gen-demo-video.mjs hardcodes a winget ffmpeg path), and VHS depends on ttyd which is Linux/macOS-first and awkward on Windows — an unstated prerequisite. Also the CLI has interactive auth/login and non-deterministic output (latencies, ids, timestamps) that must be seeded/scrubbed before recordings are stable enough for golden-file regression. Realistic effort is ~3-4 days, not 2.

findings.title: errorHint covers only 4 patterns; no broad contextual remediation registry
findings.technique: Pattern->remediation registry (Stripe/gh error UX): map common error signatures (network/timeout, 402 plan-gated, 429 rate-limit, missing toolchain, OOM/VRAM, ENOENT specific files) to a concrete next-command, declared not inferred.
findings.source: https://zircote.com/blog/2026/04/cli-error-messages-are-a-dual-consumer-problem/ (remediation declared via suggested_fix/code_actions) ; Stripe CLI actionable errors
findings.current_state: errorHint (cli/kolm.js:123) handles only 401-auth, generic auth-required, ENOENT-.kolm, and artifact-not-found. Common failures — connection refused / timeout, HTTP 402 plan gate, 429 rate limit, missing export toolchain, CUDA/VRAM OOM — fall through to a bare wrapped message with no next step.
findings.proposed_change: Grow errorHint into a small ordered registry of {matcher, hint, next[]} entries covering network/402/429/toolchain/VRAM cases, and feed the same next[] into jsonErrorEnvelope so human and agent get identical remediation. Keep it data-driven so adding a case is a one-line append.
findings.rationale: The hook (errorHint plus withErrorContext) is already in the hot path; it just under-covers. The most painful real-world errors (no network, plan gate, missing toolchain) are exactly the ones missing a next step. Cheap, additive, and it directly feeds the dual-consumer envelope so the two findings reinforce each other.

verdict.reason:
Could not refute. Verified against actual code. errorHint (cli/kolm.js:123-140) handles exactly 4 patterns: HTTP 401 status, auth_required/not-signed-in/unauthorized regex, ENOENT+.kolm path, and artifact-not-found. The finding's description is accurate. There is NO centralized pattern->remediation registry anywhere in cli/ or src/ — I searched for matcher/registry/remediation/errorRegistry and found only this one function plus scattered, hand-written inline `hint:`/`install_hint:` strings at individual call sites (not a data-driven registry, not fed into the dual-consumer envelope).

The proposed change is feasible and the hook is genuinely in the hot path: withErrorContext wraps essentially every verb in the dispatch table (~lines 39735+), and api() (line 270) already attaches err.status (so 402/429 are available) and err.code (ETIMEDOUT at line 294, ECONNREFUSED at 299-305) — so the metadata the registry would match against IS present. jsonErrorEnvelope (line 40876) already exposes both `hint` and `next[]` fields, but errorHint only returns a single string and never populates next[], so the dual-consumer reinforcement the proposal describes is genuinely absent.

Two minor caveats that soften but do not refute the finding: (1) doctor/cloud paths DO carry inline install_hint strings for missing toolchains (lines 12350, 13110, 13151, 13187, etc.), so "missing toolchain has no next step" is partially overstated for those specific surfaces — but the centralized generic errorHint path lacks it. (2) The CLI already has rich next_steps plumbing for the happy-path chat/do flows, so the marginal gain is incremental.

Effort 1 day is realistic (contained, additive, single-file, but needs per-matcher care since 402/429/network messages vary by source, plus tests). Impact medium is accurate — real painful first-run errors (no network, expired plan, rate limit) get a declared next step, but it is incremental polish over partial existing coverage, not transformative. already_partially_present:true is correct; I set alreadyPresent:false because the SPECIFIC proposed capability (broad registry covering network/402/429/toolchain/VRAM + next[] into the JSON envelope) is not implemented — only the 4-pattern stub is.

audit.summary: The account surface is a large, real product cockpit: 60 top-level pages + 18 subdir pages. The flagship console (overview.html) is genuinely data-wired against live, auth-gated tenant APIs (/v1/account, /v1/account/state, /v1/intent/next, /v1/savings/displacement, /v1/drift/status, /v1/product/graph, /v1/account/next-actions) with real skeletons, empty states, and error fallbacks. The dedicated NO-CODE builder (create-model.html) has a fully built 4-tab UI (Describe/Upload/Connect/Capture) with real client-side CSV/JSONL/JSON/TSV parsing, column auto-detection, autosave, cost estimate, and an SSE compile overlay -- BUT the compile pipeline is an explicit self-documented STUB: /v1/compile/start returns a fake cj_ job id, /v1/compile/preview returns hardcoded Q&A, and src/compile-stream.js emits 22 deterministic fake SSE events ending in a synthetic art_<jobid> slug that maps to no real artifact, so the page promise 'One outcome: a signed .kolm artifact you own' is not delivered by the no-code path. Separately there is a CSS token defect: ~42/60 pages and whats-next.js reference --good/--bad/--warn which are NOT defined in ks.css (only --green/--amber/--red/--warning exist) and live only in design-tokens.css, which 44/60 account pages do NOT load -- so status pills/dots/severity colors silently fall back to inherited ink. The wizard is also orphaned (not in the overview sidebar). Known tech debt (22 pages lack product-graph journey ownership) is already captured in docs/research/kolm-account-product-matrix-seed-2026-05-25.md. Full result written to C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/audit/w920/account-ui.audit.json.
