checks.evidence: 8 pages have no fetch/kfetch: bakeoff, continuous-monitoring, forge, governance, keys, onboarding, pipelines, sustainability -- none declare a 'reference' classification (violates seed-doc redline).

checks.name: Product-graph journey ownership for account pages
checks.evidence: Documented tech debt: 'account pages missing product-graph ownership: 22'; intended control file docs/account-product-matrix.json described as 'should seed' i.e. not yet built.

checks.name: account-shell.css token fallbacks
checks.evidence: Defensive fallbacks throughout: 'var(--bg-card, #14151a)' (37), 'var(--ink, #e8e9ec)' (54), 'var(--line, #23252b)'. Note fallbacks are dark while live default is light, but ks.css aliases mean fallbacks aren't normally hit.

checks.name: Reduced-motion + a11y plumbing
checks.evidence: prefers-reduced-motion collapses motion tokens to 1ms (451-465). Pages include skip links (overview:93-94,123), aria-live (create-model:329,127), role=tablist/tab/tabpanel (create-model:209-214), focus-visible rings (account-shell:222-227).

gaps: PRIMARY: The no-code Create-a-model wizard cannot produce a real .kolm artifact. /v1/compile/start, /v1/compile/preview, and the SSE stream (src/compile-stream.js) are explicit stubs emitting deterministic fake events and a synthetic art_<jobid> slug that resolves to no real artifact; the 'View artifact ->' CTA dead-ends. Contradicts the page promise 'One outcome: a signed .kolm artifact you own.'

CSS defect: --good/--bad/--warn undefined in ks.css (only --green/--amber/--red/--warning exist). 42/60 account pages + whats-next.js reference them with no fallback, so status pills, drift dots, and severity colors silently render in inherited ink instead of green/amber/red. Fix by aliasing in ks.css or loading design-tokens.css on these pages.
design-tokens.css (which defines --good/--bad/--warn) is loaded by only 16/60 account pages; overview.html and create-model.html are not among them.
The no-code wizard (/account/create-model) is missing from the overview sidebar and only linked from 2 pages -- hard to discover.

Auth-gate inconsistency: 24/60 account pages (incl create-model, members, org, gateway, sla) have no client-side signin redirect and account HTML is served statically with no server-side gate; logged-out users see bare shells that fail to populate.
Duplicate/overlapping key pages: keys.html (static stub) and api-keys.html coexist with no clear canonical.
8 account pages render only static markup with no data fetch and no 'reference' classification (bakeoff, continuous-monitoring, forge, governance, keys, onboarding, pipelines, sustainability).
Documented but unresolved: 22 account pages lack product-graph journey ownership (per docs seed); intended control file docs/account-product-matrix.json not yet built.
Cost estimate and live-tail readiness bars are heuristic/hardcoded, not tied to real billing or capture telemetry.

findings.title: Ship a shared command palette (Cmd/Ctrl-K) across all /account pages
findings.technique: A modal cmdk-style palette (centered overlay + backdrop, single fuzzy-filter input, keyboard-first list, scoped Navigate/Actions/Search groups), now table-stakes in Vercel/Linear/Supabase/Raycast/GitHub. Build dependency-free (kolm has no build step) as one public/account-cmdk.js reading the same nav graph the sidebar uses, plus action verbs (compile, rotate key, invite member).
findings.source: Vercel Command Menu docs https://vercel.com/docs/concepts/dashboard-features/command-menu ; Mobbin Command Palette https://mobbin.com/glossary/command-palette ; uxpatterns.dev https://uxpatterns.dev/patterns/advanced/command-palette
findings.current_state: No command palette anywhere. grep for metaKey/ctrlKey/cmd+k across public/account/*.js + *.html finds zero real palette wiring -- only keydown handler is create-model.html:581 (Enter/Space on dropzone). Navigation is a hardcoded 16-item sidebar duplicated per page across 60 pages, no global jump/search.
findings.proposed_change: Create public/account-cmdk.js (~250 LOC vanilla): flat index from account nav links + action registry; mount on (metaKey||ctrlKey)&&k; accessible role=dialog with aria-activedescendant, focus trap, reduced-motion-gated transition; reuse window.kesc. Inject the <script> via the same mechanism that already injects the theme bootstrap on all 60 pages.
files: public/account/overview.html (+ shared inject point used by all 60 pages)
findings.rationale: With 60 top-level + 18 subdir pages and a duplicated 16-link sidebar, navigation is the dominant friction. A palette is the highest-leverage UX upgrade for competitor parity, fully achievable with no build step (kolm already ships dependency-free vanilla like ks-sparkline.js), and incidentally un-orphans create-model via a 'Create model' action.

verdict.reason:
Could not refute the core claim: there is genuinely NO Cmd/Ctrl-K command palette anywhere. Grep for metaKey/ctrlKey/key==="k" across all 60 public/account/*.html, nav.js, kolm-chat.js, and shared JS returns zero palette wiring (only page-local Enter-on-input handlers and the create-model.html dropzone keydown). The finding's current_state is accurate. NOT already present.

However the finding is OVERSTATED on two fronts, so impact is downgraded high->medium:

(1) "No global jump/search anywhere" is wrong. C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/nav.js (lines ~516-569) already injects, on EVERY account page, a tenant-scoped natural-language console chat (data-kolm-chat data-kolm-chat-mode="auth", backed by public/kolm-chat.js, 405 LOC) that takes plain-English queries ("show artifacts I have not deployed", "what should I distill next?") and returns the exact CLI command, plus an 8-tile product matrix and an 8-step pipeline jump map. So a large slice of the palette's purpose (global jump + action verbs) is already served, just via chat rather than a keyboard-first modal.

(2) Two stated implementation facts are inaccurate. The proposed "inject via the same mechanism that injects the theme bootstrap" is wrong — the theme script is inlined per-page; the real cross-page inject point is nav.js, which already runs on all account pages (isAccountShell gate at nav.js:219) and already does main.insertBefore + renderAccountSidebar(). And the canonical nav graph the palette should read is accountNavGroups in nav.js (lines ~428-479), NOT the per-page sidebar HTML — the line-129 16-item sidebar in overview.html is only an SSR fallback that nav.js overwrites via renderAccountSidebar() into #account-sidebar.

Effort 4 days is defensible-to-optimistic: the two assumed-hard pieces (canonical nav graph, cross-page inject) are already solved in nav.js, but a truly accessible cmdk (role=dialog, focus trap, aria-activedescendant, fuzzy filter, real action-verb wiring like rotate-key/invite-member to endpoints, reduced-motion, mobile) exceeds the claimed ~250 LOC; realistic 3-5 days. Users coming from Linear/Vercel/Raycast expect Cmd-K parity, so it is a real polish item, but it is a nicety layered on already-dense navigation plus an existing NL console, not the dominant-friction killer the rationale claims.

findings.title: Fix the --good/--bad/--warn CSS token defect (status colors silently dead on ~42/60 pages)
findings.technique: Define --good/--bad/--warn directly in ks.css (the one stylesheet every account page loads), aliased to existing --green/--amber/--red, instead of relying on design-tokens.css which 44/60 pages do not load.
findings.source: Internal audit account-ui.audit.json check 'CSS status tokens' status:fail; WebAIM Million 2025 color/contrast failures
findings.current_state: ks.css defines --green/--amber/--red/--warning (~167-174) but NOT --good/--bad/--warn. 43 account pages + whats-next.js use var(--good)/var(--bad)/var(--warn) with no fallback; only 16/60 link design-tokens.css where they ARE defined. On the other 44 pages, pill.ok/dot.ok/severity colors resolve to inherited ink -- green/amber/red distinction silently lost. whats-next.js:18-20 SEV_COLOR uses var(--warn).
findings.proposed_change: Add to ks.css :root -- --good: var(--green); --bad: var(--red); --warn: var(--amber); (+ --warning alias). One line per token, zero risk, instantly restores status color on all 44 broken pages. Optionally add a CI guard grepping for var(--TOKEN) usages with no matching :root definition in ks.css.
findings.rationale: Cheapest high-impact fix in the surface. Status semantics (ok/warn/error) are the core visual language of a cockpit; red/amber/green collapse to ink on the majority of pages, which both looks broken and is an accessibility regression (color is often the only signal). 30-minute fix in the single shared stylesheet.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED, not refuted. Verified in actual code at C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/ks.css. The :root "Legacy token aliases" block (lines 107-202) defines --green/--amber/--red/--warning (167-176) and --ks-good/--ks-bad (78-79) but OMITS the unprefixed --good/--bad/--warn. The block's own comment (lines 115-117) states its purpose: "every public HTML page loads ks.css. Putting the aliases here means one edit covers the whole site" — exactly the pattern the fix follows, so this is a genuine omission, not intentional.

Magnitude (verified): 62 account pages use var(--good/bad/warn). 41 of them load ONLY ks.css (all 41 confirmed to link ks.css, none link design-tokens.css) AND use the no-fallback form var(--good)/var(--bad)/var(--warn) (297 such usages site-wide in account/*.html). On those pages the tokens are undefined, so color/background:var(--good) inherits the default ink (#111). overview.html (main account landing) is in this set: loads only ks.css, has 3x var(--good)/4x var(--bad)/2x var(--warn) with no fallback. whats-next.js (loaded by overview.html) SEV_COLOR uses var(--warn) with no fallback — confirmed at lines 16-19. The tokens ARE defined in design-tokens.css (lines 112-114 -> --status-good/warn/bad) which only ~20 of these pages load. So the core defect is real and the fix is correct.

Already implemented? No. No CI guard for undefined CSS tokens exists. --ks-good/--ks-bad (prefixed) exist but the product CSS/HTML uses the UNPREFIXED --good/--bad/--warn, which are absent from ks.css.

Caveats lowering impact below "high": (1) Several pages already self-heal via inline fallbacks — active-learning, confidence, diagnose, routing, seasonal, staleness, org use var(--good,#7fbf3f)/var(--bad,#cf4f3a)/var(--warn,#d9a020) — so they render fine. Truly-broken count is 41, slightly under the finding's "44/60". (2) The design system's status colors are deliberately MUTED per the cool-slate direction: --ks-pos #2d6a48 (muted green), --ks-err #8b2828 (muted red), and critically --ks-warn #56606c is a slate GRAY, not amber. So "fixing" warn shifts text from ink-black to slate-gray — a small visual delta, not a vivid amber. good/bad shifts are more noticeable (ink -> muted green/red). Net: real defect, but "looks broken / accessibility regression" is somewhat overstated; medium-high is fairer than high.

Effort 0.5 days is realistic-to-generous: the actual fix is 3-4 alias lines (--good: var(--green); --bad: var(--red); --warn: var(--amber); + --warning already exists) in the existing block, ~30 min. Optional CI guard adds the remaining time. Users would care: status pills/dots are the core visual language of these cockpit pages, and on the majority of them the ok/warn/bad distinction currently collapses to one color.

findings.title: Make the no-code Compile SSE resilient: reconnect with cursor, per-step error states, Retry
findings.technique: Production SSE UX: handle drops via EventSource auto-reconnect + the existing ?cursor=N reattach contract, render per-step error states (not just success), and a Retry button. The stub stream never fails so the UI has no failure path.
findings.source: codingwithmuhib SSE 'gracefully handles connection drops, server-side errors' https://www.codingwithmuhib.com/blogs/real-time-ui-updates-with-sse-simpler-than-websockets ; twocents https://www.twocents.software/blog/real-time-features-in-saas/
findings.current_state: compile-stream.js supports ?cursor reattach server-side (line 71) and create-model.html opens EventSource (line 867), but the client overlay only renders the happy path (the stub can't fail). No onerror reconnect-with-cursor, no per-step 'failed' rendering, no retry CTA. captured.html (line 178) is a real /v1/capture/stream consumer and is a better resilience template.
findings.proposed_change: In create-model.html: add EventSource.onerror -> if not done, reconnect with ?cursor=<lastSeq> (server already supports it); render step.status==='failed' with --bad color + error message; add 'Retry compile' button that re-POSTs /v1/compile/start. Mirror captured.html's reconnection. Pairs with the real-pipeline finding (a real job CAN fail).
findings.rationale: The server-side reattach contract exists but the client doesn't use it, and there's no failure path. Once the pipeline is real, jobs will drop and fail -- the UI must survive that or the no-code promise breaks on the first network blip. Low effort, reuses an in-repo pattern.

verdict.reason:
The finding's code-level claims are accurate. Verified in C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack:

- src/compile-stream.js line 71 reads ?cursor and line 88 skips events with seq <= cursor — server-side reattach DOES exist. Line 64 emits id: ${ev.seq} per SSE event.
- public/account/create-model.html line 867 opens EventSource. The onerror handler (lines 890-892) only calls appendLog('Stream interrupted. Reload to resume; cursor is preserved.') — it does NOT auto-reconnect with ?cursor, and the client never even tracks lastSeq (it ignores the seq field in event data). So the server's reattach contract is genuinely unused by the client.
- No Retry button: overlay actions are only Cancel + View artifact (lines 381-384). Confirmed via grep — the only 'retry'/'cursor' hits are CSS cursor: props and two log strings.
- Per-step 'failed' rendering is PARTIALLY scaffolded: the CSS .cm-overlay .step[data-state=\"failed\"] exists (line 147), but no JS handler ever sets a step to 'failed' and there is no step.error/step.fail SSE listener. So the visual hook is pre-baked but unwired.

So the specific upgrade (auto-reconnect-with-cursor, wired failed rendering, Retry CTA) is genuinely NOT implemented — isReal=true, alreadyPresent=false.

HOWEVER, the finding itself concedes this is contingent: 'Pairs with the real-pipeline finding (a real job CAN fail).' src/router.js:1357 /v1/compile/start returns a stub job explicitly noted as 'Stub job — emits deterministic progress events', and buildEventLog (compile-stream.js:39-63) only ever emits status:'done' (line 51). The stub runs locally in ~5s with no GPU and CANNOT fail or drop. The existing onerror message and the pre-baked 'failed' CSS show the resilience path was deliberately scaffolded-but-deferred, not overlooked. Until the real pipeline lands there is no failure to exercise, so all this code would be dead/untestable.

Effort: the finding's diagnosis of WHAT to change is precise and the in-repo captured.html (lines 176-192) is indeed a reconnect template, so 2 days is high — the wiring (track lastSeq, reconnect with ?cursor on onerror, add a failed listener + Retry button) is ~1 day given the server contract already exists.

Impact: overblown at 'medium'. This is no-code wizard polish on a stub that today cannot fail; it has zero user-facing value until the real compile pipeline exists (a separate, larger dependency). Adjusted to 'low' — it is a reasonable thing to do AT THE SAME TIME as making the pipeline real, but on its own it protects against a failure mode that the current code makes impossible.

findings.title: Roll out sparklines/inline trend charts to data-bearing pages (lib exists, used on only 4/60)
findings.technique: Inline sparklines + delta indicators on every metric with a time series, per the 2025 dashboard pattern (trend at a glance, drill on demand). kolm ships a high-quality dependency-free sparkline (ks-sparkline.js: 0/1/N states, accessible aria-label trend, kts-v1 contract, /v1/kscore/series backing) -- just under-deployed.
findings.source: Lazarev dashboard UX https://www.lazarev.agency/articles/dashboard-ux-design ; 5of10 2025 https://5of10.com/articles/dashboard-design-best-practices/ ; existing public/account/ks-sparkline.js + /v1/kscore/series
findings.current_state: ks-sparkline.js is production-grade (267 LOC, empty/single/N states, summarize() trend helper, cool-slate palette, headless-testable). But renderSparkline is referenced on only 4 pages (active-learning, gateway, namespaces, sustainability). /v1/kscore/series exists in router.js. Most metric tiles on overview/billing/savings/drift/distill-runs show a bare number, no trend.
findings.proposed_change: Add sparklines to metric tiles: overview.html (K-Score over runs from /v1/kscore/series), billing.html (spend), savings.html (displacement), drift.html/staleness.html (drift), distill-runs.html (per-run K-Score). Each ~5 lines: fetch series, window.renderSparkline(points,{...}), inject SVG. Add a +/- delta chip via summarize().trend.
findings.rationale: The expensive part (a correct, accessible, no-dep sparkline + its data route) is already built and tested. Deploying widely is near-free and closes the 'metrics-first but no trend' gap competitors solved years ago -- high impact-to-effort ratio because nothing new needs inventing.

verdict.reason:
PARTLY REAL but materially overstated; the core impact-to-effort rationale is broken.

VERIFIED TRUE:
- ks-sparkline.js is production-grade: 267 LOC, 0/1/N states, summarize() trend helper, accessible aria-label, cool-slate palette. Smoke test scripts/ks-sparkline-smoke.mjs passes 11/11 (I ran it).
- /v1/kscore/series exists (src/router.js:23921, backed by src/kscore-timeseries.js).
- Target pages overview.html, billing.html, savings.html, drift.html show bare numbers with NO trend chart (grep for spark/chart/series/polyline returns nothing but nav/favicon SVGs).

CLAIMS THAT ARE WRONG:
1. "renderSparkline referenced on 4 pages (active-learning, gateway, namespaces, sustainability)" -- FALSE. The shared lib (window.KolmSparkline/renderSparkline) is used on ONLY namespaces.html (the sole <script src="/account/ks-sparkline.js"> include). active-learning.html has its OWN inline paintSparkline(); gateway.html has a provider-routing BAR chart (#bar-chart), not a time-series sparkline; sustainability.html has a literal placeholder ("[ placeholder line chart -- populates after first .kolm run ]"), not a sparkline. So the shared lib is even less deployed than claimed -- but other pages (routing.html, confidence.html, distill-runs.html) already ship their own trend viz.

2. THE HEADLINE PREMISE IS BROKEN: "the data route is already built" / "each ~5 lines: fetch series". /v1/kscore/series returns ONLY K-Score. There is NO /v1/billing/series (spend), NO savings/displacement series, NO drift/staleness series. The only *-series routes in router.js are /v1/sla/series and /v1/kscore/series. So billing.html, savings.html, drift.html, staleness.html each need a NEW backend time-series aggregation endpoint (spend-over-time, displacement-over-time, drift-over-time) + a kscore-timeseries.js-style module + tests. That is real backend work, NOT "near-free." Only overview.html (K-Score) can reuse the existing route as the finding describes.

3. distill-runs.html should be dropped from the target list -- it ALREADY renders sparklines (loss curve + k-score-per-step via its own sparkline() at distill-runs.html:171-214). Adding sparklines there is duplicate work.

EFFORT: claimed 3 days is low. The frontend wiring (~5 lines/page) is cheap, but 3-4 of the named pages need brand-new server-side time-series endpoints + storage queries + tests + the per-page inline implementations standardized onto the shared lib. Realistically ~6 days.

IMPACT: claimed medium, adjusted to low. These are deep, low-traffic account sub-pages (billing/savings/drift/staleness), not the primary dashboard. The 'trend at a glance' UX win is real but marginal for kolm's actual users (developers wiring a distillation pipeline care about run outcomes and receipts, which distill-runs/active-learning/routing ALREADY chart). The finding is a legitimate consistency/polish item, but the 'high impact-to-effort because nothing new needs inventing' rationale is false for the majority of the listed files.

findings.title: Generalize drag-drop upload + make it WCAG 2.2 keyboard-operable, then reuse on datasets/lake
findings.technique: A single accessible dropzone: visible drag/hover/drop states, keyboard fallback (Tab focus, Enter/Space picker -- create-model already does this), ARIA live region announcing parse results, per-file error + Retry. WCAG 2.2 AA requires the drop zone be keyboard-operable; 2025 reports show 95.9% of sites fail basic WCAG.
findings.source: Smart Interface Design Patterns drag-and-drop https://smart-interface-design-patterns.com/articles/drag-and-drop-ux/ ; Filestack 2025 https://blog.filestack.com/building-modern-drag-and-drop-upload-ui/
findings.current_state: Drag-drop on only 2 pages (create-model.html, datasets.html). create-model's dropzone is good: keyboard handler (line 581) + real client-side CSV/JSONL/JSON/TSV parsing + column auto-detect + kept/dropped quality. But logic is page-local, not reusable; datasets.html has a separate implementation; no shared ARIA live region announcing 'parsed N rows, dropped M'.
findings.proposed_change: Extract create-model's dropzone+parser into public/account-dropzone.js (vanilla, mountDropzone(el,{onParsed})). Add aria-live=polite announcing parse results + errors. Reuse on datasets.html and lake.html. Keep the parquet/xlsx 'preview not available in browser' honesty. Dedupes two implementations and standardizes the most accessibility-sensitive widget.
findings.rationale: The hard part (a working multi-format client parser with column detection and quality stats) is already written once. Extracting + adding a live region is straightforward, removes duplicated logic, and raises the accessibility floor on the surface's most touch-heavy widget.

verdict.reason: Finding stands (not refuted). Verified against actual code. create-model.html dropzone (line 244) genuinely has tabindex=0, role=button, aria-label + keyboard handler (line 581 Enter/Space -> picker) + real client-side CSV/JSONL/JSON/TSV parser with column auto-detect and kept/dropped quality stats (lines 602-724) — exactly as described. datasets.html (line 111) has a SEPARATE inferior drop-zone: plain <div id=drop-zone> with NO tabindex, NO role, NO aria-label, NO keydown handler, so it is genuinely NOT WCAG 2.2 keyboard-operable (real AA violation). Its parser is JSONL-only (lines 185-211), duplicating logic. Neither page wraps parse results in aria-live (existing aria-live elements at create-model 202/329/380 and datasets 94 are for loading-status/live-tail/overlay-log, not the upload parse summary). No account-dropzone.js or shared dropzone helper exists anywhere in public/. So the dedup + keyboard-fix + aria-live work is NOT already implemented. CAVEATS that lower accuracy: (1) already_partially_present is correct — the hard part (parser + a keyboard-operable variant) exists once in create-model, so this is a refactor+a11y-lift, not greenfield. (2) The 'reuse on lake.html' target is speculative scope, not dedup: lake.html is a read-only telemetry dashboard over ~/.kolm/events/ (stats/tail/export) with zero upload UI; adding a dropzone there is net-new feature work that doesn't fit the page and overlaps the canonical capture/log ingest path. (3) Effort 3 days is fair-to-slightly-high for the real 2-page dedup+a11y; lake.html inflates it. (4) Impact 'medium' is fair-to-generous — real WCAG gap but on a low-traffic auth-gated internal account page used by few operators.

findings.title: Replace the Capture tab's hardcoded fake live-tail with the real /v1/capture/stream SSE
findings.technique: Point the no-code Capture tab at the real SSE endpoint that already powers captured.html, instead of seedTail() with 3 hardcoded rows and hardcoded readiness-bar widths.
findings.source: router.js:14815 real /v1/capture/stream handler; create-model.html:772 self-documented stub; captured.html:178 real EventSource consumer
findings.current_state: create-model.html line 772 '// Stub live-tail; in production this'd be an SSE feed from /v1/capture/stream'; seedTail() hardcodes 3 rows (773-781); readiness bars hardcoded width:62/88/24/41/8% (322-326). captured.html ALREADY consumes the REAL /v1/capture/stream (line 178, EventSource withCredentials) and that handler is real + authMiddleware-gated (router.js:14815).
findings.proposed_change: In create-model.html Capture tab: replace seedTail() with new EventSource('/v1/capture/stream',{withCredentials:true}) scoped to the wizard's namespace (copy the proven captured.html pattern). Drive readiness bars from real capture counts (/v1/account/state signals or /v1/capture/browse) instead of hardcoded percentages.
findings.rationale: Pure integration: the real endpoint exists AND a working client consumer of it already exists on another page. The wizard shows fake rows only because nobody copied captured.html's EventSource into it. Closing this makes the 'watch your first capture land in real time' onboarding claim actually true inside the wizard.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, NOT already implemented in the wizard. All cited code confirmed in C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack:
- create-model.html:772-782 — seedTail() stub with 3 hardcoded rows and the self-documenting comment "Stub live-tail; in production this'd be an SSE feed from /v1/capture/stream" exists exactly as quoted.
- create-model.html:322-326 — readiness bars hardcoded width:62/88/24/41/8% confirmed.
- src/router.js:14815 — r.get('/v1/capture/stream', authMiddleware, ...) is a real, auth-gated SSE handler backed by the capture-stream pub/sub broker; returns 401 without req.tenant.
- A working real consumer already exists at public/account/captured.html:178 (new EventSource('/v1/capture/stream',{withCredentials:true})). NOTE the finding's path 'captured.html:178' is imprecise — it is public/account/captured.html, not public/captured.html.

NOT already present in create-model.html: that page's only EventSource (line 867, openSSE) consumes the COMPILE stream (/v1/compile/stream/<job>), not the capture stream. So the Capture tab genuinely still uses the hardcoded seedTail().

Why impact is downgraded to LOW (finding says medium, overblown):
1. create-model.html is partly a logged-OUT / pre-signup demo surface — it explicitly states 'Using placeholder API key ... sign in to bake yours in' (line 317) and offers Sign in / Get started CTAs. /v1/capture/stream requires authMiddleware + req.tenant and 401s otherwise, so for the page's likely-largest audience (logged-out explorers) the real SSE would render an empty box, arguably worse than the current illustrative sample rows that show 'what this will look like.' The hardcoded rows read as a deliberate demo illustration, not purely a bug.
2. Authed users who want a real live-tail already have a dedicated, fully-real surface at /account/captured (the very page the finding cites as the proven consumer), so live-tail-inside-the-wizard is partly redundant.

Effort: 1.5 days is defensible for the FULL scope (SSE swap + driving readiness bars from real per-namespace capture counts via /v1/capture/browse or /v1/account/state, which requires real readiness computation, not a one-liner). The SSE-only swap is closer to ~0.5 day. Backend prerequisites both exist (/v1/capture/stream and /v1/capture/browse confirmed in router.js), so this is pure front-end integration — but it must handle the logged-out 401 path gracefully (fall back to sample/empty), which the finding does not mention. Real and feasible, modest user value given the demo-page context and the existing authed live-tail page.

findings.title: Add a 3-5 item activation checklist that reorders by completion (route -> capture -> compile -> verify)
findings.technique: Adaptive setup checklist: 3-5 items max, persisted progress, reorders/unlocks as the user completes real milestones, with a named aha-moment ('user has a signed .kolm'). Linear/Vercel pattern. kolm already has the signal source (/v1/account/state) and a localStorage resume pattern in onboarding.html.
findings.source: rocknroll.dev activation checklist https://rocknroll.dev/p/saas-onboarding-checklist/ ; saasui flows that convert 2026 https://www.saasui.design/blog/saas-onboarding-flows-that-actually-convert-2026
findings.current_state: onboarding.html has a real path-resume pattern (data-resume cards, .ob-progress 'Resume your last path', localStorage) -- but it's a path PICKER (A/B/C/D), not a completion checklist of the core loop. whats-next.js is a ranked feed, not a fixed checklist with a visible completion count toward a named milestone. No '2 of 4 steps to your first artifact' progress object on the overview.
findings.proposed_change: Add #activation-checklist to overview.html driven by /v1/account/state signals: Step 1 Route a call (route_first), Step 2 Reach compile readiness (namespace_ready), Step 3 Compile (artifacts>0), Step 4 Verify a receipt. Render N of 4, current step highlighted, completed steps checked, minimal success acknowledgment when all 4 land. Persist dismissal in localStorage like onboarding.html. Hide once complete.
findings.rationale: The data signals (route_first, namespace_ready, compile_first) already exist in /v1/account/state and are consumed by whats-next.js, and the localStorage resume mechanic exists in onboarding.html. Composing them into a fixed 4-step checklist with a named milestone is modest work targeting activation -- the metric onboarding research says matters most.

verdict.reason:
REAL, not refuted. The proposed fixed activation checklist (N of 4, route->capture->compile->verify, completed-step checks, named milestone, on overview.html) does NOT exist. What exists is adjacent but distinct: (1) C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/account/whats-next.js is a RANKED FEED — it reads /v1/account/state, maps signals via ruleFor(), sorts by score, slices top-4 (lines 41-42). No stable completion count, no checked/unchecked steps, no named-milestone celebration; cards appear/vanish by score not completion. (2) C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/account/onboarding.html (lines 74-132) has a 'Step X of Y' + 'Resume your last path' localStorage pattern (key kolm-onboarding) but it is a PATH PICKER (A/B/C/D), with steppers inside per-path partials, not a core-loop tracker on the overview. Tests wave888f #5/#12 and wave889-7 #3 lock this in as path-stepper behavior. So already_partially_present=true is accurate: the data signals (route_first, compile_first, namespace_ready) DO exist in /v1/account/state (src/router.js:9408-9479) and the localStorage resume mechanic exists in onboarding.html — but they are not composed into a checklist.

Caveats the finding understates: (a) UNSTATED DEPENDENCY — the proposed Step 4 'Verify a receipt' has NO backing signal in /v1/account/state; the route emits no verify/receipt signal, so this step needs a new server-side signal or client-side tracking. (b) The proposed Step 2 maps to namespace_ready which only fires at >=1000 captures (router.js:9457) — far too high a bar for an 'activation' checklist; most activating users never trip it, so the step would need a lower-threshold readiness notion.

Effort: 3 days is slightly generous given most plumbing exists; realistic at ~2 days including the repo's mandatory lock-in tests. Impact: 'medium' is optimistic — kolm's audience is developer/CLI-first and the overview already foregrounds a CLI console plus a ranked 'What's next' feed covering the same signals, so the marginal activation lift over the existing surface is small. Adjusted to low.

findings.title: Real cost estimate + estimate-vs-actual from actual GPU pricing instead of heuristic_v1
findings.technique: Replace the pure-heuristic cost estimator with one fed by the real GPU/billing layer (the deploy/runpod routes the surface already calls), and show estimate-vs-actual after the job. 2025 guidance: surface real numbers, not guesses, on money-facing widgets.
findings.source: compile-stream.js estimateCompile method:'heuristic_v1'; existing /v1/deploy/runpod + /v1/integrations/runpod/test routes; Improvado Stripe analytics https://improvado.io/blog/stripe-analytics
findings.current_state: create-model.html debounce-fetches /v1/compile/estimate (line 474) -> estimateCompile() in compile-stream.js, a pure heuristic (chars/4 tokens * recipeMultiplier * vramMultiplier, baseUsd 0.40) explicitly method:'heuristic_v1' with note 'final cost depends on dataset size + actual GPU SKU'. No real billing/GPU price feed, even though the surface elsewhere calls /v1/deploy/runpod and /v1/integrations/runpod/test.
findings.proposed_change: Feed estimateCompile from the real GPU SKU pricing used by the RunPod/deploy path (per-hour rate * estimated minutes) and the real dataset row count from the parsed file instead of chars/4. After a real compile, record actual_usd/actual_minutes on the artifact and show 'estimated $X -> actual $Y' on the artifacts page. Keep the method tag accurate (heuristic vs measured).
findings.rationale: Depends on the real-compile finding landing first (no real job = no real cost). Lower standalone impact than credibility/navigation fixes, but honest money-facing numbers matter for trust and the RunPod price source already exists in-repo to wire from.

verdict.reason:
REAL but partially present, and the finding understates how much already exists. Verified: the no-code surface (C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/account/create-model.html:474) fetches /v1/compile/estimate, which routes to estimateCompile() in C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/compile-stream.js:106-123 — genuinely a pure heuristic (chars/4 tokens, baseUsd 0.40, recipe/vram multipliers, literal method:'heuristic_v1'). So the surface-level gap is accurate.

However, kolm ALREADY HAS a real rate-based estimator that the finding does not credit: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/compute/estimator.js computes cost_usd = cost_per_hour_usd * duration_seconds / 3600 from real spec size (examples*epochs*seq, model param scaling, per-backend TPS), and C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/cloud/runpod.js already exports RUNPOD_GPU_PRICING_USD_HR (real SKU table: A4000 $0.30/hr ... H100 $3.89/hr) + priceForGpu(), while provisionPod/getPodStatus return live cost_per_hr from RunPod GraphQL. The catch: that good estimator is wired ONLY to the CLI (C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/cli/kolm.js:31857-31858 via estimateAll/estimate); there is NO HTTP route exposing it and the no-code page never calls it. So this is mostly 'wire an existing in-repo module to the web surface,' not 'build a new estimator.'

Effort: 4 days is overestimated for the realistic deliverable. Routing /v1/compile/estimate to compute/estimator.js (or priceForGpu) and using parsed dataset row count is ~1-1.5 days. The estimate-vs-actual half is correctly gated by the finding's own rationale: /v1/compile/start (router.js:1357) is explicitly a STUB job emitting deterministic progress events — no real compile, no real bill, no actual_usd to record on any artifact (confirmed no actual_usd/actual_minutes anywhere for the compile path). That dependency is real and large but lives in the separate real-compile finding.

Impact: low is accurate. The widget already shows an honest caveat ('final cost depends on dataset size + actual GPU SKU'), it bills no one today, and it sits behind a stub flow. Lower priority than credibility/navigation fixes, as the finding itself states. Not refuted: it is a real, honestly-self-rated upgrade — just smaller in effort and more partially-built than written.

findings.title: Add keyboard shortcuts + a '?' shortcuts cheat-sheet (g-then-key navigation)
findings.technique: Linear/GitHub-style keyboard navigation: g+o (overview), g+a (artifacts), g+b (builds), '?' opens a shortcuts overlay, j/k to move in lists. Pairs with the command palette; showcase shortcuts inside the palette (Figma/Vercel/Intercom pattern).
findings.source: Mobbin 'showcase shortcuts in command palettes' https://mobbin.com/glossary/command-palette ; Vercel dashboard shortcuts changelog https://vercel.com/changelog/quickly-navigate-the-dashboard-with-shortcuts
findings.current_state: No keyboard shortcuts on any account page beyond the dropzone Enter/Space. No '?' cheat-sheet overlay. Navigation is mouse-only via the per-page sidebar.
findings.proposed_change: Extend account-shell.js / account-cmdk.js with a small shortcut map (g-prefixed sequences for top sections, '?' to open an accessible role=dialog cheat-sheet, gated on not-typing-in-an-input). Render shortcut hints inside palette rows. Respect prefers-reduced-motion for the overlay.
findings.rationale: Genuine power-user delight and competitor parity, but lower impact than the palette itself and best built on top of the shell + palette work. Cheap once those land; standalone it's a nice-to-have.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED REAL, not already present on the account surface. The account pages do NOT load supplement.js (0 of ~95 account pages reference it; account.html loads /app.js + /escape.js, account/overview.html loads /nav.js). The only keyboard handlers reaching account pages are in C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/nav.js (line 186 ArrowDown for mega-menu, line 360 Escape) — no Cmd+K, no g-prefix nav, no '?' overlay. C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/app.js (the account shell controller, 5312 lines) has zero keydown/shortcut/palette logic.

A global command palette DOES exist, but only on the marketing site: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/supplement.js lines 64-247 implement a Cmd+K / '/' palette (kolm-cmdk overlay, role=dialog). Crucially, even that palette does NOT implement the g-then-key navigation the finding proposes — installCmdK (lines 216-228) only wires meta+K and '/'; the j/k arrows (lines 203-208) move within the open palette list, not page lists. The g-then-key bindings (G+D docs, G+S status) are DOCUMENTED on /shortcuts (public/shortcuts.html lines 99-100) but I found no implementation of them anywhere in supplement.js or nav.js — they appear aspirational. The '?' help key exists only in the TUI (shortcuts.html line 113), not on web.

So the finding's current_state is accurate: account pages have no keyboard shortcuts beyond the dropzone Enter/Space, no '?' cheat-sheet overlay, mouse-only sidebar nav.

EFFORT: bumped 2 -> 2.5. The finding's proposed files (account-cmdk.js, account-shell.js) don't exist (account-shell.css exists but is styling-only with no shortcut/dialog rules). There is an unstated prerequisite the finding acknowledges only partially: the account surface has NO command palette today, so this isn't merely 'extend the palette' — either supplement.js's palette must first be wired into account pages, or a fresh account palette built. Building g-nav + an accessible role=dialog '?' overlay + palette hint rows + the not-typing-in-input guard, across the account shell, is ~2.5 days.

IMPACT: low is correct. Power-user delight / competitor parity (Linear/Vercel), but well behind kolm's stated #1 priority (the Data Engine per MEMORY.md). kolm's account users are ML engineers who would appreciate it, but standalone it is a nice-to-have, as the finding itself states. Default-refute does not apply because the gap is genuinely real and confirmed in code.

audit.summary: The three core marketing pages are content-rich, polished, and consistent on the central thesis (compile your workload into an owned .kolm, $0/token after compile, Apache-2.0 self-host). The dominant Conversion-surface defect is pricing incoherence: the homepage and /pricing disagree on the tier ladder (homepage omits the canonical Pro $49 tier), prices, gateway-call allotments, and /pricing contradicts itself on compile-credit counts; the /pricing JSON-LD offerCount also mismatches the visible tiers. Two footer/nav links are broken across pages (/contact, /about). Most CTA targets, comparison sub-pages, doc links, and structured data otherwise resolve correctly, and compare.html is notably honest (wrong-fit + competitor-credit sections). All interactive conversion JS (ROI calculators, speed-race demo, annual/currency toggles) is implemented but reviewed statically only — several numbers are illustrative/hardcoded (speed-race constants, $5k Enterprise payback) or diverge from their own prose. findings.title: Pricing tier ladder is self-contradictory across homepage, /pricing cards, and /pricing editorial block
findings.technique: Single source of truth for pricing (one tier-data object rendered everywhere) — the standard for every reference SaaS pricing page (Stripe, Linear, Supabase render tiers from one config).
findings.source: InfluenceFlow 'SaaS Pricing Page Best Practices: A Complete 2026 Guide' https://influenceflow.io/resources/saas-pricing-page-best-practices-a-complete-2026-guide/ ; Webstacks https://www.webstacks.com/blog/saas-pricing-page-design
findings.current_state: Homepage #pricing lists Free/Indie/Team/Business/Enterprise and OMITS Pro $49 (index.html:1185-1246). /pricing lists 6 tiers including Pro (pricing.html:341-449). Compile-credit counts contradict WITHIN /pricing: cards say Free '1 compile credit/mo' (345), Team '50' (401); editorial block says Free '5/mo' (466), Team '250/mo' (468). JSON-LD AggregateOffer offerCount='4'/highPrice='499' (pricing.html:222) contradicts the 6 enumerated offers (194-201) and 6 visible tiers, and omits Enterprise 'Custom'.
findings.proposed_change: Create one canonical tiers data structure listing all 6 tiers with price, gateway-call allotment, and compile-credit count. Render homepage #pricing, /pricing cards, /pricing editorial credit row, and the JSON-LD offers array from it. Add Pro $49 to the homepage. Set AggregateOffer offerCount=6 and drop/document highPrice. Reconcile Free credits to one number (5) and Team to one number (50 or 250) matching the billing backend.
findings.rationale: Pricing contradictions are the most damaging conversion bug on the surface: a developer who notices Free '1 vs 5 credits' on one page stops trusting every other number on the site, and the missing Pro tier on the homepage breaks the anchoring ladder. Verified directly in source at the cited lines.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED, not refuted. Every factual claim in the finding checks out against the actual source at the cited lines.

(1) Homepage #pricing (index.html:1185-1245) lists Free/Indie/Team/Business/Enterprise and OMITS Pro $49. Confirmed: grep for "$49"/"plan=pro"/"Pro" in index.html returns zero tier hits (only Business $499 at 1147/1224). /pricing (pricing.html:341-449) has all 6 tiers including Pro $49.

(2) Compile-credit contradiction WITHIN /pricing is exact. Cards: Free "Includes 1 compile credit / mo" (pricing.html:345), Team "Includes 50 compile credits / mo" (401). Editorial block: Free "5 / mo" (466), Team "250 / mo" (468). These directly contradict the cards on the SAME page.

(3) Backend cross-check strengthens this: the canonical PLAN_CATALOG at src/router.js:2538-2543 sets compile_credits_monthly Free=1, Pro=50, teams=50, Business=200. So the CARDS match the backend; the EDITORIAL block (5, 250) is the outlier that contradicts both the cards and the billing backend. Note: the finding's proposed fix says "reconcile Free to 5" — that is wrong directionally; backend canonical is Free=1, Team=50, so the editorial block should be corrected to match cards/backend, not vice versa. The fix is correct in spirit (one number) but the specific target numbers in the proposal are off.

(4) JSON-LD is self-contradictory: offers[] (pricing.html:194-201) enumerates 6 offers; AggregateOffer (217-223) says offerCount "4" with highPrice "499" and omits Enterprise "Custom". Confirmed.

NOT already implemented as SSOT: although src/router.js holds a canonical tier object, the public HTML is hand-authored and has drifted from it. The existing lock-in test tests/wave889-6-pricing-overhaul.test.js only asserts data-w889="compile-credits" MARKER PRESENCE (>=3) and that compile_credits_monthly appears >=5 times in router.js — it never asserts numeric CONSISTENCY across cards/editorial/homepage/JSON-LD. So the contradictions are unguarded drift, not intentional.

EFFORT: 1.5 days is reasonable-to-slightly-high for the minimal correctness fix (the edits themselves are <1 hour: fix editorial 5->1 and 250->50, add Pro card to homepage, set offerCount=6, fix highPrice). Building a true SSOT renderer for static HTML (the "standard SaaS" framing) is the bigger lift and would push toward 1.5d, but the static pages aren't templated, so a full render-from-config rebuild is over-engineering for the bug; a targeted reconciliation plus a consistency test is ~0.5d.

IMPACT: "critical" is somewhat overblown. The compile-credit numbers (1 vs 5, 50 vs 250) appear in deliberately small 11.5px ks-mono microcopy and a secondary editorial section; few prospects will A/B these. The missing Pro tier on the homepage and the JSON-LD offerCount/highPrice mismatch (a structured-data/SEO correctness issue, invisible to humans) are real but medium-severity conversion/trust issues, not a site-breaker. Adjusted to high. Users (developers) would notice if they read carefully, but this is a polish/trust defect, not a functional blocker.

Relevant files: C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/index.html (1185-1245), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/pricing.html (194-223, 341-449, 466-468), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/src/router.js (2538-2543 canonical catalog), C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/tests/wave889-6-pricing-overhaul.test.js (only locks presence, not value consistency).

findings.title: Homepage has zero social proof — no logos, no testimonials, and it never surfaces the 3 case studies that already exist on disk
findings.technique: Trust/social-proof block immediately after hero (curated quote testimonials with name+role+company, or metrics).
findings.source: Evil Martians 'We studied 100 devtool landing pages' https://evilmartians.com/chronicles/we-studied-100-devtool-landing-pages-here-is-what-actually-works-in-2025 ('Positioned immediately after hero across nearly all pages'; 'Nearly all 100+ pages use curated testimonials')
findings.current_state: index.html has no 'trusted by' strip, no logo wall, no testimonial block. Grep returns only a schema.org logo ref and a founder-vs-team tab toggle. Yet public/case-studies/ holds 3 detailed studies (healthcare-phi-redactor, finance-sr11-7, legal-contract-extraction), each with blockquote testimonials with .who attribution (case-studies/healthcare-phi-redactor.html:38-39). None are linked from or quoted on the homepage.
findings.proposed_change: Add a social-proof section right after the hero/whatis: (1) pull-quote carousel from the existing case-study blockquotes with attribution, (2) the three case-study headline metrics as stat cards linking to /case-studies/*, (3) an OSS-credibility row (Apache-2.0, self-hostable, 6 SDKs) promoted from the hidden hero proof (index.html:467-468). Curated quotes only.
findings.rationale: The content to populate a trust block already exists (3 case studies with attributed quotes) — purely a surfacing gap, the cheapest high-impact conversion win available. Evil Martians found a post-hero trust block on essentially every successful devtool page; kolm is the outlier with none.

verdict.reason:
The core factual claims are TRUE and verifiable: public/index.html has no testimonial block, no logo wall, no "trusted by" strip (grep returns 0 user-visible hits), and zero links to /case-studies/* (grep "case-stud" = 0). The 3 case studies do exist on disk with blockquote testimonials. The hero proof stats + OSS row ($8K/mo, 125s, Apache-2.0, 6 SDKs) exist at index.html:467-468 but are HIDDEN inside a test-anchor span (position:absolute;left:-9999px), so they are invisible to users. So a genuine surfacing gap exists and is worth a small fix (tease/link the case studies + a visible metrics/OSS strip).

HOWEVER the finding materially overstates both the remedy and the impact, so I refute it as written:

1) The proposed change — "curated quote testimonials with name+role+company" and a logo wall — is INFEASIBLE and conflicts with stated product policy. All 3 case-study attributions are deliberately ANONYMIZED with EMPTY name fields and no company: healthcare ", Director, Privacy & Compliance (anonymized)"; finance ", SVP Model Risk Management (anonymized)"; legal ", Firm General Counsel (anonymized)". The case-studies index explicitly states (index.html:126) "We do not publish customer names." The studies are labeled "illustrative" (healthcare:117). You cannot legitimately build a named-testimonial carousel or logo wall from content that is intentionally anonymized and illustrative — and the product is pre/early launch (per memory: deploys wipe the DB, signups are rate-limited test signups), so no real named customers exist to put on a logo wall.

2) Impact "critical" is overblown. For an early-stage product with no real named customers, an anonymized-quote pull strip is a medium polish item, not "the cheapest high-impact conversion win available." The Evil Martians benchmark assumes real named devtool customers exist; kolm legitimately does not have them yet.

3) Effort 2 days is somewhat high for what is genuinely actionable: linking/teasing 3 existing case studies + surfacing an already-written OSS proof row (~0.5 day). The OSS credibility is ALSO already surfaced visibly elsewhere (whatis 480/490, finale meta 1522, FAQ 1326), so part of the proposed "promote from hidden hero proof" work is redundant.

alreadyPresent=false because the homepage genuinely lacks any social-proof/case-study surfacing. But the finding's framing (named testimonials + logos, critical impact) is refuted: the remedy as specified violates an explicit no-customer-names policy and the illustrative/anonymized nature of the content. Recommend downgrading to a medium-impact, ~0.5-day "tease the 3 illustrative case studies + show the hidden metrics strip" task, with anonymized attributions only.

findings.title: 'Try it without signing up' hero CTA leads to a canned animation, not a live playground
findings.technique: Live in-page product playground / interactive demo in the hero (Linear/Vercel/Stripe run the actual product in the browser; study ranks 'animated product UI' and 'live product embeds' as strongest hero visuals).
findings.source: Evil Martians study (hero visual ranking); Vercel/Linear teardown https://www.saasframe.io/examples/vercel-landing-page
findings.current_state: Hero CTA #chat (index.html:461) anchors the ks-race section (722-844), a rAF-scripted race with HARDCODED Claude vs kolm latency/answer text and a footnote disclosing it is illustrative (761). A real free-chat endpoint and kolm-chat.js exist elsewhere (MEMORY W844: /v1/free/chat, 20/IP/day) but the homepage CTA does not mount it.
findings.proposed_change: Wire the real kolm-chat.js widget to /v1/free/chat so 'Try it without signing up' runs a real prompt against a hosted compiled specialist and shows the real receipt CID + latency. Keep the race framing but make the right pane genuinely live (rate-limited, no-auth), falling back to the canned animation if the endpoint is unreachable.
findings.rationale: The endpoint and widget already exist, so wiring is the work, not building. A live 'try it, here's your verifiable receipt' beats a disclaimed fake-race for a verification-first product whose thesis is 'the receipt is the proof'. Confidence <0.9 because rate-limit abuse and hosted-GPU cost for a no-auth homepage demo need a guardrail review.

verdict.reason:
PARTIALLY VALID but the proposed change rests on a false premise. The observable surface is accurately described: index.html:461 'Try it without signing up' anchors to #chat, which is the ks-race section (index.html:722-844) — a rAF-scripted canned animation with HARDCODED answer text and fixed latencies (CONFIG.claude firstByte 1100/cps 38; CONFIG.kolm firstByte 140/cps 260) plus the 'illustrative' footnote at line 761. Confirmed NOT already present: index.html contains zero data-kolm-chat mount points and no reference to kolm-chat.js, so the live widget genuinely is not on the homepage (it only mounts on /account/overview.html and /docs.html via nav.js, and the nav.js auto-injection at line 555-568 is gated to the account page). So the gap is real and the CTA-to-fake-animation mismatch is correctly flagged.

WHERE THE FINDING IS WRONG (refuting the proposed change as written): the proposed fix claims wiring kolm-chat.js to /v1/free/chat will 'run a real prompt against a hosted compiled specialist and show the real receipt CID + latency' and that 'the endpoint and widget already exist, so wiring is the work, not building.' That is incorrect. /v1/free/chat (src/router.js:9536-9601) does NOT do inference. It calls intent.classifyIntent() (src/intent.js:1320) — a pure natural-language-to-'kolm <verb>' command CLASSIFIER. Its response is {verb, command, args, why, confidence, source, workflow, alternatives} — there is no model inference, no receipt_id, no CID, and no inference latency anywhere in that path. There is also NO hosted-inference endpoint for a no-auth demo (grep found no /v1/free/serve, /v1/free/infer, /v1/free/run, or hosted-GGUF path). So 'just wire the existing widget to the existing endpoint' would replace a fake race with a CLI-command-suggestion box, NOT the 'real prompt -> verifiable receipt' experience the finding sells. Delivering what the finding actually promises requires building net-new hosted-GPU inference + receipt signing + abuse/cost guardrails on a no-auth public surface — not 3 days of wiring.

EFFORT: 3 days is a severe underestimate for the promised outcome (stand up a hosted compiled specialist, serve it behind a rate-limited no-auth endpoint, emit signed receipts with CID + latency, add abuse + cost guardrails, fallback to the canned race). Realistically 10-14 days. If the scope were narrowed to merely mounting the existing classifier widget in the hero, that is ~1-2 days but does NOT match the finding's own value proposition.

IMPACT: 'high' is overblown to 'medium'. kolm's buyers are infra/ML engineers who convert via the CLI (the homepage already embeds a real kolm-chat.js CLI elsewhere and ships an offline-verifiable real receipt at index.html:854+ with a published verifiable CID). A live hero playground is a nice-to-have conversion lift, not a critical gap, and the no-auth hosted-GPU cost/abuse exposure is a genuine prerequisite the finding itself flags (confidence 0.78). Users would mildly care, but the existing real-receipt strip already serves the 'receipt is the proof' thesis.

findings.title: ROI calculator lede prose contradicts its own prefilled defaults
findings.technique: Interactive, no-gate cost calculator with internally consistent copy — a high-converting personalized-quote pattern.
findings.source: InfluenceFlow 2026 guide (interactive pricing tool 'extremely unique and effective'); audit input
findings.current_state: Homepage ROI lede says 'Defaults: $3 / $15 per million for the frontier teacher' (index.html:1109) but the prefilled frontier-price inputs are pin=0.25 / pout=0.55 (1136-1137). /pricing ROI hardcodes Enterprise payback cost=5000 (pricing.html:589) although the Enterprise tier price is 'Custom'.
findings.proposed_change: Reconcile the lede to the actual default inputs (change prose to '$0.25 / $0.55 per million' or change defaults to match the prose consistently). For /pricing Enterprise payback, hide the payback figure for the Custom tier or label 5000 as an illustrative assumption. Add a test asserting the lede number string matches the input defaults.
findings.rationale: The calculator exists and is genuinely good (no email gate, presets, FX, annual toggle). The prose/default mismatch is a small correctness defect that undercuts its credibility — cheap to fix, verified in source.

verdict.reason: Verified in source — both defects are real and live. INDEX: public/index.html:1109 lede states "Defaults: $3 / $15 per million for the frontier teacher", but the prefilled inputs roi-pin/roi-pout are 0.25/0.55 (lines 1136-1137), and the active default "support" preset (is-active button at line 1115, defined line 1753) is also pin:0.25/pout:0.55. No preset anywhere uses $3/$15 (extract/agent both use $5/$25). I confirmed presets only apply on click (lines 1758-1772) and the lede text is static (no JS rewrites it), so on initial load the calculator is in the 0.25/0.55 state while the prose claims $3/$15 — a genuine internally-inconsistent-copy defect that undercuts the calculator's credibility. PRICING: public/pricing.html:589 hardcodes Enterprise payback cost:5000, and the tier label is "Enterprise · Custom" (line 541); the field initializes to a dash (line 542) but paybackText (562-566) renders "X.X mo" using 5000/savings once savings>0, presenting an unstated illustrative assumption as a concrete payback for a Custom-priced tier. No existing test asserts the lede string matches the input defaults (grep of tests/ found only unrelated numeric 0.25 coincidences), so this is not already guarded. NOT REFUTED. Caveats on the finding: impact is overstated as "medium" — the computed numbers are self-consistent from the inputs; only the descriptive sentence is stale, and few visitors cross-reference prose against input fields, so low is more accurate. Effort of 0.5 days is realistic (one-line prose edit or two input-value edits, plus an optional small string-match test; the pricing fix is equally small — suppress the figure for Custom or label 5000 as illustrative).

findings.title: compare.html ships hardcoded dark hex on the light-default theme (contrast/regression risk)
findings.technique: Theme-token-only colors (no hardcoded hex) so light/dark both render correctly — a baseline of the design-token system kolm already runs.
findings.source: Audit input (compare.html:48,151); kolm design-tokens.css convention per MEMORY W836/W850
findings.current_state: compare.html .cmp-card .when uses 'background:#0c0e12' (48) and the market-update box uses 'var(--ks-bg-2,#0f1...)' (151) — a dark literal that does not adapt when the page renders in light (site default since W836 Warm Paper). Audit flagged this as partial.
findings.proposed_change: Replace the hardcoded dark hex on compare.html .cmp-card .when and the market-update box with surface/ink tokens (var(--ks-bg-2) / var(--surface-*) / var(--ink-*)) that flip per theme, matching the rest of the site. Verify both themes render readable contrast.
findings.rationale: kolm already standardized on themeable tokens; these literals are a regression that produces a dark card on a light page (poor contrast / off-brand). Cheap, mechanical, verified from the audit's cited lines.

verdict.reason:
Partly confirmed, partly refuted. CONFIRMED for line 48: `.cmp-card .when { ... background: #0c0e12; ... }` (C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/public/compare.html:48) is a genuine hardcoded near-black hex with NO token fallback. Since W836 the site default is light (Warm Paper), so this box renders near-black on a light page across all 9 "Decisive when"/"Better fit" cards (compare.html:343,349,355,361,367,373,391,397,403). Its body text uses --ks-ink-2 (light=#44494f dark gray) and its bold label uses --ks-accent (light=#111111 near-black) — dark text on a near-black background = failing contrast in the default theme. No external override exists (grep of ks.css/warm-paper.css/supplement.css found no .cmp-card rule), so the inline literal is authoritative. This is a real, contained contrast regression.

REFUTED for line 151: the market-update box uses `background: var(--ks-bg-2, #0f1115)`. --ks-bg-2 IS defined in ks.css for every theme (:root/light #e8ebef at lines 21/210, dark #1c2128 at line 231), so the box adapts correctly per theme and the `#0f1115` hex is an unreachable CSS fallback, not a non-adapting literal. The finding misread this line.

alreadyPresent=false: the bug is a real slipped literal, not already fixed. But the themeable-token SYSTEM the finding wants is already standard site-wide (line 151 demonstrates it) — so the proposed "system" isn't missing, just one literal at line 48 needs swapping to var(--ks-bg-2) or a surface token.

Effort overestimated: 0.5 days is far too high — this is a one-line CSS swap (minutes), and only one of the two cited lines actually needs changing. Impact overstated: medium -> low. It is a decorative card family on a secondary comparison page, not on the homepage or any conversion-critical CTA path; the page remains functional and the regression is purely cosmetic/readability for those 9 boxes in light mode.
