findings.title: Ship-gate is RED on the current tree (51/52) — fix the brittle wave214 source-slice
findings.technique: Robust source-anchored test assertion (match whole function body, not a fixed-char window)
findings.source: Verified by running `node --test tests/wave214-distill-from-captures.test.js` (14 pass / 1 fail) + cli/kolm.js line index; corroborated by audit/w920/tests-gate.audit.json gaps[0]
findings.current_state: tests/wave214-distill-from-captures.test.js line 127 does `CLI_SRC.slice(distillIdx, distillIdx+2000)` from `async function cmdDistill(` (cli/kolm.js:21545) and asserts /--from-captures/ + /cmdDistillFromCaptures/. W918 inserted `_w918MaybeRunAgentDistill` + a long comment block at the top of cmdDistill, so the first 2000 chars now end inside the cmdDistillRuns dispatch (~line 21570), BEFORE the real `--from-captures` dispatch which moved to cli/kolm.js:21587. Product code is intact (definition at 22119). This single brittle assertion makes ship-gate check #32 fail → exit 1.
findings.proposed_change: Widen the slice to ~3500 chars OR (better) extract the cmdDistill body by finding the next top-level `async function ` after distillIdx and slicing to that boundary, so the assertion tracks the whole function regardless of future header growth. TEST-ONLY change; product code must not be touched.
findings.rationale: This is the only thing standing between the team and a genuine green ship-gate. It is a ~1-line edit with zero product risk. Until fixed, every '52/52' claim (data/w889-11-gate-failures.json, the W910 ship gate) is false on the live tree, and the wave889-11 umbrella + CI test-suite.yml are red.

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED, not refuted. Ran `node --test tests/wave214-distill-from-captures.test.js` -> 14 pass / 1 fail. The failing test is #10 at C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/tests/wave214-distill-from-captures.test.js:127, which does CLI_SRC.slice(distillIdx, distillIdx+2000) from `async function cmdDistill(` and asserts /--from-captures/ + /cmdDistillFromCaptures/. Verified char offsets in cli/kolm.js: cmdDistill begins at char 1011071; the W918 `_w918MaybeRunAgentDistill` header + comment block pushed the real `--from-captures` dispatch (cli/kolm.js:21587, `if (args.includes('--from-captures')) return cmdDistillFromCaptures(args);`) to offset 2580 and the cmdDistillFromCaptures token to offset 2680 — both past the 2000-char window. Product code is fully intact: dispatch at line 21587, definition cmdDistillFromCaptures at line 22119. This is pure test brittleness, not a product regression. Ship-gate impact is real: scripts/ship-gate.cjs:504-505 wires check #32 (id:32, surface:cross) to this exact test via shellTest(), which returns `ok: r.ok` from the node-test exit, so the failing test fails the check and the gate exits 1. The gate has exactly 52 checks (ids 1-52). data/w889-11-gate-failures.json records ship_gate_result \"52/52 pass\" — now false on the live tree (51/52). The repo's own audit/w920/tests-gate.audit.json gaps[0] independently documents the same root cause. Proposed fix verified: a 3500-char slice captures both tokens; extracting to the next top-level `async function ` (body len 10463) is even more robust. Effort 0.25d realistic (one-line test-only edit, zero product risk). Impact 'critical' accurate: a single brittle assertion turns the ship-gate red and falsifies every 52/52 claim until fixed. alreadyPresent=false because the test is still brittle (the proposed robust assertion is not yet in place).

findings.title: release-verify gate-count lock-in (wave526 #8) drifted 12 vs 13 — add 'ship-gate'
findings.technique: Single source of truth for the advertised gate list (export GATE_NAMES from the driver; tests import it instead of re-hardcoding)
findings.source: Verified by running `node --test tests/wave526-release-verify-json-mode.test.js` → `AssertionError: 13 !== 12`; release-verify.cjs:683 records 'ship-gate' even when opted out
findings.current_state: tests/wave526-release-verify-json-mode.test.js lines 92-96 hard-code 12 gate names (no 'ship-gate') and line 109 asserts `parsed.gates.length === allGates.length`. release-verify.cjs now always records 13 gates (gateShipGate at :681 calls recordResult('ship-gate', ...) even when --include-ship-gate is off). Test fails 13 !== 12.
findings.proposed_change: Add 'ship-gate' to the allGates array in wave526 #8 (12→13). Separately, wave529 #9 ALL_GATES (tests/wave529-...:142-145) is a 10-gate subset that omits 'ship-gate', 'control-files', AND 'claim-verify' — it passes vacuously and locks in none of them; add all three. Best fix: have release-verify.cjs export a frozen GATE_NAMES constant and have both tests assert against it, killing this drift class.
findings.rationale: Second of two blockers turning the suite red. The deeper issue is that the gate list lives in 3 places (driver, wave526, wave529) and has already diverged twice; a single exported constant prevents the next W-wave from re-breaking it.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED real and not refuted. Running `node --test tests/wave526-release-verify-json-mode.test.js` reproduces `AssertionError: 13 !== 12` at line 109. Root cause is exactly as described: scripts/release-verify.cjs gateShipGate (lines 681-683) always calls recordResult('ship-gate', ...) — when --include-ship-gate is off it records skipped:true/ok:true rather than not recording at all — so the --json summary always carries 13 gates (verified empirically: lint:refs, control-files, openapi-sync, claim-verify, sdk-manifest, test, sdk-smoke, local-surfaces, ship-gate, doctor, whoami, verify-claims, billing-tiers). The wave526 #8 allGates array (lines 92-96) lists only 12 names (no 'ship-gate'), and line 109 asserts parsed.gates.length === allGates.length, hence 13 !== 12. No GATE_NAMES/ALL_GATES export or module.exports exists in the driver, so the proposed single-source-of-truth fix is genuinely absent and the gate list is duplicated across driver + two tests.

Corrections to the finding: (a) The secondary claim that wave529 #9's 10-gate ALL_GATES (lines 142-145) 'passes vacuously and locks in none of [ship-gate, control-files, claim-verify]' is partially overstated — I concatenated the actual wave529 SUITE files and all three substrings ARE present (control-files/claim-verify in wave526, ship-gate in wave528), so W529 #9 would still pass even with them added. The true weakness is that W529 #9 is only a substring-presence scan (not a real enumeration/count lock-in) and its list is stale at 10; the 'single exported constant' recommendation remains the correct fix, but the 'locks in none of them' framing is inaccurate. (b) Effort: the minimal red-fix (12→13 in wave526) is minutes; the full frozen-GATE_NAMES refactor (with CJS→ESM interop caveat, and noting wave529 #9 scans source text not a runtime import) is a few hours — 0.5 days is a generous ceiling. (c) Impact 'critical' is overblown: it is a test-only lock-in drift with zero product/user-facing behavior affected; no kolm end user sees it. It IS, however, a true blocker for a green release-verify run (it fails the suite, which fails the driver's own 'test' gate), so under the repo's '100% green before ship' mandate the maintainer must fix it. Realistic impact: medium-high, not critical.

findings.title: Wire branch/function coverage with node's built-in runner and ratchet a floor in CI
findings.technique: Node built-in test coverage (--experimental-test-coverage + --test-coverage-lines/branches/functions) OR c8 --check-coverage, enforced as a CI gate that ratchets upward
findings.source: https://nodejs.org/learn/test-runner/collecting-code-coverage + https://github.com/bcoe/c8
findings.current_state: kolm runs `node --test --test-concurrency=1 tests/*.test.js` with NO coverage measurement anywhere. No numeric coverage floor in CI; ship-gate measures presence of named test files, not whether code paths execute. 66/501 src modules (13%) are never referenced by any test (verified by scanning all 665 test files for each module's basename), including critical paths: src/pii-redactor.js (189 LOC, security), src/gateway-router.js (347), src/gateway-receipt.js (167), and the entire data-engine cycle src/data-curate.js (357), src/data-augment.js (416), src/data-evaluate.js (405), src/data-feedback.js (336).
findings.proposed_change: Add a coverage step to test-suite.yml using node's built-in coverage (Node 20, no new dep): `node --test --experimental-test-coverage --test-coverage-functions=NN --test-coverage-branches=NN tests/`. Measure current reality, set the threshold ~5% below it, add a quarterly ratchet, emit lcov for PR comments, and gate the build on the threshold. Add an npm `test:coverage` script.
findings.rationale: A 'green' suite says nothing about whether 13% of src ever runs. Node 20 ships coverage natively (zero new dependency), so the cost is config + a CI line. A ratcheting floor turns the existing 665 tests into a regression net and exposes which surfaces (data-engine, gateway-router, pii-redactor) are flying blind.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED, NOT REFUTED (with caveats). Current-state claims check out against the actual code: package.json:84 `test` = `node --test --test-concurrency=1 tests/*.test.js` with no coverage flags; .github/workflows/test-suite.yml runs `node --test tests/` with no coverage step; no c8/nyc/--experimental-test-coverage/lcov anywhere in the toolchain (the only "coverage" hits are HTML prose and tests/wave485-openapi-coverage.test.js, which is API-route doc coverage, not code coverage). scripts/release-verify.cjs and ship-gate.cjs gate on "fail 0" and presence of named test files, never on executed code paths. engines.node>=20 (local v24), so Node built-in coverage is available with zero new dep. The named modules exist with ~stated LOC (src/data-curate.js 396, data-augment.js 456, data-evaluate.js 442, data-feedback.js 373, pii-redactor.js 205, gateway-router.js 366, gateway-receipt.js 182) and the data-engine four have NO test references at all (no unit test, and product-surfaces.json smoke probes do not touch curate/augment/evaluate/feedback) — so "never runs" is literally true for those.

Two caveats temper the finding. (1) Impact is overstated as "high"; it's a maintainer-facing quality gate. gateway-router/gateway-receipt DO get incidental integration execution (tests/wave888i-gateway-overhead.test.js + router dynamic imports), so the "13% of src never runs / flying blind" framing is partly overstated — basename-scan flags them as untested but they execute indirectly. kolm's actual users (compiling/running .kolm artifacts) don't care about internal coverage numbers. Downgrade to medium.

(2) Effort 2 days is optimistic and there's a material unstated prerequisite: kolm's tests heavily spawn subprocesses (release-verify boots `node server.js`; many tests run `cli/kolm.js` and server child processes), and the data-engine/router modules are loaded via dynamic `import()` *inside the server subprocess* (src/router.js:23797-23862, 6085, 6212). Node's `--experimental-test-coverage` only instruments modules loaded in the main test process, so it would NOT count subprocess-executed code and would report misleadingly low numbers — exactly for the data-engine/router code the finding wants to illuminate. Trustworthy numbers require NODE_V8_COVERAGE + c8 merge across subprocesses, which is more than "a CI line," pushing effort to ~3-4 days. Adjusted: real upgrade, not present, medium impact, ~3.5 days.

findings.title: Add targeted tests for the DATA ENGINE 6-stage modules (highest-impact zero-coverage surface)
findings.technique: Unit + contract tests for the curate/augment/evaluate/feedback pipeline modules
findings.source: Coverage scan (this audit): src/data-curate.js, src/data-augment.js, src/data-evaluate.js, src/data-feedback.js, src/data-provenance.js have ZERO test-file references; MEMORY.md flags the data engine as the 'biggest hole in product' (KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md)
findings.current_state: The INGEST→CURATE→AUGMENT→TRAIN→EVALUATE→FEEDBACK cycle named as the #1 product hole has four core modules (data-curate 357 LOC, data-augment 416, data-evaluate 405, data-feedback 336) with no test referencing them by name. ship-gate check #11 (dataforge-from-captures scaffold) validates a 5-row JSONL inline but does not cover curate/augment/evaluate/feedback logic.
findings.proposed_change: Write per-stage contract tests: feed a small fixed input through each module's exported entry point and assert output shape + at least one behavioral invariant (curate dedups + filters by quality threshold; augment preserves provenance; evaluate emits K-score with calibration fields; feedback maps low-confidence captures back to the next compile set). These are exactly the modules a coverage floor would flag first.
findings.rationale: The data engine is the explicitly-named biggest product gap, and its core modules are untested. Tests here protect the surface the team is actively building and give the ship-gate something real to assert beyond a single scaffold's 5-row JSONL.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED, not refuted. Core claim holds: src/data-curate.js, data-augment.js, data-evaluate.js, data-feedback.js, data-provenance.js have NO test file importing them. The only data-engine test is tests/wave910-data-ingestion.test.js which covers data-ingest.js (INGEST stage) ONLY. tests/wave355-seeds-augment.test.js imports a DIFFERENT module (src/seeds-augment.js, not data-augment.js). The ship-gate test (wave910-ship-gate.test.js) is purely structural — it asserts test-family files and account HTML pages EXIST, plus cool-slate palette; it does NOT exercise curate/augment/evaluate/feedback behavior at all.

Modules are real, reachable product code (not stubs): all four are dynamically imported and called in src/router.js (mod.curatePairs / mod.evaluateRun / mod.augment / mod.identifyProdGaps around lines 23797-23874) and referenced in cli/kolm.js. No not_yet_implemented/TODO/503 markers. They hold pure, deterministic, testable logic (ordered PII redaction, hard/soft CoT flagging, 3-gram cluster dedup, costPrune, classifyFailure), so the proposed per-stage contract tests are directly feasible.

Minor finding errors that do NOT invalidate it: (a) LOC are slightly UNDER-stated (actual 396/456/442/373 vs claimed 357/416/405/336); (b) the cited 'ship-gate check #11 dataforge-from-captures / 5-row JSONL inline' does not appear in wave910-ship-gate.test.js — likely a misattribution to a release-verify script. These are incidental color.

Effort 3 days is realistic-to-slightly-generous: many exported helpers (flagCot, redactPii, gapFill, evol, classifyFailure, costPrune) are trivial pure-function unit tests; the heavier entry points take {tenant, namespace} and touch the event store / KOLM_DATA_DIR namespace dirs, so behavioral tests need KOLM_DATA_DIR isolation (pattern already demonstrated by the existing data-ingestion test fixtures). Impact 'high' is defensible — MEMORY.md explicitly names the data engine the 'biggest hole in product' (KOLM_DATA_ENGINE_PLAN.md) and these are its core stages. Users (and the actively-building team) would care: this is the surface they are shipping. Unstated prerequisite: tests should lean on the pure exports plus KOLM_DATA_DIR isolation to stay fast/deterministic in CI.

findings.title: Distinguish genuine-PASS from skip-as-PASS so a green 52/52 stops over-claiming
findings.technique: Three-state result accounting (pass / skip / fail) surfaced as separate counters, with a strict CI mode that fails on skip
findings.source: Verified: ship-gate.cjs:318 runScaffold returns ok:true on env.skipped===true ('SKIP counts as PASS per W888-L exit contract'); scripts/scaffolds/gguf-llama-cpp-roundtrip.cjs skip() exits 0. Frontier practice: explicit skip accounting (https://www.minware.com/guide/best-practices/flaky-test-quarantine)
findings.current_state: summary.passed lumps real passes and environment SKIPs together. On a bare CI box (no GPU/kubectl/llama-cpp), checks #15 (GGUF roundtrip), #26 (k8s dry-run), #34 (shard-KV VRAM), and the persona e2e extensions all report PASS via SKIP. So '52/52 green' does NOT certify those deployment surfaces executed. The skip reason is only in the per-row detail string, never in the summary counters.
findings.proposed_change: In ship-gate.cjs track a distinct `skipped_as_pass` counter alongside passed/failed/not_yet; print it in the JSON envelope and human summary (e.g. '48 ran-green, 4 skipped-as-pass'). Add a `--strict` / KOLM_SHIP_GATE_STRICT=1 mode that treats skip-as-pass as non-green (exit 2) for the release box that DOES have the deps, so production gating sees whether GGUF/k8s/shard-KV actually ran. Keep default lenient for dev boxes.
findings.rationale: The framework already emits structured skip envelopes (good), but folds them into 'passed' — precisely the over-claim that lets a deployment-path regression ship invisibly. Surfacing the count is cheap; a --strict gate for the release runner closes the certification gap without slowing dev loops.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED, not refuted. The code matches the finding exactly.

(1) ship-gate.cjs:318-326 — runScaffold returns ok:true when env.skipped===true, with the comment "SKIP counts as PASS per W888-L exit contract." The driver loop (line 740) does `if (row.ok) { passed++; }`, so a runtime SKIP is indistinguishable from a genuine pass in the `passed` counter.

(2) The named scaffolds skip exactly as described: gguf-llama-cpp-roundtrip.cjs (line 33-35) emits `{ok:false, skipped:true}` exit 0 when KOLM_GGUF_PATH unset or llama-cpp-python not importable; k8s-manifest-dry-run.cjs (line 51) and shard-kv-vram.cjs (line 30-45) do the same on missing kubectl / no CUDA torch. So on a bare CI box, checks #15/#26/#34 all report PASS via SKIP and roll into summary.passed.

(3) No existing implementation of the proposal. Grep for skipped_as_pass / KOLM_SHIP_GATE_STRICT / --strict / skip_as_pass returns zero matches in ship-gate.cjs. There is a `skipped` counter (summary, line 705/756) but it ONLY counts checks excluded via the --skip= CLI flag (line 710-718) — runtime env.skipped SKIPs never reach it; they are folded into `passed`. The two are conflated in naming but operationally distinct, which actually strengthens the finding: there is genuinely NO counter for scaffold skip-as-pass.

(4) The over-claim propagates upward: release-verify.cjs:709-716 reads parsed.passed directly and reports `${passed}/${total} passed`, never surfacing how many of those were runtime skips. So "52/52 green" in the release runner does not certify the GPU/k8s/llama-cpp deployment paths executed.

(5) Caveats on the finding's specifics:
- The finding says "persona e2e extensions" report PASS via SKIP — partially imprecise. The e2e extensions (KOLM_SHIP_GATE_INCLUDE_E2E=1, line 676-700) are opt-in and append additional check IDs; whether they skip-as-pass depends on those extension files (not read here). The core 4 checks (#15/#26/#34 plus #38/#39/#48 which are also runScaffold-based) are the solid cases.
- Effort 1 day is realistic and arguably generous: adding a `skipped_as_pass` counter (~10 lines: tag the row in runScaffold, increment a counter in the loop, add to summary + Markdown + human print) plus a --strict/KOLM_SHIP_GATE_STRICT mode that maps skip-as-pass to a non-green exit (~10 lines). The one non-trivial dependency is the smoke test (tests/wave888i-ship-gate-smoke.test.js) which hard-pins the envelope shape {total,passed,failed,not_yet,skipped} via --skip=1..52 assertions (lines 52-56) — adding a new counter key is additive and won't break those assertions (they check specific keys, not exhaustive shape), but a thorough change should add a lock-in assertion there too. Half a day to a day total.
- Impact "high" is fair but borderline. This is the project's FINAL ship gate and the value prop is verifiable receipts/proof, so a deployment-path regression shipping behind a green 52/52 is a real certification gap that contradicts the product's own "the receipt is the proof" thesis. Users running production gating would care; dev-box users would not notice (proposed --strict keeps default lenient, which is the right call). I'd call it medium-high — accurate, low-risk, cheap, and directly aligned with the product's truthfulness positioning.

findings.title: Make ship-gate part of default release-verify (or a dedicated CI job), not opt-in-and-forgotten
findings.technique: Mandatory orchestration gate in the release path, with cloud-dependent checks isolated behind a secrets-aware job
findings.source: release-verify.cjs:683 short-circuits ship-gate to skipped unless --include-ship-gate; audit gaps[3] (coverage gap)
findings.current_state: Default `npm run release:verify` records ship-gate as skipped ('opt-in via --include-ship-gate'). The 52-check sweep's server-boot/scaffold/HTTP-probe orchestration only runs if the operator adds the flag. The underlying *.test.js files do run via the 'test' gate, but the orchestration logic (shared-server reuse/respawn, scaffold runner, live HTTP probes) is uncovered in the default path.
findings.proposed_change: Add a CI job (or a second job in test-suite.yml) that runs `node scripts/ship-gate.cjs --json` on every push to main with the deps the release box has, surfacing the new skipped_as_pass count. Keep --include-ship-gate as the dev escape hatch but ensure the orchestration runs automatically on the path to ship. Cloud-key checks stay behind the SKIP envelope so the job stays green without secrets.
findings.rationale: The orchestrator catches integration regressions (the exact class wave214 #32 surfaces), yet it is the one gate the default run skips. Wiring it into CI means the next header-growth/source-drift breaks a build instead of silently rotting until someone remembers the flag.

verdict.reason:
COULD NOT REFUTE — the finding holds against the actual code. Every factual claim verified: release-verify.cjs:683 (`if (!includeShipGate) return recordResult('ship-gate', true, { skipped: true, detail: 'opt-in via --include-ship-gate' })`) short-circuits the gate to skipped by default; the flag is set at line 81 (`includeShipGate = args.includes('--include-ship-gate')`). The orchestration logic the finding cites is genuinely real and lives ONLY in scripts/ship-gate.cjs, not in the *.test.js files: shared-server boot/reuse/respawn (lines 121-250), live HTTP probes (line 256), and the scaffold runner (lines 297-318). `node --test tests/*.test.js` (what package.json:84 "test" runs, and what test-suite.yml runs) exercises the underlying behavioral assertions but NOT this orchestrator wiring. Grep across .github/ for ship-gate / release-verify returned ZERO matches — confirming the orchestrator runs in NO CI workflow today; only smoke.yml (server boot + smoke-live.sh) and test-suite.yml (node --test tests/) run. The finding maps verbatim to the prior w920 audit's gaps[3] COVERAGE GAP. NOT already present as a CI job — alreadyPresent=false (the gateShipGate() function and --include-ship-gate escape hatch exist, matching the finding's own already_partially_present:true, but the automatic CI wiring it proposes does not exist).

Effort adjusted 1 -> 1.5 days for an UNSTATED PREREQUISITE the finding omits: the prior audit's gaps[0] documents that the live ship-gate currently exits 1 (51/52) because tests/wave214-distill-from-captures.test.js #10 slices only the first 2000 chars of cmdDistill() and W918's agent-mode insertion pushed --from-captures past that window (product code intact, brittle test). Wiring ship-gate into CI as a blocking job TODAY would immediately produce a red build, so the brittle slice must be widened first (~0.5d) or the job started non-blocking. The smoke.yml job recipe (checkout/setup-node/npm ci/timeout-minutes) is a ready template, so the core wiring is ~1 day. The finding's mention of surfacing a 'skipped_as_pass count' is extra scope (maps to gaps[2]: checks #15 GGUF, #26 k8s, #34 shard-KV count env SKIP as PASS), but that is a small reporting addition.

Impact medium is accurate and not overblown — this is a maintainer/release-hygiene improvement, not user-facing. The orchestrator catches integration-class regressions (server-boot wiring, scaffold runner, respawn) that the per-file behavioral tests cannot, exactly the failure mode the audit flagged as silently rotting until someone remembers the flag. Confidence 0.85 is justified.

findings.title: Add mutation testing on the core gate/verify modules to catch assertion-light tests
findings.technique: Mutation testing (StrykerJS command runner) scoped to high-value modules, run nightly not per-PR
findings.source: https://stryker-mutator.io/docs/stryker-js/guides/nodejs/ + https://railsatscale.com/2024-01-25-catching-assertionless-tests/
findings.current_state: Many kolm tests assert on source-string presence (the exact pattern that broke wave214 #10) rather than behavior. Source-string asserts survive almost all mutations — they verify the code is WRITTEN a certain way, not that it WORKS. No mechanism detects an assertion that is too weak.
findings.proposed_change: Add StrykerJS with the `command` test-runner (`stryker run` invoking `node --test <subset>`) scoped initially to src/gateway-router.js, src/gateway-receipt.js, src/runtime-passport.js, src/data-evaluate.js. Run nightly (mutation testing is slow). Report mutation score; set a low initial floor and ratchet. Use survivors to find behavior the source-string-heavy tests miss.
findings.rationale: The wave214 failure is a symptom of a structural habit: tests that assert on source text. Mutation testing is the canonical way to surface assertion-light tests; scoping it to a few hot modules and running nightly keeps cost bounded while exposing the weak spots. Lower confidence because Stryker config on a 501-module repo needs care to stay fast.

verdict.reason:
PARTIALLY REFUTED — real-but-mis-targeted, impact overblown.

NOT already present: No Stryker config, no stryker dep, no mutation-testing harness. The only "mutation" hits in src/significance.js are statistical permutation tests (unrelated). devDeps are just eslint + playwright; no coverage tooling (c8/nyc) either. So the literal proposal isn't shipped.

But the finding's core justification is materially wrong about the EXACT modules it proposes scoping Stryker to. It claims these "core gate/verify modules" suffer from source-string-presence asserts (the wave214 pattern). Checking the actual code, none of the 4 cited modules are tested that way:
- src/runtime-passport.js (619L): tests/r1-runtime-passport.test.js imports the real module and exercises validatePassport/estimatePassport/recordTestedPassport behaviorally (runs in ~50ms). Two more behavioral tests (wrapper-r-enrich, wrapper-r1-r8).
- src/data-evaluate.js (442L): scripts/data-evaluate-smoke.mjs dynamically imports the real module and asserts on computed behavior (mean_score round-trip, ranked failure categories, regression deltas, ships true/false on CoT contamination). Genuinely behavioral; just not wired into package.json/CI.
- src/gateway-router.js (366L) + src/gateway-receipt.js (182L): exercised end-to-end by tests/wave888i-gateway-overhead.test.js, which spawns the actual server.js and drives real HTTP through /v1/gateway/dispatch, building+verifying receipts via the live pipeline.

So the source-string pattern (wave214 = assert.match on readFileSync of src/router.js + cli/kolm.js) is real, but it lives in ~24/665 test files (3.6%) and targets the MONOLITHIC router.js/cli/kolm.js wiring — NOT the 4 modular files the finding wants to mutate. Stryker on those 4 modules would mutate code already protected by genuinely behavioral tests, surfacing few survivors. The proposal mis-targets the actual weak spot.

Effort underestimated: 3d -> ~5d. The pure modules (runtime-passport ~50ms, data-evaluate fast) are easy. But gateway-router/gateway-receipt are ONLY covered by a server-spawning e2e test measured at ~4.7s/run. Stryker's `command` runner re-runs the whole command per surviving mutant with no per-test selection; 366+182 lines yield ~300-500 mutants, so the gateway slice alone is plausibly 30-60+ min/night plus timeout tuning, and you'd first have to write fast importable unit tests for those modules (which don't exist) for the score to mean anything.

Impact overblown: medium -> low. (1) The named modules are already behaviorally covered, so payoff is small there. (2) Where the real assertion-light habit lives (router/CLI wiring asserts), the finding explicitly does NOT propose mutating. (3) kolm's actual users (people compiling .kolm artifacts) never see this; it's internal test-quality hygiene with no product-surface effect. A cheaper, better-targeted fix for the stated wave214 symptom is converting the highest-value source-string wiring asserts into spawn/fetch behavioral tests (the suite already has 324 such tests) — not bolting Stryker onto modules that are already behaviorally tested.

Files cited are plausible (package.json, a new stryker.config.json, test-suite.yml all exist/are creatable). Verdict: real gap (mutation testing genuinely absent) but the rationale mis-diagnoses which tests are weak and the impact/scope are inflated.

findings.title: Make the perf gates (#51 overhead, #52 CLI startup) regression-tracking, not single-shot
findings.technique: Persisted perf baselines with delta thresholds (fail on N% regression), p50/p95 reporting
findings.source: Ship-gate checks #51 'Gateway overhead < 500ms' and #52 'CLI startup < 500ms (p50)' use fixed absolute thresholds; frontier perf gating tracks deltas vs a stored baseline rather than a hand-picked constant
findings.current_state: tests/wave888i-gateway-overhead.test.js and tests/wave888i-cli-startup-perf.test.js assert against fixed 500ms ceilings. A regression from 50ms to 480ms (10x slower) passes silently because it is still under the ceiling. No baseline is persisted, no delta computed.
findings.proposed_change: Persist a perf baseline JSON (median + p95 over a few runs) in the repo; fail the perf tests when current p50/p95 exceeds baseline by a configurable percentage (e.g. +25%) OR the absolute ceiling, whichever trips first. Regenerate the baseline deliberately when a perf change is intended. Keeps the absolute backstop while catching slow creep.
findings.rationale: The perf gates exist and are well-placed, but a fixed 500ms ceiling tolerates a 10x regression. Delta-vs-baseline is the standard upgrade and is cheap given the tests already measure latency. Low impact because absolute ceilings already catch catastrophic regressions; this catches creep.

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED ACCURATE, NOT already implemented. The two perf gates assert against fixed absolute ceilings with no persisted baseline and no delta computation:

- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/tests/wave888i-gateway-overhead.test.js line 204: `assert.ok(overhead < 500, ...)` — single-shot 500ms ceiling. Computes mean + p50 of 10 samples but never reads/writes a baseline.
- C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/tests/wave888i-cli-startup-perf.test.js line 77: `assert.ok(p50 < P50_BUDGET_MS, ...)` with P50_BUDGET_MS = 500 (1500 on win32). No baseline.
- Ship-gate wiring confirmed at C:/Users/user/Desktop/kolmogorov-stack/scripts/ship-gate.cjs lines 631-634 — checks #51/#52 just shellTest those two files.

No data/perf-baseline.json exists (Glob: none; data/*perf* none). The only benchmark JSON in data/ is w891-2550-shard-benchmark.json (a one-shot shard run, not a perf-regression baseline). The "regression" matches in the repo are FUNCTIONAL regression guards (e.g. wave416-p0-regression-guard.test.js), not perf-delta baselines.

The related audit scripts/w890-14-performance-audit.cjs regenerates perf JSON (data/w890-14-gateway-overhead.json) on every run with p50/p95/p99, but it uses the same fixed `target_under_500: p95 < 500` rule and never compares against a stored prior baseline — confirming the finding's "no delta computed" claim across the whole perf surface, not just the two W888-I tests. So the regression described (50ms->480ms passing silently) is real.

Caveats on the finding's own numbers:
- alreadyPresent=false, BUT the finding correctly flags already_partially_present=true: the measurement plumbing (p50/mean, and p95 in the audit) already exists, so the upgrade builds on real infrastructure rather than greenfield.
- effort_days 1.5 is realistic, maybe slightly generous. The code change is small (read baseline JSON, compute % delta, fail on whichever of delta/ceiling trips first, plus a regenerate path). The non-trivial part is the baseline-capture methodology — these gates run on noisy, non-isolated local/CI hardware (the test itself widens win32 to 1500ms citing ~700ms process-create jitter), so a naive +25% delta gate would be flaky and needs care (multi-run median, machine-class awareness).
- impact "low" is accurate. Absolute ceilings already catch catastrophic regressions; this only catches slow creep on internal CI gates that end users never see. The flakiness risk on shared CI runners arguably makes the net value marginal.

Verdict: real and well-scoped, but genuinely low impact — a sensible cheap CI hardening, not a product gap users would notice.

findings.title: Add SLSA build-provenance attestation to the release CI as a ship-gate concern
findings.technique: actions/attest-build-provenance (Sigstore-backed in-toto provenance) on shipped artifacts
findings.source: https://github.com/actions/attest-build-provenance + https://github.blog/security/supply-chain-security/slsa-3-compliance-with-github-actions/
findings.current_state: No workflow or composite action references attest/provenance/sigstore/cosign/slsa (verified by scanning all .github/workflows/*.yml and actions/*/action.yml). kolm has sbom.yml and a product-level packages/attestation, but the CI that builds/publishes the SDK + CLI emits no signed build provenance. The ship-gate is about functional correctness; supply-chain integrity of the shipped artifact is unaddressed.
findings.proposed_change: Add actions/attest-build-provenance to the publish workflow(s) (kolm-publish action / SDK release) so every shipped artifact (npm SDK, GGUF builds, CLI tarball) carries a Sigstore-signed in-toto provenance verifiable with `gh attestation verify`. SLSA Build L2 with a few lines; moving the build into a shared reusable workflow reaches L3. Treat 'artifact has valid provenance' as a release-gate check.
findings.rationale: kolm sells verifiability (receipts, attestation as product). Shipping its OWN artifacts without build provenance is an inconsistency enterprise buyers will flag in due diligence. The cost is low (a marketplace action), the credibility payoff for an enterprise BYOC posture is high. Lower confidence on priority because it is orthogonal to functional ship-gate correctness.

verdict.reason:
The literal gap is REAL: no workflow or composite action references attest/provenance/sigstore/cosign/slsa. I scanned all 11 .github/workflows/*.yml and all 6 .github/actions/*/action.yml — grep for those keywords returns zero matches. So kolm's CI emits no GitHub-native SLSA build provenance. NOT already implemented in CI.

BUT the finding rests on a false premise and is already self-disclosed as a known target, which substantially weakens it:

1) NO PUBLISH PIPELINE EXISTS TO ATTACH PROVENANCE TO. The proposed_change says "add actions/attest-build-provenance to the publish workflow(s) (kolm-publish action / SDK release)" for "npm SDK, GGUF builds, CLI tarball." None of those publish jobs exist. Per kolm's own audit (public/sdk/publication-audit-2026-05-26.md): 0 of 6 SDKs are published to any registry (npm/PyPI/crates/VS Code Marketplace all blocked on name collisions like the PyPI `kolm` Korean-LM squat and mismatched npm slugs). Grep for npm publish / NPM_TOKEN / NODE_AUTH_TOKEN / registry-url across the whole repo = no matches. The .github/actions/kolm-publish action publishes a .kolm PRODUCT artifact to kolm's own registry via a runtime CLI/API call — it is not a package release and is not a target of actions/attest-build-provenance. The kolm.yml / kolm-distill.yml / kolm-ci-pipeline.yml / kolm-compile-on-push.yml / kolm-template.yml workflows are all `if: false` reference templates for DOWNSTREAM USERS, not kolm's own pipeline. The only real artifact-producing CI is sbom.yml uploading an ephemeral workflow artifact. You cannot make "artifact has valid provenance" a release gate when the release pipeline ships nothing.

2) ALREADY KNOWN AND CANDIDLY DISCLOSED. public/slsa.html already states this exact gap as a documented target, not a claimed feature: line 86 ".kolm artifacts already carry local receipts, Ed25519 signatures... A public SLSA Level 3 attestation is still a target until release artifacts, workflow identity, and transparency-log entries are published"; line 128 "Kolm should not claim SLSA Level 3 until the release workflow publishes evidence"; lines 131-132 target = "in-toto SLSA v1.0 provenance emitted by the release workflow" + "Sigstore certificate identity bound to the exact repository and workflow"; line 144 maps it to NIST 800-218 PS.3.2 as a target. INTERNAL_BACKEND_SPEC.md §4.2 #4 also lists "No supply-chain provenance. We don't write SLSA build attestations" as known-missing. So the due-diligence-inconsistency rationale is muted: kolm does not falsely claim L3; it discloses it.

3) PRODUCT-LEVEL OVERLAP. src/sigstore.js + Rekor + RFC-6962 inclusion-proof verification + packages/attestation (TEE) already implement cosign/sigstore-compatible attestation for the PRODUCT artifact (the .kolm receipt). That is conceptually adjacent but distinct from CI build provenance for kolm's own shipped packages. This is why already_partially_present:true in the finding is fair, but it does NOT cover the CI gap. A SIBLING finding in this same audit batch (govern.frontier.json: "Emit in-toto v1 Statements / SLSA provenance for .kolm artifacts") is the higher-value, more user-relevant version of this concern — it improves the artifact buyers actually consume.

EFFORT UNDERESTIMATED: 2 days only covers pasting the marketplace action. The true prerequisite is building the npm/PyPI/crates/GGUF/CLI release pipeline that does not exist AND first resolving the registry name-collision blockers from the publication audit. Realistic effort to a provenance-bearing, actually-shipping release: 5-8 days, gated on external registry-ownership decisions.

IMPACT roughly accurate (medium-leaning-low). Finding's own 0.65 confidence and "orthogonal to functional ship-gate correctness" are honest. kolm's actual users consume the .kolm product artifact (already Ed25519 + sigstore/Rekor signed), not kolm's npm package, so CI build-provenance on an unpublished SDK is low practical value until the SDKs actually ship.
